title: Africa - Lake Victoria Environmental Management Project (Burundi and Rwanda)
report_name: Africa - Lake Victoria Environmental Management Project (Burundi and Rwanda)
project_id: 3A-AFCC2/RI-Lake Victoria Environmental Management Project (Burundi and Rwanda) -- P118316
abstract: The Lake Victoria Environmental Management Project of Burundi and Rwanda had a moderately unsatisfactory overall outcome, a moderately unsatisfactory performance by the Bank and the monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. The lessons learned were: (1) The ambition regarding what can be achieved at the project level needs to be realistic. (2) Selectivity and sequencing are important to achieve demonstrable results. (3) Ownership of investments is critical to their sustainability. (4) Proactive measures reduce fiduciary risks in times of political crisis. (5) Weak country systems and coordination can lead to unanticipated consequences.

title: Africa - Second Phase of the Lake Victoria Environmental Management Project
report_name: Africa - Second Phase of the Lake Victoria Environmental Management Project
project_id: 3A-AFCC2/RI-Lake Victoria Environmental Management Project II -- P103298,3A-AFCC2/RI-Lake Victoria Environmental Management Project Phase II -- P100406
abstract: The Lake Victoria Environmental Management Project of the East African Community had a moderately unsatisfactory overall outcome, a moderately unsatisfactory performance by the Bank and the monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some of the lessons learned were: (1) The ambition regarding what can be achieved at the project level needs to be realistic. (2) Collective action requires strong regional and national institutions, whose evolution takes time. The LVBC is a relatively young organization, established only a few years before LVEMP-II APL-1. (3) Consensus building for some issues turned out to be more difficult than for others. (4) Leadership continuity assures that a consistent course of implementation is maintained. (5) Investing in basin-wide monitoring is necessary to maintain an understanding of system-wide processes. In this respect, the project correctly supported a broader M&E system that would provide awareness on the state of the lake.

title: Africa - Agricultural Productivity Project
report_name: Africa - Agricultural Productivity Project
project_id: 3A-AFCC2/RI-East Africa Agricultural Productivity Program -- P112688
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Productivity Project for Africa were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was high, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the impact of agriculture research activities can be enhanced when other links in the value chain such as extension, seed multiplication and agriculture inputs are integrated into the overall strategic project design. In planning and setting up regional projects, substantial preparation time should be allowed to have all parties agree on how to proceed and determine respective roles. This competitiveness could be a force either for protecting one’s own research turf and focusing inward, or to try to outperform each other in terms of implementation and creation of new technologies, or both. The Project design steered competitiveness away from defending turf and toward mutually beneficial regional cooperation/competition as part of preparation by providing for multi-lateral meetings to negotiate how Project resources would be used, which country would be specializing in which commodity and supporting frequent regional exchanges of scientists and technicians to help understand each other’s work. The strategy envisioned that once these exchanges had been underway for a time, the potential for defensiveness would give way to cooperation and healthy competition that would ensure that the project’s targets would be met. Research focus on regionally identified and prioritized production issues leads to quick results due to the strong collaboration and sharing of knowledge.

title: Implementation Completion and Results Report (ICR) Document - AFCC2 RI -Support to NPCA TerrAfrica Secretariat - P149269
report_name: Implementation Completion and Results Report (ICR) Document - AFCC2 RI -Support to NPCA TerrAfrica Secretariat - P149269
project_id: 3A-AFCC2/RI -Support to NPCA TerrAfrica Secretariat -- P149269
abstract: Ratings for the Support to NEPAD Planning and Coordinating Agency (NPCA) TerrAfrica Secretariat Project for Africa were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: it was agreed by country representatives that the current revisioning process for TerrAfrica was important and appropriate, but that it should conclude, and the decisions be implemented as quickly as possible to minimize loss of momentum. Adequate staffing has been an on-going problem at the Secretariat, dating back to the start of the program. NEPAD’s procurement and human resources (HR) processes do seem to have been an additional impediment to staffing initially, but to have improved substantially by the time of commencement of the AF. If TerrAfrica is to be continued - or if it is replaced by some other natural resources management (NRM) program - and the current 5-year planning and budgeting cycles continue, as far as possible World Bank (WB) or other related support projects should be articulated with these cycles. The primary emphasis on the Secretariat’s role in partnership leveraging and joint planning can usefully be toned down to focus more on what the NEPAD Agency (and the respective partners) can best deliver.

title: South Sudan - Emergency Food and Nutrition Security Project
report_name: South Sudan - Emergency Food and Nutrition Security Project
project_id: SS-South Sudan Emergency Food and Nutrition Security Project -- P163559
abstract: The overall outcome of the Emergency Food and Nutrition Security Project for South Sudan is moderately satisfactory, with moderately satisfactory Bank performance and modest evaluation and monitoring quality. The Project provided several lessons: 1. As the Bank increases its support to FCV (Fragility, Conflict, and Violence) countries, it will need to establish protocols for humanitarian emergency response projects; 2. FCV countries are usually not data-rich environments; 3. Flexibility in Project Design and Implementation; 4. Technical assistance vs. capacity building; 5. The results framework should be designed in accordance with the existing circumstances of the borrower nation; 6. Additional resources are required for supervision in FCV countries because of the lower capacity of clients; 7. Humanitarian operations can lay the foundation for development; 8. Prepositioning of supplies related to health, nutrition, WASH (Water Sanitation and Hygiene), etc. before the rainy season saves on logistics costs.

title: Kenya - Climate Smart Agriculture Project
report_name: NA
project_id: KE-Kenya Climate Smart Agriculture Project -- P154784

title: Malawi - Resilience and Disaster Risk Management Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MW-Malawi Resilience and Disaster Risk Management Project -- P161392
abstract: Ratings for the Resilience and Disaster Risk Management Project for Malawi were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the project started as a drought recovery project and after the AF changed to a flood recovery and disaster management project. The decision at AF of deleting all indicators (though achieved) made the AF appear to be a new project. It would be important for project teams to ensure continuity of the project storyline after a restructuring to maintain the focus of the project. There were challenges in complying with Bank safeguard requirements due to low capacity, which was not adequately mitigated for at appraisal; safeguard implementation improved when the Bank provided more hands-on support. Based on this experience, RCRP2 is taking a more proactive approach and is providing Hands-On Expanded Implementation Support (HEIS) to the GoM in safeguard implementation. In addition to supporting the PIU, the HEIS is also being used to support training at the district level to improve sustainability. The Project was successful in engaging communities through many avenues, including through the establishment and training of WUAs, investments in community assets such as boreholes and irrigation, and the formation of local DRM committees. This participatory approach ensured that the project generated a strong partnership between beneficiaries and implementers, and helped improve implementation and led to locally adapted infrastructure. For example, community feedback was used to adjust infrastructure designs, such as for the Thangadzi West flood protection structure.

title: Angola - Smallholder Agriculture Development and Commercialization Project
report_name: NA
project_id: AO-Smallholder Agriculture Development and Commercialization Project -- P154447
abstract: Ratings for the Smallholder Agriculture Development and Commercialization Project for Angola were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the farmer field school (FFS) experience yielded several valuable lessons relating to the need for evolutionary flexibility, a clear exit strategy for the principal service provider, and the importance of accompanying FFS trainings with different levels of investment support to maximize benefits from the trainings. A low-capacity environment where certain skills are highly demanded (procurement, environmental and social safeguards) requires upfront planning and adequate mitigation measures. It might be helpful to define subsidiary targets tailored to various farming systems within the project area. Innovations, such as internally capitalized community funds can amplify the impact of input support programs. Although it is yet to undergo the test of time, the block farming concept can help trigger the emergence of functional value chains. In the case of low institutional irrigation capacity, it is better to start with individual small-scale, low technology irrigation investments, before gradually embarking on larger schemes as institutional capacity grows.

title: Burundi - Coffee Sector Competitiveness Project
report_name: Burundi - Coffee Sector Competitiveness Project
project_id: BI-Burundi Coffee Sector Competitiveness Project -- P151869
abstract: Ratings for the Coffee Sector Competitiveness Project for Burundi were as follows: outcome was unsatisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality (M and E) was modest. Some lessons learned included: in a fragility, conflict, and violence (FCV) context where the political environment can be uncertain and government policies can change abruptly, such as in the case in Burundi, it is necessary to have flexible procedures in place under World Bank projects to allow a quick transfer of undisbursed funds across the portfolio when conditions for a particular operation to continue are not met. Producer incentives: a necessary condition for the coffee sector (or any other crop sector predicated on smallholder production) to thrive, and for benefits to eventually accrue to all parties, is for farm level prices to be set at a sufficiently high level to incentivize producers. Financial transactions: the project demonstrated that timely payments need to be made along the value chain between actors, especially at upstream level from coffee washing stations (CWSs) to producers, to keep the required level of incentives with the producers. In the context of a coffee rejuvenation program, intercropping during the gestation period when new coffee plants and stumped trees are growing and not yet fully productive, is a good agricultural practice that needs to be systematically disseminated and adopted. Last mile good rural infrastructure is critical to quickly link producers to the point of first treatment and local markets following crop harvest.

title: Uganda - Agriculture Cluster Development Project
report_name: NA

title: Africa Region - Agricultural Productivity Program for Southern Africa (APPSA) Project
report_name: Africa Region - Agricultural Productivity Program for Southern Africa (APPSA) Project
project_id: 3E-AFR RI Agricultural Productivity Program for Southern Africa (APPSA) -- P094183
abstract: The overall outcome of the Agricultural Productivity Program for Southern Africa (APPSA) Project is moderately satisfactory, with moderately satisfactory Bank performance and modest monitoring and evaluation quality. The following are key lessons based on the APPSA implementation and achievements: 1. Strong partnerships with the Consultative Group for International Agricultural Research (CGIAR) centers, the private sector and producer organizations are essential to successfully increase technology generation and uptake. 2. The potential impact of agricultural technology generation and dissemination Research and Development (R and D) activities is enhanced when dissemination and delivery mechanisms are developed and considered earlier on. 3. Regional R and D project can catalyze a strong culture of regional collaboration, facilitating applied research to efficiently address shared problems with shared solutions.

title: Mozambique - Climate Change Development Policy Operations Project
report_name: Mozambique - Climate Change Development Policy Operations Project
project_id: MZ-Mozambique Climate Change Development Policy Operation -- P128434
abstract: Ratings for the Climate Change Development Policy Operations Project for Mozambique were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the cross-sectoral nature of climate risks and their impacts typically affect several sectors of a country’s economy, as in the case of Mozambique, where for example the occurrence of a severe flood impacts not only agricultural production, basic infrastructure, and access to services, but also immediately affects government’s fiscal space. This requires policy responses and decisions involving key economic and line agencies working together to reach a common understanding of the challenges and opportunities, assess urgency and tradeoffs among options and their socio-economic implications, and prioritize actions. This is conceptually appealing but practically difficult to achieve because of institutional fragmentation and rigidities, as well as political economy issues. Selectivity and sequencing of policy reforms could help sectors build capacity at their own pace, and focus Bank resources where most incremental gains can be achieved. As indicated in the previous two lessons, while effective climate change policy reform requires a deep and sustained engagement on the part of the Bank--and development partners--coupled with a national cross-sectoral platform, it is important to ensure that the sectors selected for reform have achieved minimum readiness, and that they have access to adequate support to move as quickly as possible from planning to financing to implementation. The Bank team supporting the Mozambique DPO series may well have faced this issue and found itself at times spread too thin. Therefore, an assessment at the outset of trade-offs regarding the scope, sequencing and depth of the supported reforms is a worthwhile endeavor.

title: Mozambique - PROIRRI Sustainable Irrigation Development Project
report_name: Mozambique - PROIRRI Sustainable Irrigation Development Project
project_id: MZ-MZ PROIRRI Sustainable Irrigation Development -- P107598
abstract: Ratings of PROIRRI Sustainable Irrigation Development Project for Mozambique were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) Self-sustainability of Water User Associations (WUAs); (ii) Crop mix and rate of utilization of irrigated land is important for the overall economic viability of irrigation investment; (iii) Provision of Technical Assistance(TA) for budding organizations and right skill sets are critical for project delivery; and (iv) Market-driven irrigation investment could drive smallholder commercialization.

title: Malawi - First, Second and Third Poverty Reduction Support Grant Project
report_name: Malawi - First, Second and Third Poverty Reduction Support Grant Project
project_id: MW-MW-PRSC-3 -- P117238,MW-MW-PRSC-1 -- P099313,MW-MW-PRSC-2 -- P107303
abstract: Ratings for the First, Second and Third Poverty Reduction Support Grant Project for Malawi were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was substantial; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: the importance of Government ownership, taking the time to nurture it, through dialogue and analysis of the political economy of reforms; the need to have a good understanding of political economy of sensitive reforms; merits of working within the harmonized framework for the provision of budget support; flexibility in the Performance Assessment Framework (PAF) to emerging issues should be encouraged; the need for a good and robust Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) framework for tracking progress and; ambitions and complex reforms should not be avoided completely.

title: Tanzania - Accelerated Food Security Project
report_name: Tanzania - Accelerated Food Security Project
project_id: TZ-Tanzania - Accelerated Food Security Project -- P114291
abstract: The Accelerated Food Security Project for Tanzania contributed to higher food production and productivity in targeted areas by improving farmers' access to critical agricultural inputs. The outcome of the project was rated moderately satisfactory with the Bank performing moderately satisfactory and the Borrower performing moderately unsatisfactory. The overall risk to development was rated substantial. The project had several monitering and evaluation shortcomings. Therefore, data quality is poor. Although only one indicator (maize yield achieved by farmers with subsidized seed and fertilizer) out of the six project developmetn objective level indicators was fully achieved, the project made significant progress on most of its targets. Four key lessons were learned from the implementation of this project. Firstly, successful implementation of emergency operations significantly benefits from careful advance planning, especially procurement. Secondly, mainstreaming projects in line ministries works best when complemented by pragmatic solutions to implementation issues as they arise. Thirdly, agricultural input voucher programs can significantly increase productivity, but remain difficult to administer. Lastly, a final lesson learned is that inclusion of major civil works (e.g., the irrigation infrastructure) without expedited procurement arrangements, while well meaning, may not be best suited for these type of emergency operations. Such longer gestation investments could divert focus and contribute to implementation delays.

title: Tanzania - Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project : Tanzania - Fourth, Fifth, Sixth, Seventh and Eigth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Tanzania - Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: TZ-Tanzania Poverty Reduction Support Credit 8 -- P116666,TZ-Tanzania Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P095657,TZ-Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC6) -- P101229,TZ-Tanzania PRSC-4 -- P095509,TZ-Tanzania Seventh Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P101230
abstract: Ratings for the Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC) Project for Tanzania were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: first, there is no avoiding conditionality. Good practice principles of policy-based lending are now routinely stated in almost every operation and have become second nature in designing policy-based operations. Donors should reinforce government ownership by supporting rather than duplicating government processes (aid harmonization among donors, alignment of aid with the budget cycle, use of government procurement processes, etc.). There should be an accountability framework that guides both government and donors. Prior Actions (as conditions of disbursement) should be few but critical to the achievement of agreed results. Progress reviews should be transparent and conducive to performance-based, predictable budget support. Second, unlike investment lending operations, PRSCs are able, in principle, to deal with complex cross-cutting issues when the Government has the requisite technical and coordination capacities and the operations are supplemented by sector Investment Loans (ILs), Technical Assistance (TA) and competent analytical work. If the coordination and technical capacities are limited, interventions in complex cross-cutting areas have to be prioritized and sequenced. Over time, as capacities get stronger, the number of complex interventions can be increased. This is an area in which wishful thinking often replaces hard-nosed realism, and results in less than acceptable outcomes. Third, committed political leadership makes a difference. Yet, even the best leadership can only accomplish so much in a given time period. It must overcome ever-present vested interests, old mind-sets and ingrained behaviors, even assuming that technical and organizational capacities are adequate. This is a tricky area. There are often AAA products (as was the case with a string of policy notes in Tanzania) that identify aspects of these issues and there is anecdotal evidence. But the job of transforming that knowledge into successful interventions ultimately falls to the government. The donors can at best figure out what actions are likely to succeed and back them, or decide that the government is unlikely to follow through and back off. It is extremely difficult to get that balance right. Tanzania has been no exception. Finally, measuring outcomes is important in order to increase focus and accountability. Realistic, focused monitoring frameworks that track progress over time towards ultimate objectives, concentrate the mind of government as well as the donors.

title: Southern Sudan - Emergency Food Crisis Response Project
report_name: Southern Sudan - Emergency Food Crisis Response Project
project_id: SS-Southern Sudan Emergency Food Crisis Response Project -- P113586
abstract: Ratings for the Emergency Food Crisis Response Project (EFCRP) for Southern Sudan were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: The World Bank and implementing agency must ensure a shared understanding of project goals, methodologies, and monitoring priorities up front to avoid confusion. EFCRP benefitted from county level coordinating meetings with officials that helped minimize overlap among donor programs. In addition, local authorities played a key role in resolving localized conflicts between groups when they occurred. Youth do exhibit interest in agriculture, and they are especially attracted to machinery for land preparation and crop processing, in addition to road rehabilitation and storage construction. Land preparation mechanization is critical to bringing sufficient amounts of fallow land under cultivation, so a community can start to tackle food security, and the post‐harvest machinery is important to extending storage life of crops. Close supervision is also needed to more effectively track progress, communicate, and establish teamwork among project implementation unit (PIU) and donors. It is important to counter the impacts of high staff turnover, including by staying in active communication with changing country context and PIU staff, and by working to foster capacity of and ownership by incoming stakeholders through dialogue and periodic training sessions. When the insecurity made it next to impossible to go to the field, the World Bank and Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security (MAFS) agreed with the non‐governmental organization (NGO) partners on the priorities and to decentralize decision‐making on what interventions will work in each setting. This allowed the project to keep moving forward on at least one component until security improved.

title: Burundi - Second and Third Economic Reform Support Grant Project
report_name: Burundi - Second and Third Economic Reform Support Grant Project
project_id: BI-Economic Reform Support Grant III (DPL) -- P113235,BI-BI-ERSG II -- P102508
abstract: Ratings for the Second and Third Economic Reform Support Grant Project for Burundi were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: Government of Burundi (GoB) commitment to the reform program may be present at the highest echelons. But, it is not clear that broad consensus exists at the level of the implementing agencies. Accordingly, the design of future operations (ERSGs) should attempt to engage the broader audience (including program beneficiaries). With the Bank's increasing emphasis on Governance and Anti-Corruption (GAC), greater attention is needed on: (a) political economy analysis; (b) fraud and abuse of government funds; and (c) voice and accountability (the demand side of governance). For complying with the expanding agenda, the task team will need to take cognizance of the recommendations of the Bank's GAC council.

title: Malawi - Agriculture Sector Wide Approach Support Project
report_name: Malawi - Agriculture Sector Wide Approach Support Project
project_id: MW-Agricultural Development Programme Support Project SIL (FY08) -- P105256
abstract: Ratings for the Agriculture Sector Wide Approach Support Project for Malawi were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: involvement of multiple implementing partners in SWAp improves multi sectoral collaboration and ownership, but best achieved with a strong champion. The involvement of other implementing players other than Ministry of Agriculture e.g. Ministry of Lands, Transport, Roads Authority, Trade provided a unique opportunity to strengthen multi sectoral coordination. The weak leadership of the champion (Ministry of Agriculture) reduced anticipated gains realized from such collaboration and investment. In view of this, capacity building of the champion is key to sustain this leadership for such multi sectoral projects. Use of Government systems and structures is good towards promoting ownership, but delays implementation, in a context of limited capacity. Much as the project was implemented using Government systems it was evident that implementation delays due Government bureaucracies. The complexity of the project warrants the establishment of dedicated PIU with key qualified staff. Further, for projects that has national coverage, the regional offices need to be considered for proper project backstopping including on fiduciary matters.

title: Mozambique - Market Led Smallholder Development in the Zambezi Valley Project
report_name: Mozambique - Market Led Smallholder Development in the Zambezi Valley Project
project_id: MZ-Market-Led Smallholder Development in the Zambezi Valley -- P098040,MZ-Market led Smallholder Development in the Zambezi Valley -- P093165
abstract: Ratings for the Market Led Smallholder Development in the Zambezi Valley Project for Mozambique were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, global environmental objective (GEO) outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, risk to GEO outcome was substantial, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the design of projects for implementation under decentralized arrangements, using country systems, should critically examine the existing capacity at all levels. In order to build the strong decentralization capacity at local, district, and national levels, the project should have considered a long-term approach; using flexible lending instruments for example, adjustable program loan (APL) in order to ensure consistent and continued support for a longer period of time than the planned five year period. The sustainability of community empowerment requires continued government follow-up, facilitating appropriate linkages with service providers and agribusiness value-chain players. There is need for clear targeting and beneficiary selection criteria in order to maximize project impact. It is always good practice to ensure that the targeting and beneficiary selection criteria is well spelt out in the project implementation manual, and that its application is as participatory as possible to ensure that it is fully understood by all stakeholders.

title: Sudan - South Kordofan State Emergency Project
report_name: Sudan - South Kordofan State Emergency Project
project_id: SD-South Kordofan State Emergency Project -- P101388
abstract: Ratings for the South Kordofan State Emergency Project for Sudan were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was substantial; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory; grantee performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: setting a counterpart share needs to be realistic for a country coming out of conflict. The 50 percent counterpart share in Multi-Donor Trust Fund (MDTF), although agreed by the Government, was unprecedentedly high. This became evident when the Government defaulted on its commitments resulting in many implementation delays throughout the MDTF portfolio. Close interaction and engagement with local authorities is essential from the outset. The Bank should broaden its engagement beyond its official counterpart, the Ministry of Finance. Bank engagement with the state government once the project faced counterpart funding issues proved successful and suggests closer dialogue on a regular basis. A better risk assessment is required in fragile settings, but this must translate into more appropriate project design. It is not sufficient to provide a good assessment of risks, as was done in the project, but the risk assessment needs to inform project design. Resolving the tension and trade-offs between the need to act quickly to stabilize conflict affected populations and longer-term capacity development objectives. Where there is an inherent tension between the need to respond quickly, as in an emergency operation, and the need to build local capacity, project design needs to acknowledge this tension, the potential trade-offs and ways in which these can be resolved during project implementation. Better institutional arrangements and government involvement in overall project coordination when components are implemented by different agencies, especially if they are national and international. Even where there is a need to act quickly, there is a need to ensure some level of stakeholder involvement and consultations, which can be deepened as the project is implemented.

title: Lesotho - Private Sector Competitiveness and Economic Diversification Project
report_name: Lesotho - Private Sector Competitiveness and Economic Diversification Project
project_id: LS-Private Sector Competitiveness and Economic Diversification -- P088544
abstract: Ratings for the Private Sector Competitiveness and Economic Diversification Project for Lesotho were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: tree crop growing was new to not only most of Basotho farmers but also local agriculture administration officials. The government counterpart and the implementing agencies also need inputs and guidance on what should be changed to achieve the reform objectives and how to achieve them. A project aiming to achieve sector-wide targets should plan and budget for the strengthening of data management capacity at the government agencies and or public sector institutions as they are usually the authorities for collecting and publishing macroeconomic and sectoral data.

title: Madagascar - Irrigation and Watershed Management Project
report_name: Madagascar - Irrigation and Watershed Management Project
project_id: MG-Irrigation & Watershed Management Project - GEF -- P088887,MG-Irrigation and Watershed Management Project -- P074086
abstract: Ratings for the Irrigation and Watershed Management Project for Madagascar were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, global environment objective (GEO) outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, risk to development outcome was high, risk to GEO outcome was high, Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the project did not achieve all its planned outcomes but built a strong basis on which new operations can be (and are being indeed) prepared. Regarding institutional arrangements, the project demonstrated that it is possible, and recommended, to rely on the Ministry of Agriculture’s structure, reinforced with adequate technical assistance (TA) and the support of an external financial management agency, to implement agricultural projects. The project also demonstrated the validity of the integrated watershed approach and the strong interest that beneficiaries take in that approach. The multiannual performance contract (PC) approach and the montant d’apport préalable estimé réaliste (MAPER) combined with the formalization of Dina proved to be useful tools to generate ownership and mobilize the irrigation scheme users and to consolidate their Water User Associations (WUAs). In a context of an ever increasing fragmentation of land in irrigated areas, a strong emphasis should be put on agricultural intensification and diversification in uplands. Future projects should also be more gender sensitive in order to draw on the generally observed dynamism of women and women associations in soil conservation, intensification, diversification, marketing, and processing activities. More attention has to be paid to establishing an adequate monitoring and evaluation (M and E) system that is able to define and correctly monitor useful output and outcome indicators and that does not neglect the evaluation aspect, in order to provide project management with impact and sustainability assessments that may induce shifts in strategies and resources during the project life.

title: Burundi - Economic Reform Support Grant (ERSG) Project
report_name: Burundi - Economic Reform Support Grant (ERSG) Project
project_id: BI-BI - Economic Reform Support Grant (ERSG) -- P091475
abstract: Ratings for the Economic Reform Support Grant (ERSG) Project for Burundi were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the relative success of the public finance management components of the program is partly due to the combination of policy reforms supported by the ERSG grant and technical assistance activities financed by the restructured Economic Management Support Project (EMSP). It can also be argued that a sector investment project financing the rehabilitation of coffee plantations, washing plants and rural infrastructure will have provided useful additional support to the agricultural sector reforms envisaged in the ERSG program and would have done more to help 800,000 poor families than unrealistic privatization programs. Bank country economists did most of the preparation work even on sector policies with minimal support from sector specialists. Agricultural sector specialists would have spent more time on the preparation of the agricultural component of the grant if the ERSG program had been combined with sector work and project preparation activity aimed at developing an agricultural sector development project or program. Need for a better balance between the development of social sectors and growth-oriented infrastructure expenditure programs. The availability of Heavily Indebted Poor Countries (HIPC) funds enabled the government to increase public expenditures in the social sectors, particularly education. However, while public spending on education increased to 6.1 percent of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) in 2005-06, public spending on agriculture and infrastructure remained at very low levels (respectively 0.3 and 0.6 percent of GDP). In the situation of Burundi, stimulating economic growth is at least as important as and probably more urgent than rapid increases in social expenditures.

title: Malawi - Emergency Recovery Grant Project
report_name: Malawi - Emergency Recovery Grant Project
project_id: MW-Malawi Emergency Recovery Loan -- P097292
abstract: The Emergency Recovery Grant Project of Malawi had the following ratings: outcomes were highly satisfactory, risk to development outcome was low or negligible, Bank performance was highly satisfactory, and borrower performance was highly satisfactory. The lessons learned included: 1) The Bank should consider whether direct disbursement, as is now carried out for development policy assistance, can be permitted for emergency assistance in certain circumstances - for example, where the adverse impact of macroeconomic disruption is likely to be severe but reversible within the existing policy framework; and 2) The Government and its partners should consider whether the macroeconomic effects of a future such emergency could be better provided for, either by targeting a larger build up of foreign exchange reserves, or by the development of better insurance instruments, like call options.

title: Burundi - Agricultural Rehabilitation and Sustainable Land Management Project
report_name: Burundi - Agricultural Rehabilitation and Sustainable Land Management Project
project_id: BI-Agricultural Rehabilitation and Support Project (PRASAB) -- P085981,BI-BI-Agriculture Rehabilitation & Sustainable Land Management -- P064558
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Rehabilitation and Sustainable Land Management Project for Burundi were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; Global Environment Objectives (GEO) outcomes were satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate; risk to GEO outcome was moderate; Bank performance was satisfactory; and borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: depth over breadth in training and capacity building. While the failure rate of project financed Sub-projects (SPs) is very low, a recurring explanation for failed or lower-than-expected returns for some SPs pertains to beneficiary capacity for adhering to good practices, particularly in livestock. While the project provided over 275,000 person days of training, many of these agro technical in nature, there was a need for sustained monitoring or mentoring that may have improved the efficiency of outcomes. Performance based contracts for better performance. The incentive to Local Implementing Agencies (LIAs), both public and private, needs to be performance based. Project lessons show that Sustainable Land Management (SLM) needs to be economically interesting to be sustainable: Where the economic incentives were lower in the project, there was greater deterioration of project-financed SLM infrastructure, and vice versa. One further lesson emerging from the ICR review of project Monitoring and Evaluation (M and E) design relates to support units in the Bank. In exploring the issue of Agriculture Rehabilitation and Sustainable Land Management Project (PRASAB's) weak M and E design, the ICR team concludes that a contributing factor to the poor original design was the delivery of the project at precisely the moment of transition from the project design summary to the modern results framework.

title: Tanzania - Participatory Agricultural Development and Empowerment Project
report_name: Tanzania - Participatory Agricultural Development and Empowerment Project
project_id: TZ-Participatory Agricultural Development and Empowerment Project -- P067103
abstract: Ratings for the Participatory Agricultural Development and Empowerment Project for Tanzania were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory; the risk to development outcome was moderate; the Bank's performance was moderately satisfactory; and the Borrower's performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: empowerment is an important output for Community Driven Development (CDD). One of the primary purposes of the CDD approach is to empower local communities to affect change by improving the process by which local development decisions are made. This project attempt to use participatory Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) system did not work well. The rationale for using a participatory M&E is that the beneficiaries, by monitoring their own performance, will learn and adjust accordingly. Adaptive management and learning is an important ingredient for success. The flexible CDD design used for this project allowed a rapid scaling up at the end of the project in order to achieve the full benefit of cost savings. This project demonstrated that, given a menu of choices that included both community infrastructure and livelihood investments; there was a strong demand for livelihood investments.

title: Rwanda - Economic Recovery Credit Project
report_name: Rwanda - Economic Recovery Credit Project
project_id: RW-Supplemental to the Economic Recovery Credit -- P073330,RW-Rwanda - ECON. REC. CREDIT -- P057294
abstract: Project outcome is rated as satisfactory and the sustainability of the reform program supported by the project is likely. Overall performance of the World Bank is rated satisfactory: The Bank helped to sharpen the reform agenda through frank and patient dialogue with the Government, carefully nurtured the ownership of the reform program in the country by raising awareness among stakeholders of the need for reforms to address Rwanda's deep-seated econonomic and social problems. Borrower performance is also overall satisfactory. Lessons learned include the following: To be effective, the project design needs to be sensitive to the capacities of government and private entities in a post-conflict situation. It is important to be flexible and patient, and to regularly consult with other development partners as capacity is weak and operating conditions are subject to unexpected change. There are often widely divergent perception of the risks, which can be narrowed by dialogue between the parties. Second, regular supervision missions and strong monitoring by the Bank is esential when the Borrower's capacity is limited. Provision of capacity building is essential. In the case of Rwanda, the Bank overestimated the speed of processing legislation through the bureaucracy, the Cabinet, and the National Assembly. To avoid delays and undue pressure on the National Assembly, it is recommended that conditionality be linked only to presentation of the relevant laws to the National Assembly.

title: Malawi - Third Fiscal Restructuring and Deregulation Program Project
report_name: Malawi - Third Fiscal Restructuring and Deregulation Program Project
project_id: MW-LKD FRDP III -- P050294
abstract: The project outcome is unsatisfactory, sustainability unlikely, having attained only modest institutional development impacts. While the risks of significant lack of follow-through were previously described, it would not have been sufficient for this nature of operation, where the window of opportunity is small, and the benefits lie in maintaining the general direction of reform, rather than taking quantum steps. This said, it is important that credit amounts be maintained at levels which are commensurate with the measures being undertaken. The originally proposed two tranche operation might have been back-loaded, given the weight attached to conditions for second tranche release. However, the increase in the size of the first tranche relative to the amount agreed with the government, sends wrong signals to the borrower, and reduces the credibility of our follow-up operations. In addition, as mentioned above, the funds were not used to capitalize the National Food Reserve Agency (NFRA), which had been the motivation for topping-up. Two areas of the credit represent major successes where the Malawian experience may be replicable for other countries. The approach of setting up an independent revenue authority with a fixed percentage of collections allocation for its operations, seems to have worked well in providing the motivation which the agency needed. Secondly, Malawi's success in privatizing both the commercial banks, and petroleum import sector in a small market where there is only room for a limited number of enterprises, suggests that with setting appropriate rules, these can still result in significant efficiency gains. This operation underlines the importance of having good analytic work as a basis for Bank lending and support to governments, analytic work which needs to be designed strategically, to avoid the risk of designing conditionality in the areas where solid analytical underpinnings already exist, instead of focusing on areas which are critical to the overall success of the Government's reform program.

title: Kenya - Economic and Public Sector Reform Credit Project
report_name: Kenya - Economic and Public Sector Reform Credit Project
project_id: KE-Kenya Economic & Public Sector Reform -- P069501
abstract: The Economic and Public Sector Reform Credit Project for Kenya had the following ratings: the outcome was unsatisfactory, the sustainability was likely, the institutional development impact was modest, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the borrower performance was unsatisfactory. The lessons learned indicate that conditions such as retrenching civil servants, or implementing an action plan are intermediate indicators, should be clearly specified in the program matrix. Far-reaching reforms, especially those requiring approval of parliament, require time to build support among key stakeholders. Assessing government commitment to reform is difficult, particularly when key decision-makers, such as the minister of finance, profess their commitment to the program. Understand the impact of the reforms on various stakeholders and work with government to develop mitigation strategies to gain support for reform. Focus on a limited set of key reforms. Offer a series of annual single tranche budget support credits, rather than multitranche operations. Take care to build effective partnerships with the IMF and other donors in designing and supervising policy based lending operations. Consult with the parliament, civil society, and other stakeholders in designing and implementing the reform program, and develop an effective communications strategy. Support increased participation of the private sector in the economy through deregulation, concessioning, and other forms of public-private partnerships, rather than insisting on privatization of specific enterprises.

title: Somalia - Water for Agro-Pastoral Productivity and Resilience Project
report_name: NA
project_id: SO-Somalia - Water for Agro-pastoral Productivity and Resilience -- P167826
abstract: Ratings for the Water for Agro-Pastoral Productivity and Resilience Project for Somalia were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: gradually increasing the scale of World Bank operations in fragility, conflict, and violence (FCV) contexts, from activities trust fund financed ASAs and smaller pilot projects such as the WAPP, contributes to improved implementation efficiency. In rural areas, a development approach that prioritizes the development of multiple-use water sources generates greater multisectoral benefits than a conventional water supply project design. Implementing projects through government systems in FCV contexts, while complex, can enhance government capacities and foster trust between citizens and state. Accommodating additional community needs and priorities can help foster trust and social cohesion in FCV contexts. The use of technology innovations for infrastructure site selection combines with a community driven development (CDD) approach are essential for ownership and sustainability.

title: Comoros - Integrated Development and Competitiveness Project
report_name: NA
project_id: KM-Integrated Development and Competitiveness Project -- P164584
abstract: Ratings for the Integrated Development and Competitiveness Project for Comoros were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: for large projects to succeed, transparent communication is essential to ensure stakeholder buy-in, build partnerships, and maintain commitment from the government and other stakeholders. The support to rural roads and Rural Economic Development Centers (CRDEs) had spillover effects, increasing entrepreneurship activities in the ecosystem. Shared infrastructure access, coupled with access to technical services and training, fosters collaboration among farmers, micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs), cooperatives, and other economic actors, facilitating their connection with local markets and expanding entrepreneurship culture and business networks. Importance of the Country Management Unit (CMU) support for the success of multi-sectoral projects in challenging fragile, conflict, and violence (FCV) environments. Considering a country’s structural constraints in project design and closely monitoring them during implementation is crucial, especially in FCV contexts. Clearly outlining the timeline and sequencing of interventions at the project’s start is vital to maximizing the integrated approach’s impact. An M and E system design sensitive to FCV context dynamics provides a basis for engaging government and stakeholders. Effective business plan competition (BPC) implementation requires iterations, continuous stakeholder engagement and upfront capacity building. Regular audits and reviews should be conducted to ensure compliance and address any deficiencies promptly. These measures will help reduce procurement delays, improve contract management, and ensure the successful execution of project activities.

title: Burundi - Great Lakes Regional Integrated Agriculture Development Project
report_name: NA
project_id: BI-Great Lakes Regional Integrated Agriculture Development Project -- P161781
abstract: NA

title: Implementation Completion and Results Report (ICR) Document - Water for Agropastoral Livelihoods Pilot Project - P152024
report_name: Implementation Completion and Results Report (ICR) Document - Water for Agropastoral Livelihoods Pilot Project - P152024
project_id: SO-Water for Agropastoral Livelihoods Pilot Project -- P152024
abstract: Ratings of Water for Agro-pastoral Livelihoods Pilot (WALP) Project for Somaliland were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was highly satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) addressing water fragility in FCV context is complex and not without risks; (ii) investments in institutional capacity building at all levels is critical to sustainability of investments; (iii) evidence-based, objective criteria-driven is critical to effective site selection; (iv) the wadi evaluation tool (WET), developed under the WALP project provided a valuable contribution to the process of finding potential areas for wadi water harvesting installations; (v) resources needed for supervision support and logistical challenges in FCV contexts should not be underestimated; and (vi) developing and mobilizing cost-effective, fit-for-purpose M&E systems in FCV settings is challenging.

title: Zambia - COMACO Landscape Management Project
report_name: Zambia - COMACO Landscape Management Project
project_id: ZM-Zambia COMACO Landscape Management -- P144254
abstract: The Community Markets for Conservation (COMACO) Landscape Management Project (CLMP) was a pilot project designed to apply a climate-smart landscape management approach for addressing drivers of deforestation. It provides lessons that could be useful in a future opportunity to scale up an integrated approach to jurisdictional landscape management in the Eastern Province of Zambia. The project is the first of its kind in Africa linking sustainable agriculture and forest conservation through results-based payments. The CLMP indicates that carbon finance is a catalyst to achieving results. Integrated landscape management projects can benefit from the use of blended finance instruments that use public sector finance to scale up private investments for emissions reduction. Some lessons learned included: cross-sectoral coordination is an important factor to successful carbon project implementation. Strong partnerships between implementation actors and local stakeholders should be promoted at all levels to improve project delivery and build sustainability. Access to market incentivizes sustainable agricultural land management (SALM) adoption and is important to achieving results. There is potential for scaling up the CLMP model, but this will rely on viable public and private sources of finance. Use of innovative approaches to deliver project objectives contribute to better assimilation and buy-in of beneficiaries.

title: Eastern and Southern Africa - Regional Pastoral Livelihoods Resilience Project
report_name: NA
project_id: 3E-RI-Regional Pastoral Livelihoods Resilience Project -- P129408
abstract: Ratings for the Regional Pastoral Livelihoods Resilience Project for Eastern and Southern Africa were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the regional approach of the project was useful in addressing conflict management, harmonized disease control actions and identification of investments in jointly used cross-border water and market infrastructure. The establishment of joint planning and monitoring platforms between project countries, facilitated the exchange of information, lessons and knowledge and skills that created an environment for the project to be effectively implemented. The implementation of activities in conflict prone areas added to the project’s complexity, however, interventions that yielded high enough returns to rival investments in livestock created alternative livelihoods that minimized conflicts in the region. The role of the community animal health workers (CAHW) was critical to expanding extension support for livestock services in the project areas. Building up the capacity of the CAHWs ensured that the support services will be retained within the communities even in the absence of project support. Livestock water resource investments made focused mainly on water supply for livestock, however, there was an emergence of human settlements near developed water points, which, if properly managed, can contribute towards a more sustainable management of such water resources. The introduction of alternative livelihoods was to diversify into activities that were less prone to risk, providing a buffer to pastoral communities to better withstand and recover more quickly from shocks including conflict.

title: Zambia - Livestock Development Animal Health Project
report_name: Zambia - Livestock Development Animal Health Project
project_id: ZM-Zambia: Livestock Development and Animal Health Project -- P122123
abstract: Ratings of Livestock Development Animal Health Project for China were as follows: outcome was moderately unsatisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was negligible. Some of the lessons learned include: (i) Addressing challenges of livestock production and productivity require a multipronged approach and a balanced attention to their main drivers or constraints; (ii) Institutional strengthening takes time and a project should not rely too heavily on expected reforms to achieve certain results; (iii) Simple and well adapted procedures, mechanisms and template for the preparation and approval of sub‐grants are critical not only to facilitate and accelerate the process but also, to ensure that the risk of elite capture is minimized; (iv) It is important to ensure clarity upfront on indicator definitions and scope to avoid ambiguities and minimize mid‐term revisions to indicators unless the scope of the project has changed; and (v) Results expectations should be realistic when project implementation relies exclusively on country systems to avoid setting the project up for eventual failure.

title: Kenya - Coastal Development Project
report_name: Kenya - Coastal Development Project
project_id: KE-Kenya Coastal Development Proejct -- P094692
abstract: Ratings for the Coastal Development Project for Kenya were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: in countries with decentralization policies (or constitutional devolution, as in Kenya) strong and proactive involvement of local governments may require additional institutional arrangements and capacity building efforts, but it is essential to enhance local ownership, support implementation requirements, and provide commitment to the long-term sustainability of project interventions. Projects with spatial development focus especially on coastal areas to be sustainable, the design phase must assess the appropriateness of the relevant policy and regulatory framework, and if needed, incorporate support to develop improved policies and or instruments to ensure that project investments are mainstreamed in public-sector mandates. The alternative of direct implementation by technical staff of activities such as studies, management plans, or other tasks requiring considerable field work can be a cost-effective measure compared to the time-consuming process of recruiting consultants. Business-related training of prospective or newly established small and medium enterprises (SMEs) can be of limited effectiveness if conducted in the absence of financial support to investments, particularly when high levels of poverty predominate among SME members. Early project restructurings can be instrumental in reverting potentially severe quality at entry limitations to achieve project objectives, including weak borrower ownership, inadequate institutional arrangements, and absence of basic implementation instruments.

title: Kenya - Second Phase of the Arid Lands Resource Management Project (ALRMP II)
report_name: Kenya - Second Phase of the Arid Lands Resource Management Project (ALRMP II)
project_id: KE-Arid Lands Resource Management Project Phase Two -- P078058
abstract: Ratings for the Second Phase of the Arid Lands Resource Management Project (ALRMP II) for Kenya were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: putting in place flexible institutional arrangements that development partners can use to channel support quickly, before climate emergencies occur, can help to improve coordination and efficiency. Implementation arrangements need to allow climate-stressed communities 'time out' to cope with emergencies. Community-driven development projects, or components, through the emphasis on beneficiary participation and empowerment, can be instruments for reducing the risks of corruption and poor governance, especially at the local level, provided that social accountability arrangements are properly designed and implemented by the Borrower and closely supervised. There is a particular need to design workable disclosure and complaints handling mechanisms, assign responsibilities for implementing them, and ensure that this takes place. The first phase of ALRMP did not include conflict resolution, but it very quickly became necessary and the project responded creatively. With this experience, ALRMP II designers planned to integrate conflict management into the natural resource management component. Beneficiaries and other stakeholders point to the resulting activities as having been among the project's most important contributions. Addressing conflicts over natural resources needs to be an integral part of projects in fragile locations that are experiencing in-migration or are over-populated (in relation to the resource base) and subject to increasingly adverse climate change impacts.

title: Kenya - Inclusive Growth and Fiscal Management Development Policy Operation
report_name: NA
project_id: KE-Kenya Inclusive Growth and Fiscal Management DPO 2 -- P172321,KE-Inclusive Growth and Fiscal Management Development Policy Financing -- P168204
abstract: Ratings for the Inclusive Growth and Fiscal Management Development Policy Operation for Kenya were as follows: overall outcome, relevance of prior actions, and achievement of objectives (efficacy) was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the need to ensure a triumvirate of support among key stakeholders including strong buy-in from higher level decision makers in government, strong senior management support at the World Bank, and support from development partners. The importance of identifying the confluence between government priorities and those of the World Bank. The importance of strong government co-ordination role, spear-headed by the national treasury. An important factor in the successful execution of the reform program was the strong technical capacity and reform appetite that exists among the policy making civil servant cadre in the Ministries, Departments, and Agencies (MDAs). The program illustrates the advantages of a strong analytical foundation for the choice of prior actions and the benefits of complementary support from World Bank operations (ASA, investment project financing (IPFs) and PFoRs).

title: Seychelles - Third South West Indian Ocean Fisheries Governance and Shared Growth Project (SWIOFish3)
report_name: NA
project_id: 3E-Third South West Indian Ocean Fisheries Governance and Shared Growth Project (SWIOFish3) -- P155642
abstract: NA

title: Eastern and Southern Africa - First South West Indian Ocean Fisheries Governance and Shared Growth Project
report_name: NA
project_id: 3E-AFCC2/RI-South West Indian Ocean Fisheries Governance and Shared Growth Project 1 -- P132123
abstract: Ratings for the First South West Indian Ocean Fisheries Governance and Shared Growth Project for Eastern and Southern Africa were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: at project closing, Comoros, Mozambique, and Tanzania had in place regional, national, and local governance systems that are key to the management of their fisheries sector, and efforts are ongoing by the World Bank to secure their sustainability, including by strengthening existing and additional revenue streams for the sector. Operations should be tailored to the absorption capacity and unique settings of each country. Operations in support of fisheries must strike a better balance between short-term visible results and long-term necessary reforms. Local co-management structures are an effective mechanism for engaging communities in decision making and orchestrating fisheries management actions. There is a case for continued support for the work of Southwest Indian Ocean Fisheries Commission (SWIOFC) given the shared resources, common issues, and the need for a strong regional voice.

title: Ethiopia - Second Pastoral Community Development Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Second Pastoral Community Development Project
project_id: ET-Pastoral Community Development Project II -- P108932
abstract: Ratings for the Second Pastoral Community Development Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included:  the first lesson learned is that communities gain confidence in their ability to solve their own problems when they receive appropriate training to support decision-making. The second lesson learned is that community empowerment can be achieved by consultation early in the process on their priorities for use of scarce project funds. The third lesson learned is that an Statement of Expenditure (SOE)-driven approach to CIF replenishments is too unwieldy and creates implementation delays. The fourth lesson learned is that RUSACCOs work well in promoting IGAs that communities are very familiar with, but less so on promoting new IGAs. Finally, the fifth lesson learned is that, with small-scale irrigation (SSI) interventions, crop yield improvements are almost automatic, but crop diversification is not.

title: Ethiopia - Rural Capacity Building Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Rural Capacity Building Project
project_id: ET-Rural Capacity Building Project -- P079275
abstract: Ratings for the Rural Capacity Building Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was substantial; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: the project offers a broad and diverse range of lessons, many of which are identified, discussed, and documented in the various case studies and implementation completion studies commissioned by the Project Management Unit (PMU) at the end of the project. Strengthening agricultural information and knowledge systems can yield significant returns. While this only confirms a broad body of literature, the project showed that the results can be achieved under difficult circumstances such as in the civil service system and a weak implementation capacity. The concept of 'capacity building' incorporates both the strengthening of existing systems and institutional reforms. Human resource development is a key part of capacity building. Because in-service training supported by projects is often not institutionalized and therefore does not follow normal training parameters, it runs the risk of being implemented in such a manner that the training is not effective or does not meet a required standard. To enhance women's participation, affirmative action and gender disaggregation can be a useful starting point.

title: Democratic Republic of Congo - First and Second Foundational Economic Governance Reforms Development Policy Financing Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ZR-Second DRC Foundational Economic Governance Reforms Development Policy Financing -- P179141,ZR-DRC Foundational Economic Governance Reforms DPF -- P177460
abstract: Ratings for the First and Second Foundational Economic Governance Reforms Development Policy Financing (DPF) Project for Democratic Republic of Congo were as follows: overall outcomes and achievement of objectives (efficacy) were moderately satisfactory, and relevance of prior actions and Bank performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: this first DPF series supported the promulgation and implementation of the new Telecom law, the implementation of a Treasury function at the Ministry of Finance, and reforms in the governance of the energy and water utilities with the competitive recruitment of key positions. However, it required a detailed understanding of the political economy, perseverance, and bold positioning. For instance, it was made clear to the client that the failure of the executive to enact the best practice Telecom Law supported by Bank TA and passed by the parliament would undermine the credibility of any reform that entails the adoption of a draft law by the cabinet. Recommendation: DPF TTLs need to identify and work with champions, stay the course when vested interests threaten to undermine reforms, and maintain a focus on effective implementation through programmatic support. Management needs to support TTLs by creating an environment conducive to informed risk-taking. DPFs in a low-capacity environment must be accompanied by technical assistance and/or investment projects. Most of the prior actions were supported by World Bank projects. Coordination with other donors was also important, for example in non-tax revenue collection where AfDB played an important role. The Central African Forest Initiative was instrumental in supporting forestry governance reforms, as the World Bank was re-engaging in the sector. Pressure from the IMF helped move the EITI agenda forward. However, investment projects are not always fully aligned with the timetable required by a DPF. This contributed to the delays in the de-concentration of spending. A new two-operation series approved by the Board in May 2025. The program supports continued de-concentration of public spending as well as further reforms in procurement and SOE governance, including special attention to the electricity utility. It also introduces asset declaration and promotes private sector development.

title: Mozambique - Second Phase of the Conservation Areas for Biodiversity and Development Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MZ-Mozambique Conservation Areas for Biodiversity and Development - Phase 2 -- P166802
abstract: Ratings of the Second Phase of the Conservation Areas for Biodiversity and Development Project for Mozambique were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: Continuous Institutional Capacity Building and Strengthening institutions like National Administration of Protected Areas (ANAC) and BIOFUND are crucial for sustainable conservation efforts; Conservation depends on formal and effective community engagement and long-term engagement in the landscape approach; Land use planning is another core factor in conservation efforts; Collaborative Management Partnership (CMP) model in Mozambique helped integrate local communities, private entities, and Non- Governmental Organizations (NGOs) into enhanced biodiversity conservation; and Simplifying procurement processes and decentralizing clearances was crucial in Matching Grant Scheme (MGS).

title: Ethiopia - Resilient Landscapes and Livelihoods Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ET-Ethiopia Resilient Landscapes and Livelihoods Project -- P163383
abstract: Ratings for the Resilient Landscapes and Livelihoods Project (RLLP) for Ethiopia were as follows: outcome and Bank performance were satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: a strategic lesson from Sustainable Land Management Project Phase 1 (SLMP-I) and SLMP-II reflected in the design and implementation of RLLP is the need to provide reasonable time to achieve the stabilization of degraded areas, complemented by mechanisms and supporting elements to allow watershed communities to evolve from project-based assistance and adopt and consolidate sustainable management of restored landscapes through government programs. In the design of any NRM project, security of land ownership or user rights represents a key factor for strengthening community incentives for investment in and maintenance of SLM interventions. Highly decentralized SLM implementation is a major factor of project success. Fiduciary lessons regarding the overall support to the SLM Program highlight the importance of: (a) aligning project budgeting systems with the Government of Ethiopia (GoE) budget calendar; (b) including the costs of environmental mitigation measures in subproject designs; and (c) providing adequate resources to upgrade planning, budgeting, and monitoring of inputs and outputs that are essential for decentralized operations.

title: Zambia - Integrated Forest Landscape Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ZM-Zambia Integrated Forest Landscape Project -- P161490
abstract: NA

title: Eastern and Southern Africa - Development Response to Displacement Impacts Project in the Horn of Africa
report_name: NA
project_id: 3E-Development Response to Displacement Impacts Project (DRDIP) in the Horn of Africa -- P161067
abstract: Ratings of Development Response to Displacement Impacts Project in the Horn of Africa for Kenya were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some of the lessons learned include:The project design, which favored national level control, did not take full advantage of the unique opportunity afforded by devolution; Lack of systematic integration between components/concentration of resources; Implementation structures need clear lines of responsibility and accountability; Subprojects should have more adaptable budgets; and Engineering capacity needs to better incorporated into project design for climate adaptation and sustainability.

title: Mozambique - Forest Investment Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MZ-Mozambique Forest Investment Project -- P160033
abstract: Ratings for the Forest Investment Project for Mozambique (MozFIP) were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the project design can critically affect the quality and sustainability of forest plantations with rotation period longer than the project length. MozFIP demonstrated the need to improve the selection criteria of project beneficiaries to avoid allocation of questionably high subsidies to larger, already established commercial enterprises. Use of private specialized service providers for field-intensive projects presents a cost-effective alternative to ensure adequate support to beneficiaries during project implementation. The matching grant system did not generate the desired results for the project. Although the assessment suggests that this unsatisfactory performance is largely due to the extremely short implementation period of the scheme, the criteria, and conditions for the effective use of matching grants should be further evaluated to determine if such instrument represents an appropriate mechanism to contribute to the objectives of forest-related subprojects. he encouraging results recorded from the successful implementation of the gender-based gender action learning system (GALS) within MOZFIP will suggest the convenience of mainstreaming the initiative in other projects, particularly those associated with agroforestry systems, as is now being adopted by Sustainable Rural Economy Program (MozRural) and Northern Mozambique Rural Resilience Project (MozNorte), not only for the rural communities but also within fisheries communities.

title: Uganda - Water Management and Development Project
report_name: Uganda - Water Management and Development Project
project_id: UG-Water Management and Development Project -- P123204
abstract: The Water Management and Development Project for Uganda had a satisfactory outcome. Bank performance was moderately satisfactory while M&E Quality was modest. The project development objectives were to improve (i) integrated water resources planning, management and development; and (ii) access to water and sanitation services in priority urban areas. The project will contribute to higher level goals of sustaining natural resources, improving service delivery, and increasing economic productivity. The PDO indicators were not formally revised during project implementation. The components were not formally revised. However, the scope of components one and two was reduced for two reasons. Some lessons learned during the project include: Assist the client to build capacity as early as possible; Restructure as soon as a required change in design or scope is known; and Maintain a focus on delivering services to the end customer.

title: Madagascar - Third Environmental Program Support Project
report_name: Madagascar - Third Environmental Program Support Project
project_id: MG-Third Environment Program Support Project -- P074235,MG-Support to Madagascar's Foundation for Protected Areas and Biodiversity -- P113976,MG-Madagascar Third Environment Program Support Project -- P074236
abstract: Ratings for the Third Environmental Program Support Project for Madagascar were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was high, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: since poverty and natural resource degradation are closely linked, economic development needs to be part of all conservation efforts, and local communities need to have economic incentives to engage in conservation activities. Community participation is instrumental in reducing pressures on natural resources. Community participation in natural resources management has been underutilized in EP3 and has not produced the expected results. Despite the inherent complexities, the Bank must make all possible efforts to identify effective and suitable models of integration between PAs and communities. Addressing the illegal exploitation of flora and fauna species requires a set of skills often found in enforcement agencies. EP3, particularly following the approval of the AF, supported the efforts of the government to curb the traffic in precious woods. It was thanks to the collaboration with Interpol and the World Customs Organization that the satellite surveillance activities financed by EP3 could help track shipments of precious woods from Madagascar. Future projects dealing with natural resources poaching should be designed in partnership with organizations such as the ones that form the International Consortium on Combating Wildlife Crime (ICCWC), of which the Bank is a partner.

title: Congo, Democratic Republic of - Forest and Nature Conservation Project
