report_name: Congo, Democratic Republic of - Forest and Nature Conservation Project
project_id: ZR-DRC Rehabilitation and Participatory Management of Key Protected Areas in the DRC -- P111621,ZR-Forest and Nature Conservation Project -- P100620
abstract: Ratings for the Forest and Nature Conservation Project for Democratic Republic of Congo were as follows: outcomes was moderately unsatisfactory, global environment facility (GEO) outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, risk to development outcome was high, risk to GEO outcome was high, Bank performance was unsatisfactory, and Borrower performance was unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: relying on policy or regulatory pre-conditions, such as the government decrees for the timber production control and marketing program (PCPCB) and for community forestry, poses risks, as commitment to meeting those preconditions can change over time. Complex projects in challenging settings require the best project management talent the Bank can muster. Investments in community income-generating opportunities in forest areas offer promising avenues for poverty reduction. Engagements in governance issues in settings with a complex political economy should perhaps consider phased approaches, with incentives for delivery, such as disbursements linked to performance. Reliance on co-financing is inherently risky in countries with constrained fiscal space. Cost-sharing between different financial sources can create obstacles for disbursements.

title: Kenya - Natural Resource Management Project
report_name: Kenya - Natural Resource Management Project
project_id: KE-Kenya - Natural Resource Management Project -- P095050
abstract: Ratings for the Natural Resource Management (NRM) Project for Kenya were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the project offers a number of lessons on institutions, investments, information and implementation for future GOK programming and external financial support to natural resources and the sectors it drives or helps protect. It also provides important lessons for Kenya as it devolves to lower levels of political and resource management. The project adds to the evidence base that natural resources including water, forest, and soils typically benefit from strong and fair participatory approaches to managing the resource and sharing the benefits from the resource, underpinned by robust resource monitoring and grievance redress. Sustainable NRM actions should therefore consider including: (i) strong environmental and socio-economic monitoring underpinned by strong data gathering and information management, (ii) good incentives for local resource users to avoid free rider behavior that degrades natural wealth, (iii) secure and predictable rights to resources by local communities, and (iv) effective coordination on planning and prioritization among stakeholders and sectors, given that changes in an upstream area (forested watersheds) affect downstream actors (villages, irrigation users, cities) and can drive conflict.

title: Congo, Democratic Republic of - Transitional Support for Economic Recovery Operation
report_name: Congo, Democratic Republic of - Transitional Support for Economic Recovery Operation
project_id: ZR-Democratic Republic of Congo Transitional Support for Economic Recovery -- P091990
abstract: Ratings for the Democratic Republic of the Congo Transitional Support for Economic Recovery Operation were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was high, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: 1) By providing a framework for a policy dialogue not only with individual sector ministries but also with the Government as a whole (including key ministries such as the Ministry of Finance), a development policy lending/loan (DPL) operation provides increased leverage for a number of improvements in each sector which may not have been achieved through specific sectoral programs or projects; 2) a DPL operation with a single tranche disbursement for prior actions and progress indicators for future reforms is sensible only if there is a follow-up operation when the progress indicators are being achieved; and 3) in post conflict countries, it is important to strike the right balance between insisting on solid, immediate reforms and not jeopardizing the fragile peace.

title: Ethiopia - Energy Access Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Energy Access Project
project_id: ET-ENERGY ACCESS PROJECT -- P077380,ET-ENERGY ACCESS PROJECT -- P049395
abstract: Ratings for the Energy Access Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, global environmental objective (GEO) outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, risk to GEO outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: multi-sector energy project without a strong, cross-sector institutional focus can result in a loss of commitment and underachievement of objectives. Notwithstanding the need to address urgent issues in multiple energy subsectors, if project scope and design are not tailored to the institutional capacity of the implementing agencies, there is a substantial risk of delays and limited achievement of project objectives. The lack of performance indicators for capacity building and the absence of a clear linkage to planning for staff-development limit the extent of impact assessment for training and technical assistance programs. If the detailed design of a major project is not completed prior to credit approval for an investment operation, there is a high risk of delay and or cancellation. The lack of a clear results chain in a monitoring and evaluation (M and E) framework can lead to major data gaps and missed opportunities for impact evaluation. The lack of a system of standardized testing for improved fuel wood stoves prevents a full assessment of the program's impact on reducing emissions from the burning of wood. The lack of detailed coordination of rehabilitation work for urban power distribution systems with city planning authorities and shortages of local construction materials can be major obstacles to timely completion of the work.

title: Congo, Democratic Republic of - Economic Recovery Project
report_name: Congo, Democratic Republic of - Economic Recovery Project
project_id: ZR-Democratic Republic of Congo Economic Recovery Credit -- P057293
abstract: The project outcome is satisfactory, and sustainability likely, although institutional development impacts are rated as modest. The main lesson highlights the fact that, despite countries affected by conflict, with institutions weakened by years of institutional decline and conflict, the design and implementation of programs, with a visible impact on the human scale is attainable. The bold project approach contributed significantly to the peace process, in that it provided a perspective of renewed cooperation, and return to economic growth, supported by a political leadership, and commitment to the implementation of reforms, the drafting of legislation, and institutionalizing reforms in key areas of governance, and finance. The appropriate identification of the country's specific needs through the Bank's strategy - the transitional support strategy (TSS) - conduced to stabilization, followed by the adjustment program, and, served as an instrument guiding, and ensuring consistency of the project objectives. Upon the authorities' concern on delays regarding the transfer of funds, and, in response to this issue, the Bank agreed - that given the functional problems in the banking system, and, under a follow-up operation to this project - funds would be deposited directly into well-regulated commercial banks, subject to appropriate fiduciary arrangements already in place.

title: Tanzania - Forest Conservation and Management Project, and Eastern Arc Forests Conservation and Management Project
report_name: Tanzania - Forest Conservation and Management Project, and Eastern Arc Forests Conservation and Management Project
project_id: TZ-Eastern Arc Forests Conservation and Management Project -- P057234,TZ-Forest Conservation and Management Project -- P058706
abstract: Ratings for the Forest Conservation and Management Project, and Eastern Arc Forests Conservation and Management Project for Tanzania were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, risk to Global Environmental Objective (GEO) was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: sustainable forest sector reforms require strong political and technical leadership. Reforms also need to be in line with government priorities. Transparency in the consultative process ensures that staff/employees, at all levels, feel ownership and consider change as an opportunity rather than a threat to their livelihood. Projects implemented during times of significant policy change require enhanced, constant engagement and supervision. This includes utilizing outside expertise with strong in-country experience, mobilizing senior Bank management, and ensuring flexibility in the design to respond to changes in priorities while retaining the overall goals. Forest sector projects tend to be overly ambitious, aiming to address multiple challenges (e.g. sector reform, sustainable management, governance, community participation, etc.) in a single operation. Recipient implementation capacity is often insufficient to address existing complexities within the given timeframe and limited resources are spread too thinly. As a consequence, restructuring is often required to adjust ambitious project designs to realities on the ground, helping to achieve a few tangible results. So as to yield sustainable project outcomes, project designs should be more focused, allocating sufficient and targeted financial resources.

title: Lesotho - Maloti-Drakensberg Transfrontier Conservation and Development Project
report_name: Lesotho - Maloti-Drakensberg Transfrontier Conservation and Development Project
project_id: LS-Maloti-Drakensberg Transfrontier Conservation and Development Project -- P052367
abstract: Ratings for the Maloti-Drakensberg Transfrontier Conservation and Development Project for Lesotho were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some key lessons learned from the project include: transfrontier cooperation in biodiversity conservation can be achieved through parallel implementation that takes into account the uniqueness of the countries involved, while maintaining synergies for cross-learning and collaboration. Such a design allows sovereign decision making and gives sufficient freedom to choose specific implementation speed and processes, while generating the necessary level of national ownership. While the transfrontier cooperation was fully accomplished with significant transfer of knowledge from the advanced conservation and tourism experiences in South Africa, Lesotho was able to deliver on its own achievements. This appears a particularly suitable model in the context of countries that differ in size and development status. Good project design and detailed preparation is necessary especially when local implementation capacity is low. The design should be simple and should take into account local institutional and human capacity. It is not advisable to expect major tasks to be completed between appraisal and effectiveness (e.g. condition of effectiveness). Moreover, such gaps indicate lack of readiness of the project. A well prepared logical framework should be the backbone of the project design and should be fully agreed prior to project implementation. Such framework needs to clearly show the linkages between components and their outcomes with the overall development objective. Frequent changes in task management during project design and implementation can cause serious disruption, when task managers are leaving without finishing important milestone tasks.

title: South Africa - Maloti-Drakensberg Transfrontier Conservation and Development Project
report_name: South Africa - Maloti-Drakensberg Transfrontier Conservation and Development Project
project_id: ZA-Maloti-Drakensberg Transfrontier Conservation and Development Project -- P052368
abstract: Ratings for the Maloti-Drakensberg Transfrontier Conservation and Development Project for South Africa were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: transfrontier cooperation in biodiversity conservation can be achieved through parallel implementation that takes into account the uniqueness of the countries involved, while maintaining synergies for cross-learning and collaboration. Such a design allows sovereign decision making and sufficient freedom to choose specific implementation speed and processes, while generating the necessary level of national ownership. While the transfrontier cooperation was fully accomplished with significant transfer of knowledge from the advanced conservation and tourism experiences in South Africa, Lesotho was able to deliver on its own achievements. This appears a particularly suitable model in the context of countries that differ in size and development status. In countries with high local capacity it is of particular importance to closely involve local institutions in project design and detailed preparation. In Republic of South Africa (RSA) the local capacity was high and many project design concepts evolved and have been developed during project implementation by the local institutions and implementers (e.g. PES (Payment for Eco-services schemes) or stewardship mechanisms), while or original design concepts proved inappropriate for the local situation (e.g. tourism development or conservancies). A well prepared logical framework should be the backbone of project design and should be fully agreed prior to project implementation. Such framework needs to clearly show the linkages between components and their outcomes with the overall environment objective. Objective, indicators and targets need to be realistic, helpful for management and take into account the time-frame of project implementation. A baseline not only helps to measure success, but also disciplines the designers to pay attention to realistic and measurable indicators. Frequent changes in task management during project design and implementation can cause serious disruption, when task managers are leaving without finishing important milestone tasks. Examples under this project were the finalization and agreement on the logical framework at the design stage, the completion of the restructuring of the project at mid-term or finishing the complex and time consuming design and procurement process of the environmental centre at the later stage of the project.

title: Kenya - Accelerating Reforms for an Inclusive and Resilient Recovery Development Policy Financing
report_name: NA
project_id: KE-Accelerating Reforms for an Inclusive and Resilient Recovery DPF 2 -- P176903
abstract: Ratings for the Accelerating Reforms for an Inclusive and Resilient Recovery Development Policy Financing in Kenya were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the relevance of prior actions was satisfactory, and the Bank's performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: while this operation was being prepared, the world was experiencing a high degree of economic, political, and social uncertainty due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Even after the initial 2021 recovery and as vaccines are applied, new strains of the disease appeared. In this environment, the team adjusted the operation to reflect new government priorities, while at the same time keeping the focus on building a stronger healthcare system. For example, as described, pillars were modified as well as some triggers which became prior actions. Although the size of the policy matrix raised initial concerns, it was an opportunity to build consensus around policy reforms which had already reached a point of maturity. The June 2024 youth-led protests opposing tax reforms under the proposed FY2024-25 budget highlight the need to further strengthen the political and social risk evaluation of reforms supported by future operations. Although not directly related to WBG operations in this case, these events pose the need to strengthen and confirm the public consultation process by Government for future reforms and maximize the assent from the population. This is not restricted to DPFs and could extend to other World Bank financing instruments including PforRs and IPFs. The protests have strengthened the awareness for greater accountability on needed reforms, such as expenditure control and anticorruption measures that could contribute to the achievement of results indicators agreed upon in future DPFs.

title: Mozambique - Smallholder Irrigated Agriculture and Market Access Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MZ-Smallholder Irrigated Agriculture and Market Access Project- IRRIGA  1 -- P164431
abstract: Ratings for the Smallholder Irrigated Agriculture and Market Access Project for Mozambique were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: irrigation kits using solar-powered pumps can be effective in enabling farmers to generate high returns, especially when used for high-value, off-season vegetables. The project distributed a pump per two farmers to maximize returns on the investment. Farmers obtained high vegetable yields and high returns, especially in the off-season. This arrangement needs to be explored further to assess the impact on maintenance, as the pumps were distributed late in project implementation, preventing a full assessment. Irrigated agriculture provides a great opportunity for fostering private sector participation in financing agricultural inputs. Unreliable weather patterns create hesitation in financing agriculture, including from agro input dealers. However, with irrigation, many agro-dealers were willing to provide inputs to farmers. Although the matching grants activities under the project did not take off, private input supply arrangements stepped in to fill the gap and are more sustainable. In the 2021 project restructuring, fiduciary responsibilities were transferred to the FNDS, while INIR retained the planning, regulatory, and coordination roles. This institutional arrangement proved effective in supporting climate-sensitive reforms. INIR with its sector-specific expertise and mandate to oversee irrigation in the country, demonstrated strong responsiveness to the need for updating institutional frameworks and took a proactive approach in designing irrigation schemes for rehabilitation damaged the extreme climate events that could be adapted throughout the project lifecycle, particularly to mitigate climate risks such as the impacts of cyclones. This level of technical foresight and adaptive responsiveness may have been less likely if FNDS had taken on these technical roles. This experience underscores the value of leveraging the technical expertise and institutional knowledge of sector-specific agencies to enhance climate resilience in irrigation infrastructure and planning.

title: Rwanda - Second Phase of the Fourth Transformation of Agriculture Sector Program-for-Results
report_name: NA
project_id: RW-Transformation of Agriculture Sector Program 4 Phase 2 -- P161876
abstract: Ratings for the Second Phase of the Fourth Transformation of Agriculture Sector Program-for-Results (PforR) for Rwanda were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: PforR instrument can be a great tool to support policy reforms and transformational interventions. PforRs should integrate a gradual process for delivery of introduced reforms and other best practices. Public-private partnerships in productive alliances can sustain smallholder farmers linkages to markets. Increased capacity for developing commercially viable projects and a legal framework integrating agribusiness contextual aspects will be critical for sustainable and profitable investments.

title: Malawi - First Phase of the Shire Valley Transformation Program Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MW-Shire Valley Transformation Program - Phase 1 -- P158805
abstract: Ratings for the First Phase of the Shire Valley Transformation Program Project for Malawi were as follows: outcomes were unsatisfactory, the Bank's performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: a concept similar to the ‘dam safety panel of experts’ could be applied to large distribution canal infrastructure, such as the SVTP. Ensuring the feasibility studies engage a highly qualified consulting firm with a multidisciplinary team is essential to achieve cost-effective designs. The shortcomings identified late in the main canal designs could have been avoided with an independent review by reputable irrigation project design specialists, especially if conducted at multiple stages of completion. Additionally, projects in countries prone to frequent climate-related events should integrate resilience measures into their design. In complex projects, while hiring international and external experts is necessary, it is equally essential to build a solid knowledge base among government staff responsible for implementing the project. Strengthening local expertise enables government officials to critically assess deliverables from service providers, a challenge that the SVTP has faced due to substandard outputs. Although the PAD acknowledged the limited institutional capacity to manage the project and emphasized the need for specialized technical assistance and project management support, these measures proved insufficient to ensure high-quality deliverables. The PMT placed considerable confidence in the capabilities of the international engineering firm, which failed to deliver the expected standards. Furthermore, project management support was limited to periodic World Bank missions rather than continuous on-the-ground assistance. Projects of this complexity demand more sophisticated monitoring and may even require an international management company to support local project teams.

title: Madagascar - Sustainable Landscape Management Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MG-Sustainable Landscape Management Project -- P154698
abstract: Ratings for the Sustainable Landscape Management Project for Madagascar were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the project’s innovative nature highlighted the significance of a flexible design and a practical approach to integrated landscape management. Maintenance of irrigation schemes can be hindered by inadequate user fee collection and ineffective maintenance funds for the functioning of community organizations. Institutional strengthening through capacity building of government staff, communities, and farmer groups contribute to sustainability of project outcomes. Community management expectations were found to be unrealistic within the project's timeframe, as communities require more time to build expertise and mobilize funds. The payment for ecosystem services (PES) component was overly complex and ambitious, resulting in delayed payments beyond the project’s duration and causing imbalances between upstream and downstream communities.

title: Mozambique - Agriculture and Natural Resources Landscape Management Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MZ-Moz Agriculture and Natural Resources Landscape Management Project -- P149620
abstract: Ratings for the Agriculture and Natural Resources Landscape Management Project for Mozambique were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: the landscape small emerging commercial farmer (SECF) model was effective in enhancing smallholder farmers’ capacity to use knowledge and adopt sustainable agricultural practices and technologies to increase agricultural productivity. The lack of comprehensive evaluation report and the impact study at the end of the project implementation was a missed opportunity as the project experience suggest that the Landscape model has a great potential to be effective in promoting sustainable market-oriented agriculture and simultaneously investing on sustainable natural resources management. The matching grants mechanism was a successful instrument for financing farmers to purchase equipment and other modern inputs. However, the low adherence of financial institutions to provide complementary finance to SECFs needs to be further evaluated. The project developed a weather-based insurance product, which appeared to be very attractive to farmers. Successful scale up of operations requires a realistic assessment of the existing capacities based on lessons learned.

title: Mozambique - First and Second Agriculture Development Policy Operations Projects
report_name: Mozambique - First and Second Agriculture Development Policy Operations Projects
project_id: MZ-MZ Second Agriculture Development Policy Operation AgDPO-2 -- P146930,MZ-MZ First Agriculture Development Policy Operation AgDPO-1 -- P129489
abstract: Ratings for the First and Second Agriculture Development Policy Operations Projects for Mozambique were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: reforms actions are not business as usual and requires the commitment of a government that is reform minded for it to happen. Institutional leverage is key to effective coordination and monitoring of progress in the reform actions supported. It is equally important to ensure that any risks related to data availability are identified and accounted for upfront before concluding project preparation. Defining development policy operation (DPO) series objectives in concrete and measurable terms and linking them to implementation on the ground can improve demonstration of impact and enhance attribution. In a context where sector DPO series seldom reach completion, it is important to ensure that this risk is mitigated in the way that reform actions are sequenced. In this instance, the termination of the third operation in the series was a missed opportunity to consolidate on the gains of preceding operations.

title: Kenya - Kenya Water Security and Climate Resilience Project
report_name: NA
project_id: KE-Kenya Water Security and Climate Resilience Project -- P117635
abstract: Ratings of the Kenya Water Security and Climate Resilience Project for Kenya were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and M&E quality was substantial. Lessons learned include that combining large-scale irrigation investments with broad institutional and policy reforms in a single investment operation can dilute focus and reduce efficiency; large irrigation schemes are better implemented as stand-alone projects, while legislative and policy reforms that require Cabinet or Parliamentary action are more suitable for Development Policy Financing. Land acquisition is a critical path item that demands early and sustained attention; future operations should align engineering and land teams from the outset to sequence site handover effectively, communicate compensation arrangements to farmers clearly and consistently, plan and fund crop compensation in sync with construction schedules, strengthen institutional and legal capacity, secure financing early, and maintain continuous coordination. Irrigation strategy should pivot toward farmer-led irrigation to achieve faster, scalable impact, with any necessary large-scale schemes delivered through a two-stage approach: Stage 1 to complete feasibility, detailed design, and land acquisition, and Stage 2 to execute construction and embed sustainability measures such as agricultural support and operation and maintenance. Technical outcomes must be matched by robust financial planning for operation and maintenance; institutions such as the Water Resources Authority require reliable revenue and timely regulatory support to sustain hydrometeorological networks and early warning systems, and irrigation schemes need predictable financing through well designed irrigation service fees or public transfers. Clear financial strategies that identify funding sources, assess revenue potential, and set affordable, sustainable pricing can strengthen accountability and ownership, improve service performance over time, and secure the long-term benefits of water investments.

title: Comoros - Economic Reform Development Policy Grant Project
report_name: Comoros - Economic Reform Development Policy Grant Project
project_id: KM-Comoros Development Policy Grant 2 -- P122941
abstract: Ratings for the Economic Reform Development Policy Grant Project for Comoros were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: timely capacity assistance through adequate coordination with other sector operations and other donors is critical to improve governments capacity to implement reforms. Although technical assistance is critical to support the implementation of reforms, it is not always sufficient for entrenching change in national processes. The design of reforms must also complement the existing capacities, be of relevance from a political economy perspective, and be tailored to the existing practicalities. Additional consensus may be warranted when implementing reforms that touch upon the power sharing between the Island governments and the Union. Better understanding should foster trust among the parties and help move the agenda forward. Bank supervision needs to follow up with the government more closely regarding the steps they need to take to achieve the indicators supported by the operation.

title: Malawi - Shire River Basin Management Program Project
report_name: Malawi - Shire River Basin Management Program Project
project_id: MW-Malawi: Shire River Basin Management Program (Phase-I) Project -- P117617
abstract: Ratings of Shire River Basin Management Program Project for Malawi were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) Reversing watershed degradation requires significant investment in strengthening institutional capacities at all levels; (ii) A community-led landscape approach to watershed restoration can achieve results at scale and help build resilience at basin scale; (iii) Improving water resources management requires a combination of investments in institutions, information and infrastructure; (iv) Choices about project design and scope should consider the capacity of implementing agencies; and (v) Inter-agency collaboration on multi-sectoral projects requires strong government commitment and deliberate design choices.

title: Rwanda - Third Rural Sector Support Project
report_name: Rwanda - Third Rural Sector Support Project
project_id: RW-Third Rural Sector Support Project -- P126440
abstract: Ratings for the Third Rural Sector Support Project (RSSP) for Rwanda were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the project proved that important impacts can be achieved if a strategically relevant approach is continued and perfected over time. The relevance and benefits of irrigation are now well documented and understood in the Rwandan agriculture context, yet much of the infrastructure and accompanying governance structure were developed and introduced through the RSSP projects (in the marshlands) and land husbandry, water harvesting, and hillside irrigation (LWH) (in the hillsides). The successive RSSP interventions revealed that, besides a favorable policy environment, there is a critical mass of interventions targeted at value chain actors that can turn around the respective value chains, and set them on a path of sustainable performance.

title: Zambia - Irrigation Development and Support Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ZM-Irrigation Development and Support Project -- P102459
abstract: Ratings of Irrigation Development and Support Project for Zambia were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some of the lessons learned include: Importance of timely government funding; Community involvement in project planning; and Enhancing community engagement and capacity building.

title: Burundi - Agro-Pastoral Productivity and Markets Development Project
report_name: Burundi - Agro-Pastoral Productivity and Markets Development Project
project_id: BI-BI - Agro-Pastoral Productivity and Markets Development Project -- P107343
abstract: Ratings of Agro-Pastoral Productivity and Markets Development Project (PRODEMA) for Burundi were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: (i) PRODEMA under its specific community driven development (CDD) design has, over a decade, successfully combined support to agriculture production, infrastructure development, and access to market; (ii) The introduction of innovations in agriculture, particularly at the processing stage, which often requires sophisticated equipment, should be carried out with a sufficient lead time, so that the equipment can be tested and made functional, and the operators can be trained; (iii) The integration of crop and animal husbandry permits to achieve a symbiotic production process leading to greater productivity; (iv) The solidarity chain regarding animal allocation further to the first round of project support is instrumental to ensure that benefits are widely shared in the larger rural community; (v) Market analysis is a must for the development of value chains when surpluses are expected to be generated; it needs to be conducted in realistic fashion adapted to the local market context; and (vi) A specific legislation to establish Producer Organizations (POs) is needed to accompany development efforts and ensure that producers are in command.

title: Rwanda - Land Husbandry, Water Harvesting, and Hillside Irrigation Project
report_name: Rwanda - Land Husbandry, Water Harvesting, and Hillside Irrigation Project
project_id: RW-Land Husbandry, Water Harvesting and Hillside Irrigation -- P114931
abstract: Ratings of Land Husbandry, Water Harvesting and Hillside Irrigation Project for Rwanda were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) The Project proved that site specific conditions were critical for determining what type of land husbandry package should be applied; (ii) Whole value chain interventions, and public-private partnership for sustainable results; (iii) Effective cooperation between Government and development partners; (iv) Building good governance systems; (v) The government’s hillside intensification objectives could not have been successfully achieved without the strong ownership and engagement of farmers in production and marketing activities; (vi) Involvement of local leaders (village leaders and GRC leaders) was key in the resolution of conflicts over plots among neighbors, land owners and owner of assets on that land, as well as in the resolution of inter-family issues over ownership of assets or of social issues, such as the proper management or use of compensation funds received by some displaced Project Affected Person (PAPs).

title: Republic of Rwanda - Poverty Reduction Support Series four to seven
report_name: Republic of Rwanda - Poverty Reduction Support Series four to seven
project_id: RW-Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Grant -- P113241,RW-Fourth Poverty Reduction Strategy Grant -- P104990,RW-Seventh Poverty Reduction Support Grant -- P117495,RW-Fifth Poverty Reduction Support  Grant -- P106083
abstract: Ratings for the Four to Seven Series Poverty Reduction Support Project for Republic of Rwanda were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, risk to development outcome was low or negligible, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned include: second poverty reduction support financing (PRSF) series proved to be effective in advancing government-owned reforms. The PRSF series has provided important support to the Government to operationalize its ambitious medium-term development strategy i.e., the economic development and poverty reduction strategy (EDPRS) by translating its objectives into priority actions. The PRSF series provided substantial financing to Rwanda's budget, including during the 2008 crisis. It was successful in fostering better donor coordination, improved harmonization and reinforced support for reforms among the country's budget support donors: the World Bank, African Development Bank, and bilateral donors -all partners in the Budget Support Harmonization Group and part of the semi-annual joint budget support reviews (JBSR). PRSF series focused on capacity building, including sub-national levels of government in planning. The success of the PRSF series was also largely helped by its ability to base its design on strong sector dialogue. This has helped to: (i) ensure the PRSF reflects substantive policy agreements reached at the sector level; (ii) strengthen the capacity of sector ministries to present their needs and priorities during national level policy discussions and budget negotiations; and (iii) facilitate coordination of the Bank's inputs into the common performance assessment framework (CPAF) policy discussions in a timely manner. Considering the preparation and implementation of the series coincided with the CPAF establishment in 2008, this period can be regarded as a transition or learning period for all parties involved in budget support in Rwanda. Also, growing mutual understanding of what each partner would need to get out of the process helped it to become smoother over time.

title: Angola - Market Oriented Smallholder Agriculture Project
report_name: Angola - Market Oriented Smallholder Agriculture Project
project_id: AO-AO-Market Oriented Smallholder Agriculture Project -- P093699
abstract: Ratings for the Market Oriented Smallholder Agriculture Project (MOSAP) for Angola were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the Farmer Field School (FFS) approach to agricultural extension was effective in enhancing smallholder farmers’ capacity to generate and use new knowledge and adopt improved agricultural practices and technology. A capable and internationally experienced service provider will be required to help farmers’ organizations develop and implement investment proposals, especially for value chain investments. The use of small, local service providers to assist farmers’ associations in the preparation and implementation of MOSAP subprojects was problematic because of their limited technical and organizational capacity. Improving the quality of technical and commercial assistance and making it available to a much larger number of beneficiaries will require recruitment of a highly capable and internationally experienced service provider who will also train the local NGOs and private sector subcontractors with whom they will work. During MOSAP implementation, the project had difficulty finding local qualified professionals to effectively undertake the fiduciary and M&E functions. The lack of qualified procurement staff was in part responsible for the delayed start of project implementation. While hiring was possible within a reasonable period, the selected consultants were not familiar with World Bank procedures.

title: Rwanda - Second Rural Sector Support Project
report_name: Rwanda - Second Rural Sector Support Project
project_id: RW-Second Rural Sector Support -- P105176
abstract: Ratings for the Second Rural Sector Support Project (RSSP 2) for Rwanda were as follows: outcomes were highly satisfactory, risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was highly satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: The strong, demonstrated commitment of government at various levels, and effective mechanisms to sustain that commitment, are vital for success and essential to embark on a scaling up phase. Close implementation support by the Bank and sector was invaluable for resolving implementation challenges. The marshland investment model developed in RSSP offers a basic framework for replication throughout Rwanda. The model has been the basis for new policies and institutions throughout the sector. Approaches that enhance farmers' business skills, promote value chains, and link farmers to financial institutions provide inclusive, sustainable benefits. RSSP 2 demonstrates the wisdom of continuity in the financing mechanism and implementation teams. A programmatic approach (financing mechanism) is advantageous to consolidate, scale up, and sustain project benefits from a previous phase, while ensuring clear criteria and initiatives for new interventions. Water User Associations (WUAs) should be established and strengthened at the start of irrigation works. Instead of working with multiple hillside cooperatives spread throughout the country, RSSP 3 is adopting a more targeted and approach of establishing and strengthening cooperatives on the hillsides that surround RSSP 3 marshlands.

title: Tanzania - Agricultural Sector Development Project
report_name: Tanzania - Agricultural Sector Development Project
project_id: TZ-Tanzania Agricultural Sector Development Project -- P085752
abstract: Ratings of Agricultural Sector Development Project for Tanzania were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, risk to development outcome was high, Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately unsatisfactory. Some of the  lessons learned included: (i) It is important to design the M&E system prior to project appraisal; (ii) For projects mainstreamed into Government Systems, the Government’s patterns for budget discussions and release of funds should be studied during project preparation, possible impacts assessed, and mitigation measures instituted; (iii) The anticipated aggregate cost of sub-projects solicited from project beneficiaries should not be at great variance from resources available under the Medium Term Expenditure Framework (MTEF); (iv) Beneficiary Communities have to be involved at all stages of sub-project development to maximize ownership; (v) A Sector-Wide Approach (SWAp) requires comprehensive participation by all key stakeholders; (vi) A nation-wide program, whether a SWAp or otherwise, needs a progressive rollout to iron out teething issues before full country-wide expansion; (vii) It is crucial to have a comprehensive understanding of pathways for the envisaged policy actions in order to properly calibrate expectations about policy outcomes; (viii) Effective value chain development requires optimal concentration of activities to facilitate the emergence of viable backward and forward linkages; (ix) Basket-Funding reduces transaction costs, but harmonizing implementation support traditions among participating development partners can be a challenge; (x) In cases where a project contributes to a bigger government program, it is essential to ensure that the project title does not coincide with the name of the government's program; (xi) Appropriate skills mix is key to quality supervision; and (xii) Investments in irrigation at a national scale should be prefaced with a proper understanding of the water balance at the appropriate scale.

title: Malawi - Irrigation, Rural Livelihoods, and Agricultural Development Project
report_name: Malawi - Irrigation, Rural Livelihoods, and Agricultural Development Project
project_id: MW-Irrigation, Rural Livelihoods and Agricultural Development Project -- P084148
abstract: Ratings for the Irrigation, Rural Livelihoods, and Agricultural Development Project for Malawi were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: small-scale irrigation is very beneficial and within the management capacity of smallholders, especially if gravity-fed. The integrated approach, combining hardware and software has higher start-up costs and is prone to delays, but eventually has the highest return on investment. It is advisable that governments have a coherent investment pipeline with actual studies on the shelves for implementation. Irrigation performance has not usually been sustainable in Malawi, hence the need for repeated rehabilitation, hence the approach to build ownership (real and perceived) and capacity has been paramount in the project, through trainings, recorded participatory agreements on irrigation development, land and water rights, and formal irrigation management transfer (IMT). The combination of social protection elements, disaster financing elements with a more production oriented agricultural development project has proven to be a successful model that supports both broad and deep development. Both Government and Bank were proactive in restructuring and through frequent (at minimum fortnightly) coordination meetings to support quick problem solving and processing where required, and this enabled smooth restructuring, scaling up as required.

title: Madagascar - Rural Development Support Project
report_name: Madagascar - Rural Development Support Project
project_id: MG-Rural Development Support Project -- P051922
abstract: Ratings for the Rural Development Support Project for Madagascar were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the promotion of new and economically viable businesses requires strong long-term technical and organizational support, including rigorous screening of potential agro-businesses. The market analysis is the critical point of a large number of small investment projects in rural areas. Under the project, the level of market analysis was very heterogeneous. In those cases where a link to the market was identified early on, with the establishment of commercial contracts that guaranteed a certain degree of security for the additional production generated by the subproject, subprojects remained functional with increased chance of sustainability. Leveraging maximum impact from resources allocated for subprojects requires the identification of investment proposals in a transparent, strategic and participatory manner, promoting competition for support through targeting mechanisms which ensure high quality, sustainable subproject proposals consistent with project development objectives and sector priorities. When Community-Driven Development (CDD) projects are heavily focused on demand-driven approaches tensions can arise between responding to the communities' needs and building a critical mass necessary for sustained growth and rural development. Implementation arrangements for national programs requiring a strong and massive Project Implementation Unit (PIU) are not conducive to strong alliances with local-level governments and institutions and decentralization efforts.

title: Angola, Lesotho - Agricultural Productivity Program for Southern Africa Project
report_name: NA
project_id: 3E-Agricultural Productivity Program for Southern Africa - Angola & Lesotho -- P164486
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Productivity Program for Southern Africa Project for Angola, Lesotho were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory; and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the APPSA experience reaffirmed that a regional approach to agricultural research and technology dissemination offers significant benefits, including shared expertise, economies of scale, and harmonized standards. However, it also highlighted the inherent complexity of multi-country operations, requiring strong institutional readiness, clear governance arrangements, and early government commitment. It is recommended that sufficient preparation time be allocated to align national policies, clarify governance and coordination mechanisms, and secure commitments from finance and agriculture ministries before implementation. Infrastructure and research capacity risk underutilization without operational budgets and post-project support. Sustainability planning and government commitments must be built into project design. It is recommended that future projects require government commitments for long-term operating costs and staffing, base infrastructure on needs assessments, and include detailed sustainability plans during preparation. Governments must commit to performance benchmarks and monitoring systems to track utilization and impact. Private sector participation in research, seed systems, and technology transfer was limited. It is recommended that future projects identify clear entry points for private engagement, such as co-financing seed multiplication, fostering agritech partnerships, and establishing public-private innovation platforms, to strengthen sustainability and support the commercialization of research outputs.

title: South Africa - Land Bank Financial Intermediation Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ZA-Land Bank Financial Intermediation Project -- P150008
abstract: Ratings for the Land Bank Financial Intermediation Project for South Africa were as follows: outcome was highly unsatisfactory, Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: lines of credit that involve new products or strategic changes within the implementing entity will benefit from components that provide resources to support these changes. World Bank projects that support Development Finance Institution (DFIs) that are not adequately supervised should address this as part of the project or prior to project approval. The World Bank should ensure that replacing subsidized lending instruments with ones that move towards full cost recovery is carefully studied to ensure that lending without subsidies is viable. For credit line projects that focus on specific sectors, it is important to include a co- Task Team Leader (TTL) from both the relevant sector and finance, competitiveness, and innovation (FCI) global practice (GP) as well as have the relevant ministry involved in the project implementation arrangement. Providing financing to entities that depend significantly on commercial borrowings exposes the project implementation to significant risks that are extraneous to the project. There are gaps that were identified in the World Bank’s management of local part of the project implementation that should be reviewed.

title: Kenya - National Agricultural and Rural Inclusive Growth Project
report_name: NA
project_id: KE-National Agricultural and Rural Inclusive Growth Project -- P153349
abstract: Ratings of National Agricultural and Rural Inclusive Growth Project for Kenya were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: the lead farmer model of technology transfer and extension encourages community building and strengthening, and peer to peer learning; Delivering TA via multiple channels is crucial; Market linkages and access to finance is key; Excellent implementation is crucial in a devolved context; and Building strong institutions is crucial for sustainability.

title: Uganda - Multisectoral Food Security and Nutrition Project
report_name: NA
project_id: UG-Uganda Multisectoral Food Security and Nutrition Project -- P149286
abstract: Ratings for the Multisectoral Food Security and Nutrition Project for Uganda were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was low or negligible, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was high. Some lessons learned included: multisectoral nutrition action with robust supra-ministerial coordination provides an effective institutional structure for improved nutrition outcomes critical to human capital development. Future nutrition initiatives should leverage and strengthen the national nutrition coordination structures under the leadership of the supra-ministerial coordination structures like the OPM to achieve better coordination and greater impact. The choice of the coordination anchor is extremely vital in the success of the approach as was the case of the OPM, which is also nationally mandated to anchor the country’s nutrition strategy. Due to their complex nature and locational variability, successful implementation of multisectoral nutrition projects requires constant learning from implementation experience and evaluation outcomes. Therefore National-level policy dialogue and widespread dissemination of project learnings are essential for obtaining the desired commitment from the highest levels of government to maintain project gains. The Primary Schools, Primary Health Care facilities including community health workers, strong involvement of local governments coordination structures, and Community-based nutrition education fora are critical vehicles for the delivery of agriculture, nutrition, and public health interventions. Using primary schools as a key vehicle was instrumental in delivering an integrated package of interventions that cut across several sectors to not only enhance nutrition outcomes but also reduce the cost of delivery for even greater impact. Nutrition projects should integrate key service delivery platforms, actors, and capacity building plans to enhance beneficiary participation and utilization. Continuous stakeholder mobilization and training are critical for community-based nutrition action, as they increase participation and sustainability during crises. The results are crucial for promoting integrative programming, which is one of the strategies currently being advocated by the World Bank.

title: Lesotho - Smallholder Agriculture Development Project
report_name: Lesotho - Smallholder Agriculture Development Project
project_id: LS-Lesotho Smallholder Agriculture Development Project -- P119432
abstract: Ratings for the Smallholder Agriculture Development Project for Lesotho were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: changing mindsets about commercial agriculture is challenging but an achievable task with right investments and institutional support. Adequate assessment of enabling conditions is critical to achieve intended results and impacts. service providers (SPs) played a major role to facilitate and support the development of subproject activities. Building resilience, including from climate shocks, is critical for sustainability of project outcomes. Linking farmers to markets does not necessarily require formal agreements, but achieving such formal linkages require greater focus on aggregation. Building a robust and effective M and E system is critical to facilitate effective monitoring, evaluation, and learning. Inadequate attention and measures to environmental and social safeguards compliance measures at the appraisal can pose serious challenges. Projects implemented in a low capacity environment and with demand driven interventions need better appraisal to avoid a slow start up and setting unrealistic targets for early years.

title: Uganda - Agricultural Technology and Agribusiness Advisory Services Project
report_name: Uganda - Agricultural Technology and Agribusiness Advisory Services Project
project_id: UG-Agricultural Technology and Agrribusiness Advisory Services -- P109224
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Technology and Agribusiness Advisory Services Project for Uganda were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: large‐scale, flagship projects must minimize their exposure to policy risk to operate in dynamic, short‐term political environments. Project effectiveness can be accelerated by bringing parliamentarians on board during the preparation stage, in countries where parliamentary approval is required. Government commitment and leadership at both the national and local levels is critical for the success of large projects. The establishment of a single project coordination unit (PCU) can enhance coordination and administrative efficiency of complex projects with a multiplicity of implementation actors. The mainstreaming of sustainable land management (SLM) at the local‐government level is a blueprint for success for future projects focused on landscape management.

title: Mozambique - Competitiveness and Private Sector Development(MCPSD)Project
report_name: Mozambique - Competitiveness and Private Sector Development(MCPSD)Project
project_id: MZ-Competitiveness and Private Sector Development -- P106355
abstract: Ratings for Competitiveness and Private Sector Development Project for Mozambique were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, risk to global environment outcome was moderate, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some of the lessons learned included: (i) the differences in World Bank (WB) and Government (Administrative Court (TA)) requisites and procedures for approval of procurement processes and financial auditing hindered the project performance; (ii) good Monitoring and Evaluation (M and E) is necessary for good project management; (iii) there were important benefits and efficiencies derived from the established partnerships with other donors; (iv) the project effectiveness can suffer if activities are not properly sequenced or if implementation timelines are curtailed by a delayed project start up; (v) the successful implementation of the matching grant provides valuable lessons for future World Bank Group (WBG) programs; (vi) ensuring the fast tracking of contract approvals by the TA given the experienced delays impacting the project implementation; (vii) making provisions for contingencies to avoid exchange rate risk, such as the Special Drawing Rights (SDR)  or United States Dollars (USD) that the project experienced; and (viii) making provisions to ensure key Project Implementation Unit (PIU) positions remain active until the disbursement deadline.

title: Mozambique - Third, Fourth, and Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Mozambique - Third, Fourth, and Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: MZ-Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P103277,MZ-Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC5) -- P107313,MZ-PRSC 3 -- P083459
abstract: Ratings for the Third, Fourth, and Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project for Mozambique were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: importance of Government ownership, taking the time to nurture it, through dialogue an analysis of the political economy of reforms; working together with the rest of the donor community through the Group of nineteen donors (G19) is helpful, notwithstanding the difficulties and tensions resulting there from; the Performance Assessment Framework (PAF), as currently used, is too rigid to permit rapid and flexible response to emerging issues; the political economy of sensitive reforms needs to be well understood; and coordinated capacity building is essential.

title: Southern Sudan - Livestock and Fisheries Development Project
report_name: Southern Sudan - Livestock and Fisheries Development Project
project_id: SS-SD - Livestock and Fisheries Development Project - MDTF -- P101912
abstract: Ratings for the Livestock and Fisheries Development Project (LFDP) for Southern Sudan were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: supervision missions should try to have a balanced skill mix that reflects the makeup of the project's subject matter and other specific requirements, particularly when project preparation was constrained by lack of information. This will give opportunity for improving some proposals that were rushed during preparation. Perhaps the project needs in preparing environmental safeguard instruments will have been accomplished had the supervision missions included an environmental specialist. Likewise, a thorough technical review of the high-tech mobile clinic proposals by an experienced veterinarian will have led towards a choice of more appropriate technology. In an organizational setup with an apparent need for clear line of command, project organization should provide for well-defined staff responsibilities and authority. Considerations should be given in South Sudan to the possibility of relying on stronger project coordination office, albeit within the existing ministerial structure, where the Coordinator becomes primus inter pares, and has a say on matters related to the plan of work and budget of a project as well as in the release of funds. The formulation of results framework in projects that are designed to be implemented in phases should endeavor to include specific objectives for each of the phases instead of establishing global project development objectives (PDOs) for the whole project. This will avoid the risk of underestimating project outcome if one or more of the anticipated phases are not implemented. Failure in setting up key performance indicators for the first phase of LFDP has prevented the project from getting credit for the achievements it made in institutional capacity building and improved animal health services. Projects in post-conflict environments should be designed with simplicity and the greatest analytical rigor allowed by the availability of information. Gaps in information should be shown transparently, and measures built into design to adjust activities as more information becomes available.

title: Ethiopia - Protection of Basic Services Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Protection of Basic Services Project
project_id: ET-Protection of Basic Services -- P074015
abstract: Ratings for the Protection of Basic Services (PBS) Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was satisfactory, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: PBS-type operations should use existing government systems, complemented by systematic and comprehensive support to enhance capacity, especially at the local level. Efforts must also be made to ensure government ownership. Government of Ethiopia (GOE) demonstrated strong ownership for most PBS activities, contributing to the overall successes observed in the development outcomes. During the preparation of PBS, there was a tradeoff between a robust preparation process (including fully addressing implementation readiness conditions) and the need to be adequately responsive to the immediate fiscal, economic and social needs of the situation. While this did result in some delays in implementation of some components, the sense is that it often makes sense to prioritize critical and pressing country needs, ensuring that there is an adequate risk assessment with appropriate mitigating actions, as well as a strong implementation support structure to resolve any implementation bottlenecks that may arise. Simple steps can and should be taken to facilitate financial management.

title: Zambia - Agricultural Development Support Project
report_name: Zambia - Agricultural Development Support Project
project_id: ZM-Agricultural Development Support Program -- P070063
abstract: The Agricultural Development Support Project for Zambia has a satisfactory outcome rating. The rick to development is low or negligible; the Bank's performance is satisfactory; and the borrower's performance is satisfactory. This project supports increased commercialization of smallholder agriculture through improved productivity, quality and efficiency of value chains where smallholders participate. The project development objective was not changed during implementation. With respect to the key performance indicators, the mid-term review noted that the National Coordination Office (NCO) faced issues in regularly updating the indicators due to unclear data collection methodology and analysis between the NCO and the project implementing agencies (IAs). The indicators were therefore revised to better link to the components and logically reflect the impact of ongoing activities specifically supported by the project. The revised key performance indicators were: i) agricultural exports for target value chains (tons/year); ii) targeted commodities produced by smallholder householders (tons/year); iii) smallholder households growing targeted crops (number); iv) average income per household in targeted areas; and v) direct project beneficiaries (number), of which females (percent). Improved value chain productivity, quality and efficiency were assessed using above key performance indicators in terms of increases in volumes and numbers. There are six major lessons learned from the project. First, innovations in project design, which the project had in the Market Improvement and Innovation Facility and Rural Roads Improvement Facility components, should address capacity limitations upfront in order to assure implementation success. In general, projects of this type need to take into account country capacity in project. Second, the OPRC-type contract, where road rehabilitation design includes sufficient resources and practical arrangements for post-rehabilitation maintenance for several years, combined in one contract proved successful. Third, the link between producers and markets is critical in a project that seeks to increase commercialization in agriculture. Fourth, strengthening government core functions can build private sector trust in government. Fifth, good continuous monitoring; periodic and regular evaluation; and quality impact assessments played an important role in learning from the project throughout implementation, and once it was completed. Sixth, flexibility and being opportunistic in implementation was key to the success of this project.

title: Kenya - Agricultural Productivity Project
report_name: Kenya - Agricultural Productivity Project
project_id: KE-Kenya Agricultural Productivity Project -- P082396
abstract: Ratings for the Kenya Agricultural Productivity Project (KAPP) for the Government of Kenya (Gok) were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: although first phase of the project had some elements that focused on promotion of sector-wide approach, it nevertheless became clear during its implementation that there is need to invest more on these sector-wide approaches for greater impact. The GoK, the Bank, and other development partners have, therefore, agreed to promote the sector-wide approach in the agricultural sector, as well as harmonization of programs, in line with the Kenya joint assistance strategy. The formulation of the GoK's agricultural sector development strategy, which is a sector-wide policy, has brought in the necessary impetus and platform on which to promote the sector-wide approaches in the sector in phase two. Equally important is the need to strengthen focus on crosscutting issues such as gender mainstreaming. Although attempts were made under KAPP one to mainstream implementation structures into the GoK system, some project structures and systems were maintained. The rationale for maintaining stand alone project structures and systems was based on low capacity within sector ministries. However, during KAPP I implementation, it became clear that the stand alone project structures were not only expensive and unsustainable, but also tended to distort the incentives and other conditions for public civil servants. As the capacity of sector ministries has improved over time, the implementation of KAPAP will be mainstreamed within the GoK structures, and aligned for sector coordination. The results from the learning pilots under KAPP I have shown that adoption of new technologies can increase if funds are provided directly to empowered farmer groups for the preferred extension approaches. Therefore, increased provision has been made for investments in farmer groups' subprojects under KAPAP.

title: Ethiopia - National Fertilizer Sector Project
report_name: Ethiopia - National Fertilizer Sector Project
project_id: ET-National Fertilizer Sector Project -- P000753,ET-Supplemental to National Fertilizer -- P073307
abstract: The outcome of the National Fertilizer Sector Project for Ethipia was unsatisfactory, the sustainability was unlikely, the institutional development impact was modest, and the Bank and borrower performance were unsatisfactory. Involvement of agricultural extension agents in distribution and credit administration impeded development of the private sector and undermined the longer term function of extension. Financing fertilizer imports through separately mechanisms of foreign exchange allocation introduces inefficiencies in the fertilizer industry. There is a need to move away from project based fertilizers imports in favor of general foreign exchange management. In a country that is highly susceptible to drought like Ethiopia, use of fertilizer can accentuate the hardship caused by insufficient precipitation, putting in peril large numbers of farmers and the financial institutions that serve them. It is important to adopt a holistic approach to input market development and seek coverage in seeds availability and fertilizers as one without the other cannot give optimal returns. The grain market crash of 2001, and the resulting hardship in credit repayments and the precipitous drop in fertilizer consumption in 2002, clearly illustrate the importance of focusing as much on agricultural input markets as on output markets.

title: Uganda - National Agricultural Advisory Services Project
report_name: Uganda - National Agricultural Advisory Services Project
project_id: UG-National Agricultural Advisory Services Project -- P044695
abstract: Ratings for the National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS) Project for Uganda were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Lessons learned include: the NAADS Project was the first in Africa to incorporate public funding and pluralistic provision of services as a national program, with farmers empowered to select their service providers. Prior to introduction of the NAADS Program, questions were raised as to whether such a program shown to work elsewhere, particularly in Latin America, will be suitable and feasible in Africa. The NAADS experience offers many lessons about how best to adapt such a model, but a fundamental conclusion can be drawn that the programs are suitable and superior to the centralized traditional model of public funding and public provision of services. The fact that the Government of Uganda (GOU) saw NAADS as a public program, rather than an enclave project of the donors, gave the program weight and an opportunity to be transformative, through scale and mainstreaming into governmental systems. Few countries have the equivalent of the NAADS Act to safeguard design of programs. This can be a very constructive measure when programs require long duration to reach impact, but governments and priorities may change frequently. Advisory services appear to have had a greater impact than subsidized provision of inputs under the program. This finding is very provisional and warrants more careful investigation, since it is critical to policy dialogue in African agriculture at present. Many governments are of the view that widespread distribution of subsidized fertilizer will achieve rapid gains in productivity and incomes. The NAADS experience suggests that the most impressive gains were achieved in the period when the program concentrated on provision of advice and demonstration of technologies. The returns when inputs became relatively more important in the program appear to be more mixed.

title: Madagascar - Integrated Growth Poles and Corridor SOP-2 Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MG-Madagascar Integrated Growth Poles and Corridor SOP-2 -- P164536
abstract: NA

title: Democratic Republic of Congo - Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises Development and Growth Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ZR-DRC- SME Development and Growth Project -- P160806
abstract: Ratings for the Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises (SME) Development and Growth Project for Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) were as follows: outcome and Bank performance were highly satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was high. Some lessons learned included: the achievements of the Project are a result of the multi-faceted instruments deployed under the Project, which reinforced each other and were tailored to the specific needs of different beneficiary groups. The analytical work carried out on micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) and entrepreneurial ecosystem needs as well as the detailed preparation of SME instruments at the design stage were crucial for the Project’s success. The fragile and conflict-affected states (FCS) nature of context such as the DRC requires flexibility and modularity in the Project’s design and management. The Project employed a multi-faceted effective outreach model to inform and engage with women entrepreneurs that can serve as a model for similar projects in FCS contexts. The decentralization strategy to target select urban areas of interventions was critical to ensure proximity with the main beneficiaries (e.g., the MSMEs, including women and youth entrepreneurs); however, it requires engage with national and provincial stakeholders to establish ownership, build their capacity, and improve the enabling business environment. The reforms to strengthen the enabling business environment remain key to sustaining the development of MSMEs and value chains in strategic sectors of the economy. Procurement can become a major hurdle to overcome for multiple stakeholders. The SME hub pilot was successful, leading to the mobilization of United States (US) 15 million dollars in private capital, and can be scaled up as a key lever for the development of services, infrastructure, and partnerships crucial to the sustainable growth of MSMEs, supporting the overall ecosystem. The employment of private providers to implement activities was important for the Project’s success, particularly given the challenging context, while the systematic use of international-national consortiums facilitated knowledge transfer and local capacity building. In FCS contexts, private consortiums can provide crucial support with data collection, while capacity building to the Project Coordinating Unit (PCU) on M and E is also essential to ensure a well-functioning M and E system. The access to finance dimension is essential for supported firms to continue growing and giving them the freedom to use their banks for the reception of their cash grants, as opposed to employing only a selection of banks, can facilitate access to credit.

title: Malawi - Agricultural Commercialization Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MW-Malawi Agricultural Commercialization Project -- P158434
abstract: Ratings of Agricultural Commercialization Project for Malawi were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: the success of the PA approach hinges on targeting the right subset of smallholder farmers - that is, those who are relatively more productive, already mobilizing to aggregate, and have the financial capacity to meet the self-contribution required for matching grants; Supporting commercially oriented smallholder farmers successfully formalize into PAs requires a comprehensive approach that includes fostering an enabling business and investment environment, to fully unlock their potential; and Targeted interventions are necessary to bridge the gender gap and enhance youth participation in agricultural commercialization.

title: Malawi - Agricultural Support and Fiscal Management Development Policy Financing
report_name: Malawi - Agricultural Support and Fiscal Management Development Policy Financing
project_id: MW-Malawi Agricultural Support and Fiscal Management DPO -- P153753
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Support and Fiscal Management Development Policy Financing for Malawi were as follows: overall outcome and achievement of objectives (efficacy) was moderately satisfactory and relevance of prior actions was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: there should be a reconsideration in Malawi on the way prior actions and results indicators are defined and agreed upon.
