Strengthening linkages with Bank technical assistance (TA) and investment lending operations, when possible, will support implementation and supervision of the development policy operation (DPO). A DPO series needs to adequately account for a lengthy process of legislative change. The analysis in the implementation completion and results (ICR) indicates that in Malawi it takes a long time to implement legislative change.

title: Zambia - Agribusiness and Trade Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ZM-Zambia Agribusiness and Trade Project -- P156492
abstract: Ratings of Agribusiness and Trade Project for Zambia were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some of the lessons learned include:  Matching grants can be effective if sufficient resources are invested in the selection and coaching of beneficiaries; Mentoring and role of customized support is vital; Working with intermediaries can be very successful in reaching small farmers; working with distributors was important for SMEs; Importance of working capital; and Such projects should focus more on job creation and income growth as the key development outcome.

title: Democratic Republic of Congo - Regional Great Lakes Integrated Agriculture Development Project
report_name: NA
project_id: 3E-AFR RI-Regional Great Lakes Integrated Agriculture Development Project -- P143307
abstract: Rating of Regional Great Lakes Integrated Agriculture Development Project for Democratic Republic of Congo were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank Performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: Fragile environments often lack adequate information to allow locking in the full design and implementation modalities at appraisal; In a low-capacity environment, strategic partnerships with organizations of vast experience in the project domains can greatly facilitate implementation; Regional Collaboration in areas such as research can be effective in amplifying the impact of research and development; and Addressing policy-level Barriers is essential for long-term impact.

title: Madagascar - Agriculture Rural Growth and Land Management Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MG-Madagascar Agriculture Rural Growth and Land Management Project -- P151469
abstract: Ratings for the Agriculture Rural Growth and Land Management Project for Madagascar were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the Bank's performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: actively promoting private sector participation, particularly among essential EOs in VCs, is crucial for value chain development and integrating small producers. To ensure a consistent supply of quality products, cooperatives need increased working capital and investment in equipment while maintaining marketing autonomy. Despite improvements in property rights and guarantee funds to enhance access to commercial bank loans, banks remain hesitant to finance family farms due to perceived risks. In response, MINAE is creating a more favorable financial environment for the sector, including improved FDA regulation, an e-voucher system for efficient subsidy transfers, and insurance services for family farms with IFC support. Strong M&E systems are critical to collecting reliable information and data, especially for projects covering decentralized and remote areas. Ministry teams in charge of agriculture and land at the decentralized level need to be adequately trained and equipped. The use of ICT/remote sensing can significantly improve the efficiency of M&E systems. The need for a sustainable land information management system should be raised and clearly addressed at the concept stage rather than during or at the end of the project’s implementation. The issue of a land information system must be addressed in parallel with the issue of the land institution in charge of its development. In the future, any investment in the land sector must first ensure that a single operational entity, equipped with qualified human resources and appropriate technology, can manage a dynamic land database sustainably.

title: Ethiopia - Competitiveness and Job Creation Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ET-Ethiopia Competitiveness and Job Creation Project -- P143302
abstract: Ratings for the Competitiveness and Job Creation Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: political economy and ownership are the most critical factors for smooth and timely implementation of a high-profile project that implements new policy level initiatives. Having complementary technical assistance, implemented directly by the World Bank, was essential to mobilize global expertise and knowledge and build institutional capacity and systems at the early stages of the program. Impact evaluation should be more closely aligned with project needs. Pre-project resettlement can create problems over which the World Bank has little control. The results framework for such projects needs to be broader and less ambitious. Linkage programs should be modest in ambition and informed by demand analysis. There needs to be effective coordination and active collaboration among development partners. The World Bank needs to reflect on how to resist major changes in project design which risk undermining a project’s economic viability and the achievement of its objectives.

title: Democratic Republic of Congo - Eastern Recovery Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ZR-DRC Eastern Recovery Project -- P145196
abstract: Ratings of the DRC Eastern Recovery Project for Democratic Republic of Congo were as follows: Outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, M&E quality was substantial. Lessons learned include that adaptive management is essential in fragility, conflict, and violence contexts, enabling timely pivots through additional financing, restructurings, and contingency components to maintain relevance and impact amid crises; that strategic institutional anchoring within core government structures is critical to balance rapid execution with policy dialogue and system building, while transitions between implementing agencies require robust planning, documentation, and capacity support to minimize disruption; and that decentralized project implementation capacity at provincial level shortens decision loops, strengthens daily accompaniment of community structures, and sustains momentum in challenging geographies. It is also clear that an early pivot from community-driven development toward adaptive safety nets is necessary when shocks intensify, while retaining local governance functions to preserve cohesion; that Community Liaison and Development Committees (CLDs) are central to inclusion and social cohesion in displacement settings when they explicitly integrate refugees and hosts and use equitable representation and joint decision-making; and that remote, geo-enabled monitoring through tools aligned to the geo-enabling initiative for monitoring and supervision and a strong Management Information System enhances oversight, data quality, and course correction when field access is curtailed. Recommendations are to build integrated, inclusive, and system-aligned social safety nets for displaced and host populations with harmonized targeting, digital payments, accessible grievance redress mechanisms, and interoperable registries; to predefine triggers for rapid scale-up of cash responses while maintaining community platforms; to fund subnational capacity for implementation and monitoring; to codify coordination protocols with humanitarian and government actors; and to embed adaptive mechanisms that allow targeting and payment adjustments during shocks.

title: Lesotho - Second Private Sector Competitiveness and Economic Diversification Project
report_name: NA
project_id: LS-Second Private Sector Competitiveness and Economic Diversification Prj -- P144933
abstract: Ratings for the Second Private Sector Competitiveness and Economic Diversification Project (PSCEDP II) for Lesotho were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: the high quality and committed leadership of the project management unit (PMU) was vital for successful project implementation and achievement of impact. Institutional support and strengthening of the business environment in low-capacity environments can be a difficult process, but it provides the foundations for more rapidly moving reforms later. Adoption of the underlying legal framework is a time consuming but critical factor in establishing new (or reforming existing) institutions. Enhancement of technological capacity and systems upgrades should be undertaken in the view of existing government capacity and resources available, to ensure their sustainability in the long run. Economic diversification policy in Lesotho benefits from not be exclusively centered on products that have preferential treatment in free trade agreements. The possibility of external shocks should be better captured at project design, especially considering the growing impact of climate change. Effective collaboration - among the participating World Bank teams and between the World Bank and the Government of Lesotho - is required due to the cross-cutting nature of private sector development and contributed to the success of the PSCEDP II.

title: Uganda - Competitiveness and Enterprise Development Project
report_name: NA
project_id: UG-Competitiveness and Enterprise Development Project (CEDP) -- P130471
abstract:
Ratings for the Competitiveness and Enterprise Development Project for Uganda were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: one of the key
challenges in the SLAAC program was the gap between demarcated parcels and titles issued, caused in part by delayed feasibility studies, underestimated follow-up logistics, and limited district-level capacity. A stronger project design could have anticipated these bottlenecks by frontloading feasibility assessments, clarifying regulatory procedures for mass titling, and securing dedicated operational funding for issuance. Broader legal mandates and institutional incentives, such as simplified validation protocols and reduced ancillary costs, could have improved claimant follow-through. These design level improvements are critical for future land formalization efforts to translate physical demarcation into secure tenure. : Implementing a multicomponent project like CEDP highlighted the need for robust coordination mechanisms and strategic integration of components whose interrelationships have high pay offs. For example, improving the security of land tenure will contribute to the growth of a more economically and socially sustainable tourism industry and for that reason, clarifying property rights under the SLAAC component and setting up governance rules that promote transparency and equity in
access to opportunities could be important components to address to enable acquisition of land for tourism related investments. Challenges arose from overlapping mandates, varied readiness levels among implementing agencies, and the absence of unified systems for data sharing and monitoring. Future complex operations should ensure upfront agreements on institutional roles, deploy shared digital platforms or interfaces, and build joint accountability frameworks to enhance coherence, efficiency, interoperability, and learning across components.

title: Rwanda - Governance for Competitiveness Technical Assistance Project
report_name: Rwanda - Governance for Competitiveness Technical Assistance Project
project_id: RW-Rwanda - Governance & Competitiveness TA Project -- P127105
abstract: Ratings for the Governance for Competitiveness Technical Assistance Project for Rwanda were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to global environment outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included:(1) The results chain should be credible; (2) The assessment of capacity and timeframes during design and implementation should be realistic; (3) Relatedly, appraisal of uncertainty should also be realistic; (4) Sequencing and complementarity should be carefully assessed and leveraged; and (5) Projects could actively foster sustainable government collaboration.

title: Zambia - Second Economic Management and Growth Credit Project
report_name: Zambia - Second Economic Management and Growth Credit Project
project_id: ZM-Second Economic Management and Growth -- P074445
abstract: Ratings for the Second Economic Management and Growth Credit Project for Zambia are as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the design and sequencing of reforms need to take into account the track record of the government especially in those areas that have been chosen by the operation. This will ensure that expected outcomes can be more realistic and focused on reforms in areas where chances of success are higher. In order to improve accountability that will lead to better implementation of reforms, there is a need for strengthening the link between the poverty reduction budget support (PRBS) processes and other national processes through the sectoral advisory groups (SAGs). This will enable increased involvement of other stakeholders such as civil society and parliament. It must be recognized that operating in a donor harmonized framework can lead to trade-offs, especially since policies governing budget support can differ between some donors and the Bank. The limits of using a single instrument to achieve multiple objectives (e.g. harmonization, predictability, contribution and encouragement to reforms) need to be acknowledged. Consistent dialogue among donors and between donors and government can lead to improvements in the content of the performance assessment framework (PAF).

title: Uganda - Second Private Sector Competitiveness Project
report_name: Uganda - Second Private Sector Competitiveness Project
project_id: UG-Private Sector Competitiveness II -- P083809
abstract: Ratings for the Second Private Sector Competitiveness Project for Uganda were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was unsatisfactory, and Borrower performance was unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the desire to realize efficiencies and comprehensiveness with a larger project should not over-ride the risks inherent in a complex project design. The importance of getting the monitoring and evaluation framework right, from the beginning, cannot be overstated. When fiduciary problems do emerge, Bank management and Integrity Vice Presidency (INT) need to be consulted promptly to ensure timely and decisive actions. The Bank's own team should sufficiently involve the right technical expertise to identify potential problems (for example, civil engineer to conduct on-site supervision). Monitoring documents should not only be candid, but ratings need to be consistent with disbursement lags. Strong Borrower oversight (for example, project steering committee) of the project must be taken seriously from the beginning, rather than only activated once problems emerge. Having the Private Sector Foundation Uganda (PSFU) manage the delivery of the public sector agencies may achieve some objectives, but it can risk reducing ownership by the beneficiary agencies. A Monitoring and Evaluation (M and E) specialist should be part of the Borrower's project preparation team and continue to be actively engaged throughout project implementation. For high profile projects like industrial parks, an economically rational and financially viable land pricing policy needs to be applied consistently and transparently. Attracting investment to such endeavors may backfire without infrastructure and serviceable land in place.

title: Tanzania - First Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Tanzania - First Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: TZ-Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P074073,TZ-Tanzania Poverty Reduction Support Credit 3 -- P087256,TZ-Tanzania First Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P074072
abstract: Ratings for the First Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project (PRSC) for Tanzania were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: Government ownership of the reform agenda is the single most important factor determining the success of reforms. The performance assessment framework has proven useful for the monitoring of reforms, but linking disbursement decisions to specific actions has been a two edged sword. While in some cases this has helped to accelerate the pace of reaching a specific milestone, in other instances, the processes of dialogue and conditionality have become closely interwoven, crowding out the space for a dispassionate, objective, and non-committal sharing of views. The focus on a large number of actions contained in the performance assessment framework (PAF) may also have reduced the strategic focus of the budget support. There appears to be a tension between broad-based progress, which is necessary to ensure improvements of government institutions and systems, and more focused policy dialogue to align PRSC with the critical bottlenecks to growth and poverty reduction. More focus on public expenditure efficiency and effectiveness, and implementation of policy through the budget may be required. This may require a significant revamping of the public expenditure review (PER) process, to bring it back to supporting effective and timely dialogue on key budget priorities between the government and the development partners.

title: Tanzania - Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit and Grant
report_name: Tanzania - Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit and Grant
project_id: TZ-Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P074073
abstract: The outcome for the Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit and Grant for Tanzania was satisfactory, the sustainability was likely, the institutional development impact was substantial, and the Bank and borrower performance were both satisfactory. Findings and implications: A stable political environment contributed to the continuing successful implementation of macroeconomic and structural reforms in Tanzania. Development and implementation of a common performance assessment framework and a joint review process, which harmonizes general budget support of 12 donors, is a major achievement. Weaknesses in government process such as the poverty monitoring system or the budget system impact directly on the ability of the Poverty Reduction Support Credit program to present adequate results orientation and monitoring frameworks.

title: Ethiopia - Emergency Drought Recovery Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Emergency Drought Recovery Project
project_id: ET-EMERGENCY DROUGHT RECOVERY PROJECT -- P081773
abstract: Ratings for the Ethiopia Emergency Drought Recovery Project were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: 1) A clear results structure is critical to facilitate effective project implementation, monitoring and evaluation; 2) the community-driven implementation approach has helped in significantly building up local community capacities for project identification, planning, and implementation; 3) there is a need to proactively consider larger than normal limits to authorized allocations in order to facilitate flow of funds in quick-disbursing emergency projects; and 4) it is critical to establish a robust monitoring and evaluation system, including arrangements for data collection and use, during the project design process.

title: Ethiopia - Food Security Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Food Security Project
project_id: ET-Food Security Project -- P050383
abstract: Ratings for the Food Security Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was substantial; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: targeting the poor, vulnerable groups and women can be done effectively in a highly decentralized setting if communities are appropriately mobilized to confirm the status of households; results tend to be better where there is political commitment and where communities have been effectively engaged in decisions regarding program delivery; community determination of interest rates and payback period, while encouraging ownership of revolving fund management, if not well directed can also lead to inappropriate loan products; and while it is possible to provide financial services through many channels, effective and sustainable management of such services requires that they be provided through financial institutions.

title: Rwanda - First Rural Sector Support Project
report_name: Rwanda - First Rural Sector Support Project
project_id: RW-Integrated Management of Critical Ecosystems Project -- P070700,RW-RURAL SECTOR SUPPORT PROJECT -- P064965
abstract: Ratings for the First Rural Sector Support Project (RSSP1) for Government of Rwanda (GoR) were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was low or negligible, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the importance of maintaining a tight focus on an agenda that is manageable given the capacity constraints under which the project operates. As initially designed and approved by the Board, RSSP1 included seven components spanning a wide range of activities. Early implementation experience revealed that the scope of the design was overly ambitious, and that the Government lacked the technical and institutional capacity needed to implement such a broad array of activities. The December 2005 restructuring successfully focused the Project on a core set of activities. The need to build capacity in the Project Support and Coordination Unit (PSCU) to ensure effective implementation of the four safeguards policies triggered by RSSP2, and the need for the Bank to assist the Borrower effectively on safeguards. RSSP was in early compliance with the environmental and social safeguards triggered at appraisal and was found to have strong environmental capacity in the PSCU, but general safeguards capacity was found to be low. In hindsight, the Bank team was slow to act, and should have informed GoR of the policy earlier. The policy has now been fully integrated in the current phase. The importance of a consistent, coherent and straightforward M&E system clearly articulated in the Project Appraisal Document (PAD), with indicators clearly connected to the Project Development Objectives (PDO). Over time, the streamlining of the results framework allowed the borrower to develop a workable process for collecting data and reporting on output and outcome indicators, which saved the situation.

title: Tanzania - Land Tenure Improvement Project
report_name: NA
project_id: TZ-Land Tenure Improvement Project -- P164906
abstract: Ratings of the Land Tenure Improvement Project for United Republic of Tanzania were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory; Bank performance was satisfactory; monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Lessons and recommendations are as follows. First, addressing rural–urban differences requires distinct technical and institutional approaches that reflect differing legal frameworks, readiness, and settlement conditions; future operations should sequence work to capitalize on existing rural models while allowing sufficient time to pilot and refine urban regularization systems, with realistic timelines, tailored strategies, and capacity building at local levels. Second, ambitious gender targets can catalyze institutional change even when not fully met; future projects should retain or raise aspirational goals, embed joint titling and gender-sensitive communication, and sustain targeted outreach to women to deepen inclusion and maintain high satisfaction. Third, sustaining systemic gains beyond the project lifetime depends on resilient institutions, standardized procedures, and durable digital platforms; continued government commitment and financing, routine training, and clear operational guidance are recommended to maintain and scale the national digital land administration platform (E-Ardhi) and associated certification processes. Fourth, complex land administration reforms benefit from a Multiphase Programmatic Approach, enabling early phases to focus on institutional strengthening, system rollout, and capacity building, followed by subsequent phases that scale certification and services; the design of each phase should integrate supervision lessons, including robust procurement planning, strengthened environmental and social capacity, and streamlined inter-agency coordination to ensure continuity and effective nationwide expansion.

title: Ethiopia - Agricultural Growth Program Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Agricultural Growth Program Project
project_id: ET-ET: Agricultural Growth Program -- P113032
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Growth Program Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality performance was modest. Some lessons learned included: Private sector actors successfully implemented Small-Scale Irrigation (SSI) schemes, exceeding targets.Exchange visits can serve as an effective mechanism to convince policy-makers to consider new initiatives that have worked in other places with a similar context.The success of capacity-building activities in the project can be enhanced using a more consistent, uniform approach.The project demonstrated how agricultural growth originated at the ground level through the (Common Interest Group) CIGs.Creating and strengthening CIGs is an effective way to mainstream gender into projects.Group formation and collective action are necessary, but not sufficient conditions, to achieving agricultural commercialization in Ethiopia.

title: Congo, Democratic Republic of - Agriculture Rehabilitation and Recovery Support Project
report_name: Congo, Democratic Republic of - Agriculture Rehabilitation and Recovery Support Project
project_id: ZR-DRC - Agriculture Rehabilitation and Recovery Support -- P092724
abstract: Rating of Agriculture Rehabilitation and Recovery Support Project for Congo, Democratic Republic of were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some of the lessons learned include: (i) Careful attention to Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) from the project start is essential; (ii) Need for prudence in the setting of Project Development Objective (PDO) and intermediate results targets in large post-conflict countries; (iii) Yield measurements should be complemented by measurement of cropping patterns and area of land cultivated; (iv) Major executing agencies need special supervision arrangements; (v) Use of local World Bank staff on the ground should be increased; (vi) The involvement of public entities in charge of certain public goods is essential as it can help build capacity while serving as a vehicle for sustainability; (vii) Breakthroughs in agricultural technology should be accompanied by supportive policy measures; and (viii) Need for timeliness in additional financing.

title: Malawi - Community-Based Rural Land Development Project
report_name: Malawi - Community-Based Rural Land Development Project
project_id: MW-Community-Based Rural Land Development Project -- P075247,MW-Community Based Rural Land Development Project -- P115226
abstract: Ratings for the Community-Based Rural Land Development Project for Malawi were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate; Bank performance was satisfactory and borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: the voluntary community-based approach to land reform prevents social tensions and political interferences but requires time and sound capacity building efforts. Beneficiaries prefer to relocate within or close to their original homes. There is a need for a supportive policy environment and efficient land administration services. Land reform programs should be embedded within broader rural development support.

title: Ethiopia - Third Productive Safety Nets Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Third Productive Safety Nets Project
project_id: ET-Productive Safety Net APL III -- P113220
abstract: Ratings for the Third Productive Safety Nets Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: recent market studies indicate that, with few exceptions, there is limited justification for continued food transfers in highland areas on the basis of unavailability of food in markets. Market assessments also indicate the potential to trial the use of a combination of food and cash transfers in lowland areas. In areas where specific efforts were made to link public works to livelihood investments, greater impacts were seen. As watersheds have become more productive, more substantial livelihoods-based public works such as land reclamation and small-scale irrigation schemes have been successfully implemented. Further, linking public works and livelihood activities to climate smart initiatives and activities provides an effective instrument that adapts to and manages disaster and climate risks. The PSNP risk financing mechanism was effective in responding to transitory food insecurity and helped protect the investments of the chronically food insecure, but needs to be even better prepared for larger shocks. The ambition to transform the role of the extension service required: a fundamental shift to a demand drivenmodel, the simultaneous engagement of financial service providers to extend credit to PSNP clients, the introduction of a large-scale marketing and information campaign and the reform of the roles and responsibilities of staff who had been working primarily as agricultural extension agents. These changes were ambitious and, in many cases, beyond the reach of the program to effectively address during the time period envisioned for reform.

title: Mozambique - Land Administration Project (Terra Segura)
report_name: NA
project_id: MZ-Mozambique Land Administration Project (Terra Segura) -- P164551
abstract: Ratings of Land Administration Project for Mozambique (Terra Segura)  were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include:hiring of firms to carry out an independent quality assurance of the work and support the implementation of environment and social safeguard aspects; the formulation and adoption of the 2023 National Land Policy and the drafting of the new Land Law provide valuable lessons for other governments; and experience from the World Bank’s projects involving significant investments in land reform, land titling, institutional changes, and development of information systems indicates that they take longer than the ‘typical five years’ allocated.

title: Comoros - Statistics Project
report_name: Comoros - Statistics Project
project_id: KM-Comoros Statistics Project -- P159437
abstract: Ratings for the Statistics Project for Comoros were as follows: overall outcomes were satisfactory, bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the project was linked to the government Strategy for Accelerating Growth and Sustainable Development (SCA2D) and the 2015-19 NSDS which raised the importance of strengthening statistical national capacity and underscored the need for continued capacity building support. The management of INSEED and the government were also consulted during project design to identify their needs. This has contributed to smooth implementation of the project activities. The project combined methodological improvements through technical assistance and training, equipment, and information communication technology (ICT) upgrading. It helped to initiate the use of technologically advanced methods for data collection with the transition from the use of PAPI to CAPI and the digitization of the enumeration areas during primary sample units’ maps. The following intermediate indicator was not met, organizing focus groups to provide feedback on the quality, relevance, and timeliness of statistics- A more concrete indicator measuring improvement in the production and dissemination of statistics would be  easier to implement and measure.

title: Ethiopia - Enhancing Shared Prosperity through Equitable Services Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ET-Enhancing Shared Prosperity through Equitable Services -- P151432
abstract: Ratings for the Enhancing Shared Prosperity through Equitable Services Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: in a decentralized context like Ethiopia’s, development of a platform for harmonized engagement on local service delivery is essential. The choice of scope and balance of sectors with a multi-sectoral service delivery operation should reflect national priorities and also be consistent with the relative importance of the sectors at the decentralized level. Improving service delivery outcomes at the local level relies on a viable funding framework. Equity considerations require targeting lagging or low-performing districts. Improving service delivery indicators is a long-term commitment and may not be linear. Improved service delivery relies not only on financing but also on the capacity of institutional mechanisms to translate that funding into service outcomes. Local government commitment to improving fiduciary management systems is key to sustaining transparency and accountability. Ethiopia Social Accountability Program (ESAP) is unique in its attention to the demand side of local governance and service provision, providing a number of lessons learned for social accountability efforts beyond the enhancing shared prosperity through equitable services (ESPES) context. Focusing on government environmental and social assessment systems rather than donor or project-specific requirements leverages significant impacts, well beyond the more limited focus of traditional investment projects. Information on results provides a critical feedback loop, and technology can be leveraged to enhance information sharing. In an environment where development financing is limited, with right government ownership a PforR supports sustained development. The use of a program-for-results (PforR) instrument proved critical to achieving results in such a complex cross-sectoral program, enabling countrywide systemic changes. A multi-sectoral PforR is by its nature a highly complex instrument and should be given effort and time for success. A PforR assumes a certain level of underlying stability in projecting results over time. Inclusion of an investment project financing (IPF) component mid-implementation supported the completion of disbursement linked indicator and disbursement linked result (DLI and DLRs), but added complexity to the PforR, particularly when DLI and DLR achievements are dependent on IPF activities.

title: Tanzania - Kihansi Catchment Conservation and Management Project
report_name: Tanzania - Kihansi Catchment Conservation and Management Project
project_id: TZ-Kihansi Catchment Conservation and Management Project -- P126361
abstract: The overall outcome of the Kihansi Catchment Conservation and Management Project for Tanzania is moderately satisfactory, with satisfactory Bank performance and substantial monitoring and evaluation quality. There was one restructuring of the project which was approved in June 2017. This restructuring resulted in a change of wording of the Project Development Objective (PDO) indicators for clarification. A supplemental PDO indicator was also added to reflect the conservation outcome and another one revised and moved to the intermediate level. The project had important lessons of and recommendations for the reintroduction process, implementation arrangements, and community engagement and participation. (a) Reintroduction is a long-term process that will require dedicated resources, careful monitoring and continued research; (b) Community engagement and participation fundamental for long-term sustainability; (c) A multi-sector project needs strong involvement from relevant sectors and (d) Commitment is needed at a high level to ensure ownership and accountability over the long-term.

title: South Sudan - Local Governance and Service Delivery Project
report_name: South Sudan - Local Governance and Service Delivery Project
project_id: SS-Local Governance and Service Delivery Project -- P127079
abstract: The overall outcome of the Local Governance and Service Delivery Project for South Sudan was moderately unsatisfactory with moderately satisfactory Bank performance and modest monitoring and evaluation quality. Lessons learned from the Project are: 1. In FCV (Fragile, Conflict, Violence) settings, the World Bank needs more flexible instruments. 2. Building in a robust risk management mechanism is crucial for operations in FCV settings. 3. It is possible to achieve results in FCV settings, especially through projects with a Community Driven Development (CDD) component. 4. Early post-conflict transitions present choices about how to appropriately sequence and manage trade-offs between supporting CDD and state-building. 5. Monitoring and evaluation (M and E) and supervision are challenging in FCV settings, and so need to be strengthened, including through providing task teams with detailed guidance on M and E design, emphasizing early third-party arrangements and iterative conflict and social analysis.

title: Ethiopia - Third Phase of Promoting Basic Services Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Third Phase of Promoting Basic Services Project
project_id: ET-Ethiopia Promoting Basic Services Program Phase III Project -- P128891
abstract: Ratings of Third Phase of Promoting Basic Services Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) Devolution of power and responsibilities to regions and woredas must be matched with resources; (ii) It is important to identify how to engage sectoral agencies within such a decentralized structure; (iii) Over time a decentralized system will need more diversified instruments than a single base general Block Grant to address issues of equitable access and quality; (iv) Improvements in basic services rely on important cross-cutting systems that may be overlooked in more narrow sectoral operations; (v) Specific lessons learned in respect to the screening tool designed for Ethiopia include the requirement for the procedure to be simple and, embedded within existing Environmental and Social Management Frameworks already in use; (vi) Improvements in basic services rely on important cross-cutting systems that may be overlooked in more narrow sectoral operations; and (vii) Despite the higher risks, there were significant advantages in PBS's reliance on national systems.

title: Uganda - Eighth and Ninth Poverty Reduction Credits Project : Uganda - Eighth and Ninth Poverty Reduction Credits Project
report_name: Uganda - Eighth and Ninth Poverty Reduction Credits Project
project_id: UG-Uganda Eighth Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P101232,UG-Uganda Ninth Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P097325
abstract: Ratings for the Eighth and Ninth Poverty Reduction Credits Project  for Uganda were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: spending staff time on ensuring an appropriate JAF up front greatly facilitates program management and assessment of achievement of program objectives. Intensive coordination efforts with development partners paid off in terms of a results framework that could be used for program tracking and management. The flowchart in Annex 8 provides an example of an effective tool to help coordinate the dialog with counterparts and development partners in building a results framework. Achieving broad cooperation amongst development partners complicates the country dialog by introducing many considerations that are particular to development partner's country. Innovations such as e-procurement and bulk purchases of items across ministries through framework agreements may meet with less resistance. Ministries are willing to give up a small portion of their procurement, more than they are willing to concede changes to the procurement system as a whole. And these quick and focused changes that can delivery quick results may make broader reforms easier in the future.

title: Rwanda - Eighth Poverty Reduction Support Financing Project
report_name: Rwanda - Eighth Poverty Reduction Support Financing Project
project_id: RW-Eighth Poverty Reduction Support Financing -- P122247
abstract: The Eighth Poverty Reduction Support Financing for Rwanda had an overall outcome of satisfactory and the risk to development was moderate. The Bank's and borrower's performance was rated as satisfactory. The program development objective of a programmatic series, the PRSF 8 and deferred and subsequently shelved PRSFs 9 and 10, was to adopt policies that facilitate increased private sector investment and involvement and ease constraints to broad based growth. Even though the PRSFs 9 and 10 did not materialize, the Government of Rwanda and the Bank continued monitoring implementation progress for the triggers and respective development outcomes through Joint Sector Reviews of relevant sectors. The preparation of the PRSF series was built upon comprehensive analytical works and lessons learned from the previous PRSF series (i.e., advancing Government-owned reforms, providing substantial financing and fostering better donor coordination). The key lesson learned from the PRSF series was that the Government’s commitment and ownership were essential to advance the reforms. This was especially evident after the deferment and subsequent shelving of follow-up operations as the Government achieved 9 out of 11 observable outcome indicators in target years. One of the remaining two met in the following years and progress towards achieving the other one is steadily improving. The expectation of impact on private economic activity is likely to materialize over the medium term, given the continuation of policies and efforts.

title: Comoros - Emergency Crises Response Project
report_name: Comoros - Emergency Crises Response Project
project_id: KM-Comoros - Emergency Crises Response Project -- P120631
abstract: Ratings for the Emergency Crises Response Project for Comoros were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: in a situation where the government does not assume the responsibility of ensuring the provision of basic social infrastructure in remote and poor communities, the project demonstrated that community-managed development is feasible even in today’s context and has highly desirable characteristics such as creating assets that are of high priority to the community, engendering community ownership and full use of the assets. The low capacity environment of the country meant that it will have been beneficial for the implementing agency as much as its partner institutions to receive more technical support for the works, specifically regarding engineering and planning. While the project contributed somewhat to improving the capacity of local partner institutions, more could have been done to directly enhance the growing capacity of the various bureaus. The unique character of the implementing agency with its capacity for semiautonomous operation despite strong government support and a multisectoral board, including representatives of the beneficiary communities, is worthy of emulation, especially in difficult economic and political circumstances. An implementing agency experienced in handling Emergency Projects is a good resource in a disaster prone country to deal with all potential/future shocks. The development of such an agency is also one of the recommendations of the Bank’s social protection and labor strategy.

title: Mozambique - Sixth, Seventh, and Eight Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project : Mozambique - Sixth, Seventh, and Eight Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Mozambique - Sixth, Seventh, and Eight Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: MZ-Seventh Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC7) -- P122470,MZ-Eighth Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC8) -- P126226,MZ-Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC6) -- P117234
abstract: Ratings for the Sixth, Seventh, and Eight Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project for Mozambique were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate; Bank performance was satisfactory and borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: Performance Assessment Frameworks (PAFs) must be driven by clearly specified policy and institutional reforms ensuring that outcome indicators are linked to concrete actions rather than vague aspirational goals. Political-economy constraints should be evaluated in terms of the risk they pose to the continuity of the reform process. Implementation-capacity constraints must be identified in order to guide the provision of technical assistance. Meeting these challenges will require an adequately designed and targeted program of technical assistance, in the short run, coupled with sustained capacity-building support over the long run.

title: Uganda - Second Northern Uganda Social Action Fund Project
report_name: Uganda - Second Northern Uganda Social Action Fund Project
project_id: UG-Second  Northern Uganda Social Action Fund Project (NUSAF2) -- P111633
abstract: Ratings for the Second Northern Uganda Social Action Fund Project  for Uganda were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: Project design ensured that government machinery was engaged in the project at all levels, from national to local government levels. Community-driven Development (CDD) needed to be integrated well into local government structures to avoid duplication with their regular infrastructure work and to ensure ownership and maintenance budget once the projects are complete. In the  Second Northern Uganda Social Action Fund Project (NUSAF2), the GoU mainstreamed CDD operations into the decentralized service delivery mechanisms. Low level of benefit for the PWP affected delivery of the initial intention of the project to reduce vulnerability and provide alternative employment to vulnerable people with labor. strengthening institutional capacity is to assist communities to realize their development needs, efforts such as training and technical backstopping of communities need to be sustained.  Anti corruption measures in the project can become models for broader social accountability. Project implementation at the local level use of participatory approaches to identify beneficiaries and community priorities empowered the local people to articulate and share their own opinions, needs, problems, and abilities. Capacity enhancement and consolidation of capacities over time has shown positive outcomes. The participation of community facilitators proved vital in facilitating sub project generation. The mainstreaming of the project within local government, through engagement with district officers and the identification of NUSAF2 desk officers, strengthened the delivery of the project at the local level and provided space for communities and local authorities to work jointly in the delivery of the project at the community level. Delivery of social protection programs in contexts of post conflict and fragility.

title: Ethiopia - Second Phase of the Protection of Basic Services Program Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Second Phase of the Protection of Basic Services Program Project
project_id: ET-Ethiopia Protection of Basic Services Program Phase II Project -- P103022
abstract: Ratings for the Second Phase of the Protection of Basic Services Program Project (PBS II) for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: designing project outcome goals that are supporting a national program is challenging, but beneficial. The financing of recurrent expenditures has raised an interesting question, as posed by the inspection panel, of how far the Bank can be held responsible for indirect or outside-of-program actions by government employees that are partially financed by World Bank and donor funding. Areas like social accountability and financial transparency need time for 'changing mindsets' and this factor is difficult to predict when setting targets and evaluating a program. The PBS has emerged as a long term aid instrument to enhance service delivery, social accountability and transparency in Ethiopia, providing a productive forum to discuss challenges and constraints to meeting those objectives. Very significant progress has been made on Financial Transparency and Accountability, or FTA and the Government is now fully committed and moving forward on South Asia (SA). While the change in mindset has taken longer than expected, it is now deeply accepted at the central government level and is making headway at local levels.

title: Malawi - Third Social Action Fund : two additional financings
report_name: Malawi - Third Social Action Fund : two additional financings
project_id: MW-Malawi Third Social Action Fund (MASAF 3) APL II (LDF Mechanism) -- P110446
abstract: The Malawi Third Social Action Fund received a satisfactory outcome with moderate risk to development outcome. Both the Bank and the Borrower received satisfactory performance ratings. In addition to the restructurings done during the approval process of the additional financings, the Project had only one restructuring. It was done just before project closing to extend the closing date and was needed in order to apply some final reallocation and adjustment of amounts among components and categories. An Additional Financing (AF) project reworded the PDO to make it more concise and measurable without changing its substance. The revised PDO was: to improve the livelihoods of poor and vulnerable households and to strengthen the capacity of local authorities to manage local development. The changed PDO did not substantively change the objectives of the project, but clarified some aspects, including areas for which the project was to be held accountable. There were two AF projects. The first AF aimed to (i) develop, implement, and monitor a scaled-up public works-plus-savings and investment program to help alleviate the poverty, social, and economic impact of the crisis and (ii) provide financial assistance to protect core spending on education infrastructure investments through the reconstruction/retrofitting of education structures damaged in the Karonga and Chitipa earthquakes. The second AF was implemented as a stabilization package for the project which included additional support for the project, general budget support, and AF for an irrigation, rural livelihoods, and agricultural development project. There were several lessons learned from this project including the following: (1) safety net program parameters face trade-offs between cost, coverage, generosity, and durability of the infrastructure created and there is no right formula, (2) the focus on unskilled labor, largely road maintenance and afforestation, at less than market wages tended to increase womens participation, (3) finally, public works will definitely remain an important part of the social assistance tool kit in the country. The public works program (PWP) is scalable in times of crisis and can be directed to the right geographic locations and target groups.

title: Ethiopia - Tana  Beles Integrated Water Resources Development Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Tana  Beles Integrated Water Resources Development Project
project_id: ET-Tana & Beles Integrated Water Resources Development -- P096323
abstract: Ratings for the Tana and Beles Integrated Water Resources Development Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: The scale and scope of projects need to be matched with the existing implementation capacity.Project time frame should be examined carefully for projects that are required to address potential institutional capacity gaps and simultaneously expected to conduct other development or investment activities. Collaboration between organizations or initiatives that have common interest and are engaged in similar activities benefits both initiatives. Integrating livelihood development activities (entry points, off-farm income generating) within natural resources management is tested and demonstrated by the project, which promotes participation, trust, and ownership by the community. Integrating the hydrologic monitoring with Integrated Watershed Management efforts as a unique approach benefited the project.Post-project transition arrangement is critical for sustainability of project outcomes.

title: Uganda - Fifth, Sixth, and Seventh Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Uganda - Fifth, Sixth, and Seventh Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: UG-Uganda  Poverty Reduction Support Credit 6 -- P090219,UG-Uganda  Poverty Reduction Support Credit 5 -- P090881,UG-Seventh Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC7) -- P101231
abstract: Ratings for the Fifth, Sixth, and Seventh Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC 5-7) Project for Uganda were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some key lessons learned included: the PRSCs are an excellent vehicle for dialogue, not only between the Government and Development Partners, but also between the Ministries. This is a well-known benefit of PRSCs, but it bears repeating. The ministries reported widely that the PRSC series created a valuable forum to discuss cross-cutting issues such as issues related to public service reform and decentralization. The ministries also expressed that their cases could be better heard in inter-ministerial, multi-donor settings. In the PRSC series, most of the indicators missed some element of the specific, measurable, attributable, and relevant or time bound (SMART) criteria, all of which are critical for indicators to be useful. For example, if an indicator is attributable, but not time-bound, then it cannot be useful to measure results of a program. Conversely, an indicator may be time bound, but too general to be attributable to the activity whose results are being measured. While the indicators for PRSC 5-7 were driven by the Government's results framework and Bank's desire to foster ownership, the Bank should have recognized that these were not suitable for the Bank's goal of measuring results. Nonetheless, there is frequently a concern that a policy operation that "takes its time" and which needs to advance incrementally may be viewed internally by the Bank as simply repeating actions of the prior credit. One solution might be that gradations should be better specified and nuanced, and actions and targets better calibrated, so that it will be clear that the policy agenda is moving forward while also moving at a rate where there is sustained government ownership and an actual ingraining of the policy reform. At the same time, the Bank may also need to better temper its ambitions about the pace and scope of reform that can realistically be achieved each year, recognizing that many reforms will be incremental, rather than substantial.

title: Ethiopia - Sustainable Land Management Project
report_name: Ethiopia - Sustainable Land Management Project
project_id: ET-Sustainable Land Management Project -- P107139,ET-ET-Sustainable Land Management Program  (FY08) -- P090789
abstract: Ratings for the Sustainable Land Management (SLM) Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, global environmental objective (GEO) outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was moderate, risk to GEO outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: establishing and scaling up SLM can be facilitated by putting in place a programmatic approach that can deliver multiple benefits downstream despite upstream transaction costs associated with convening and aligning financing, experiences, and approaches among partners and stakeholders. SLM should be considered as an integral part of rural development strategies that can deliver livelihood opportunities and improving environmental security. The demand-driven bottom-up approach adopted under SLMP-1 is relevant for natural resources management and local development in Ethiopia's rural space. It is important to provide enhanced support in technical design and implementation and operation and maintenance (O and M) of subprojects involving road improvements and small irrigation, as well as structural and vegetative land management practices. The need to build sustainable institutions at the local level is equally important since they are crucial for delivery of service and attainment of project objectives. Implementation of the project was initially constrained by inadequate monitoring and evaluation (M and E) capacity and poor financial management and procurement capacity at the Woreda level coupled with a high staff turnover. The experience of SLMP-1 highlights the importance of enhanced recruitment procedures, appropriate incentive mechanism (working conditions, training, etc.), and harmonization of salaries and benefits among Woreda staff working on different projects. To maximize dissemination (both within Ethiopia and the Africa region), visibility and perception of project actions and results, the implementation of SLMP-1 demonstrated the importance of including, within the structure of the Ministry of Agriculture (MoA), a knowledge management and communications team staffed by specialized professionals.

title: Kenya - Western Kenya Community Driven Development and Flood Mitigation Project
report_name: Kenya - Western Kenya Community Driven Development and Flood Mitigation Project
project_id: KE-Western Kenya CDD and Flood Mitigation Project -- P074106
abstract: Ratings for the Western Kenya Community Driven Development (CDD) and Flood Mitigation Project for Kenya were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was high, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: First, the project provides considerable evidence that a broad-based CDD initiative is highly relevant to the needs and aspirations of poor rural communities. CDD is an effective approach to bring quality benefits to a large number of people, over a wide geographic area, quickly and sustainably. Second, the project has demonstrated the benefits of empowering communities to plan development interventions and manage the implementation of local initiatives for income generation, food security and sustainable land management. Third, design of CDD projects, while taking a holistic and integrated approach, needs to be guided by an explicit theory of change and impact pathways that reduce complexities into manageable components that are clearly aligned to a specific and clear development objective. Fourth, the project developed a number of useful tools that can be scaled up in other projects, sectors and countries. Fifth, the design and implementation of this project demonstrate the challenge of balancing depth and breadth, selecting between deeper approaches and consolidation in a smaller number of communities to deliver quality benefits, and the thin spread approach across a large number of communities to reach more people which seemed to characterize this project. Finally, market access was a cross-cutting challenge across CIGs as they increased production. Economic and financial analysis revealed a clear pattern between farm models, with a declining rate of return as the distance to the market increased.

title: Mozambique - Transfrontier Conservation Areas and Tourism Development Project
report_name: Mozambique - Transfrontier Conservation Areas and Tourism Development Project
project_id: MZ-Transfrontier Conservation Areas and Tourism Development Project -- P076809,MZ-Transfrontier Conservation Areas and Tourism Development Project -- P071465
abstract: The Transfrontier Conservation Areas and Tourism Development Project for Mozambique received a moderately satisfactory outcome with a moderate risk to development outcome. Both the Banks and the Borrowers performances were moderately satisfactory. In March 2009, a reallocation of GEF proceeds was approved to increase the allocation of funds for training. In September 2009, a mid-term review (MTR) of the Project was conducted and several changes were agreed. The Project Implementation Unit (PIU) adopted these changes immediately after the MTR, including changes in staff, adoption of revised indicator targets and a revised work plan. The corresponding formal restructuring (Level 2) happened in August 2011, due to Governments delays in officially sending the request for Project restructuring. At the time of restructuring, partial disbursement of IDA credit and GEF grant had been disbursed, and resulted in changes to the Projects indicator targets, reallocation of Grant proceeds and changes to some Project activities. In March 2012, a second Level 2 restructuring was approved to allow some corrections in the disbursement categories of Consultant Services and Training of the IDA Credit and GEF Grant Agreements. In March 2013, the closing date was extended from June 30, 2013 to June 30, 2014. There were several lessons learned from this project and are as follows: (1) Continued and high-level political commitment to conservation is needed to move the agenda forward, particularly when pressures from other sectors on protected areas are strong. (2) Long-term World Bank engagement in the conservation and tourism agendas is needed to foster institutional changes. (3) Developing nature-based tourism asset is a long-term process. (4) Tourism as a means to generate sustainable financing for PAs. (5) Enhancing the benefits from PAs to local communities needs to go beyond the promotion of tourism, and encompass a broader array of integrated conservation development activities, particularly those related to sustainable natural resource management. (6) Professional facilitation is needed for the public sector to be able to attract private investment into PA concessions. (7) Importance of innovative partnerships in conservation. In order to mitigate the lack of local capacity and appropriate institutional coordination, the Project partnered with NGOs.

title: Tanzania - Marine and Coastal Environment Management Project
report_name: Tanzania - Marine and Coastal Environment Management Project
project_id: TZ-Marine and Coastal Environment Management -- P082492,TZ-Marine and Coastal Environmental Management Project -- P084213
abstract: Ratings for the Marine and Coastal Environment Management Project for Tanzania were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, GEO outcome was moderately unsatisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, risk to GEO outcome was substantial, Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and borrower performance was moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: complex, difficult operations in the fisheries sector with a high degree of innovation require an extended time horizon, a high degree of flexibility, continuous support for progressive capacity-building and repeat financing to maximize impact, learning and sustainability; given the complexity of the fisheries sector, which addresses both coastal and offshore issues and involves a wide range of institutions, a phased approach is recommended for improved management; the scope of pilot operations need to be carefully defined at the outset; when adopting a multispectral approach, the scope of activities needs to be focused on what the project can realistically achieve; when projects are heavily focused on mainstreaming into government structures, tensions can arise between the need to build the institutions' capacity and the need to effectively deliver the project.

title: Uganda - Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Operation Project
report_name: Uganda - Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Operation Project
project_id: UG-Poverty Reduction Support Credit 4 -- P074082
abstract: The Uganda Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Operation Project has a satisfactory outcome, is likely to be sustainable, and has a substantial institutional development impact. The performances of both the Bank and the Borrower are satisfactory. Among the lessons cited were the following: 1) In Uganda, the project was able to ensure specific reforms in local government procurement and these were included in the Cabinet paper and subsequently enshrined in law. Specific actions guiding reforms in anti-corruption, financial management, management of public finances and budget process were all realized. 2) Poverty Reduction Support Credits (PRSCs) are most effective when sequenced over a period of time, at least three years, and where each PRSC builds on actions taken in previous years. Expecting major reforms over a one-year period is unrealistic. However, designing actions each year which lead to outputs over a three year period is possible. 3) Definition of measurable outputs which lead to improved infant and maternal mortality is important. It is also important that the design of these interim outputs and their measurement is carried out in partnership with Government. 4) PRSCs may be the only external operational modality which can get interaction between different Government ministries on reforms: this strength of the PRSC instrument needs to be used sparingly. 5) Coordination of PRSCs should be carried out at the highest technical level of Government. When the Office of the Prime Minister took over coordination, other ministries were willing to recognize their right to coordinate as this is the arrangement agreed within Government. This ensured that very senior officials, often Permanent Secretaries attended the various PRSC meetings and ensured that the actions for which their ministry was responsible were taken. 6) Lastly, it is critical that other development partners are involved in the preparation of the PRSC.

title: Zambia - Support for Economic Expansion and Diversification Project
report_name: Zambia - Support for Economic Expansion and Diversification Project
project_id: ZM-SEED Biodiversity -- P074258,ZM-Support  for Economic Expansion and Diversification (SEED) -- P071407
abstract: Ratings for the Support for Economic Expansion and Diversification Project for Zambia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; GEO outcome were also moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was substantial; risk to GEO outcome was also substantial; Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory and borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: the complexity and diversity of the project required a substantially increased supervision bank budget due to the need to recruit additional sectoral specialists and consultants. The project's strategic choice was to build capacity of Zambia Wildlife Authority (ZAWA) mainly at park and regional management level and not to provide support for policy, legal or institutional reforms at ZAWA Head Quarter (HQ) level. A Project Development Objective (PDO) dependent on the streamlining of licenses that required legislative reforms to achieve outcome indicators without any proper risk mitigation measures was risky and reflecting poor project design. Legislative reforms in most cases are outside the control of a project, especially where they result in loss of discretionary power for the agency responsible under the law. The project should have used the restructuring process to refine the PDO, change outcome indicators and put into place more effective measures to mitigate risk.

title: Madagascar - First, Second and Third Poverty Reduction Support Operation Project
report_name: Madagascar - First, Second and Third Poverty Reduction Support Operation Project
project_id: MG-Poverty Reduction Support Credit I -- P070999,MG-MG-Poverty Reduction Support Credit  II -- P083326,MG-Third Poverty Reduction Strategy Credit -- P096102
abstract: Ratings for the First Poverty Reduction Support Operations (PRSC I) for Madagascar are as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, risk to the development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also rated moderately satisfactory. Ratings for the Second Poverty Reduction Support Operations (PRSC II) for Madagascar are as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, risk to the development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also rated satisfactory. Ratings for the Third Poverty Reduction Support Operations (PRSC III) for Madagascar are as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, risk to the development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also rated satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: 1) PRSCs I-III rightly focused on the critical areas of public finance management and human development. Future PRSCs may want to deepen support in these areas and focus on critical bottlenecks rather than broadening the areas of support to new areas. It may also be important to identify the institutional and absorptive capacities of each sector, to provide budget support to those sectors with the highest capacities and to use PRSCs in combination with sectoral projects to those areas where capacities are lowest. 2) The amount provided under the form of budget support has been revised downward for a better balance of overall Bank support between budget support and projects. The next series of PRSCs which would consist of three annual operations for US$40 million each seems to strike an adequate balance. Increase in budget support could be linked to credible and sustained improvement in revenue collection, civil service, and public sector management systems. 3) Both the Bank and the Government need to have realistic expectations about the breadth and pace of reform implementation. In particular, the political appetite for reforms must be aligned with the country's technical capacity to implement and absorb these reforms.

title: Madagascar - Poverty Reduction Support Operation Project
report_name: Madagascar - Poverty Reduction Support Operation Project
project_id: MG-Poverty Reduction Support Credit I -- P070999
abstract: The Madagascar Poverty Reduction Support Operation Project had a moderately satisfactory outcome, likely sustainability, substantial institutional development, overall satisfactory Bank performance, and overall moderately satisfactory Borrower performance. Some implications for subsequent operations are: The first year of implementation demonstrated the need for continued and increased support for public and financial management reforms. In particular, increase efforts to strengthen and build capacity in the sector ministries for budget formulation, procurement, and financial management. Greater realism is needed in setting targets and the timeframe. Prioritize and scale up the reform process to match the Government's capacity. Unpredictability in resource flows seriously hampered budget execution during 2004; improve predictability through better revenue forecasting. A better alignment of donor disbursements with the budget cycle might also help improve predictability. A firm commitment is actually more relevant than the timing of the disbursement; in the case of Madagascar, however, the resource availability at the outset is critical in providing the level of confidence and certainty needed to execute the budget and avoid stop-and-go cycles.

title: Ethiopia - First and Second Poverty Reduction Support Operations Project
report_name: Ethiopia - First and Second Poverty Reduction Support Operations Project
project_id: ET-FIRST POVERTY REDUCTION SUPPORT OPERATION -- P074014,ET-ET-PRSC 2 DPL (FY05) -- P077749
abstract: Ratings for the Ethiopia First and Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC) Operations Project were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was low or negligible, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the strength of the PRSC instrument in addressing a wide-range of cross-cutting themes can also be its greatest weakness if such a program needs to be prematurely terminated for any reason. Government ownership is crucial for proper design, implementation and results. Quality time and effort put into project formulation and preparation can put a project on a sound footing and minimize the need for mid-course corrections. Resources committed to ESW can strongly underpin project design and facilitate better implementation, especially where data on baseline conditions are inadequate to begin with. A project's scope and content should be matched well with the commitment and capacity displayed by the borrower. The Bank should retain the option of reducing the quantum of a DPL prior to effectiveness, if performance falls considerably short of initially and mutually agreed levels. Finally, where reform in vital area is slow, the Bank should continue to be engaged and serve as a facilitator.

title: Uganda - Third Poverty Reduction Support Credit
report_name: Uganda - Third Poverty Reduction Support Credit
project_id: UG-Third Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P074081
abstract: The ratings for the Poverty Reduction Support Credits for Uganda were as follows: the outcome was satisfactory, the sustainability was likely, the institutional development impact was modest, and the Bank performance was highly satisfactory and the borrower performance satisfactory. The lessons learned indicate that the most vital element in poverty reduction is arguably the improvement of incomes of the poor. While many efforts have been made to ensure civic involvement and participation, especially in the preparation of the Poverty Eradication Action Plan (PEAP) and in carrying out participatory poverty assessments, work remains to be done at the district level. Future Poverty Reduction Support Credits (PRSCs) should avoid being over-reaching, stating goals and results that cannot be achieved rapidly. Future PRSCs need to state results and outcomes in more concrete and measurable terms, although this is much easier said than done. Because of the learning-from-experience potential of the PRSC, and because of its lack of formal implementation beyond the agreement and enactment of Prior Actions, As the PRSC instrument evolved, there has been a clear improvement in the understanding of what needs to be done and of the most effective ways of doing it. Because Uganda is highly dependent on donor aid for its poverty reduction efforts, and because the more efficient delivery of aid through the PRSC has resulted in absorption problems, it is essential that the Bank, donors, and government develop and agree on a long-term financing strategy which should govern and coordinate future levels of donor aid. The two most problematic aspects of the PRSC, namely corruption and defense spending, need to be translated more effectively into essential requirements and pre-requisites for the entire Bank program of support, and not left to the PRSC as a burden to carry.

title: Ethiopia - First Phase Pastoral Community Development Project
report_name: Ethiopia - First Phase Pastoral Community Development Project
project_id: ET-Pastoral Community Development Project -- P075915
abstract: Ratings for the First Phase Pastoral Community Development Project for Ethiopia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: do not shy away from formally restructuring a project. Rather than hoping for the best when repeatedly confronting implementation problems, Bank teams should be proactive in proposing changes (such as in the results framework and indicators), which will result in greater clarity and a more substantive definition of project outcomes. Certain projects require closer supervision.
