Projects such as Community-Driven Development (CDD) projects, with new and unfamiliar processes, projects spread across large geographical areas where local procurement is required, projects covering different ethnic and/or religious groups where decision-makers often tend to bias allocations, in situations where very few or no projects have ever been implemented, and projects that include zones with known political tensions and civil unrest, require intensive supervision. A consistent results framework, with outcome indicators that are clearly linked to the project development objective (PDO) and differentiated from input/output indicators is very important. Institutional and community capacity building should be stressed more when there are new approaches and institutions.

title: Zambia - Emergency Drought Recovery Project
report_name: Zambia - Emergency Drought Recovery Project
project_id: ZM-Emergency Drought Recovery Project -- P080612
abstract: The overall objectives of the Emergency Drought Recovery Project (EDRP) for Zambia were to assist the government in maintaining key commitments to its economic and investment priorities as laid out in the Poverty Reduction Strategy Paper (PRSP) while meeting the exigencies of the food crisis, and to help restore productive capability of the affected population. The development objective was not revised during project implementation. Principal performance ratings are as follows: project outcome was satisfactory; sustainability was likely; institutional development impact was modest; and Bank and borrower performance was satisfactory. A number of lessons have been derived from the project and, it is hoped, will be learned by both the Bank and its borrowers. Some of the lessons learned are: The EDRP aimed at incorporating lessons learned from previous emergency projects, such as ' emergency operations must be simple and rapidly executable, and not undertake too broad an agenda of cross-cutting issues ', ' speed in implementation is a critical element of success '. These lessons remain valid, but they were not practiced during EDRP implementation. 2) Short term and long term objectives should not be combined in one lending instrument. 3) The Bank needs to apply its disbursement and procurement guidelines more flexibly in future emergency operations in order to adequately meet the urgent needs of the client, 4) Appropriate outcome-orientated monitoring and evaluation indicators must be identified at appraisal, even in an emergency operation when project preparation time is limited. The PIU was charged with identifying additional project performance indicators, but ultimately failed to complete the task. 5) Finally, the implementation of a multi-sectoral project such as the EDRP, should be complemented by multi-sectoral donor coordination to avoid duplication and inconsistencies.

title: Madagascar - Emergency Economic Recovery Project
report_name: Madagascar - Emergency Economic Recovery Project
project_id: MG-Emergency Economic Recovery Credit -- P080345
abstract: Ratings convey satisfactory project outcomes, likely sustainability, and modest institutional development impacts. Main lessons highlight the international community's immediate reaction to soften the crisis' social and economic impacts, and to avoid a severe shortfall of financing, as key to success. The challenge was to combine the quick-disbursement process, with adequate tracking of the use of credit proceeds, despite cumbersome administrative procedures that slowed the process, that potentially could undermine the impact on beneficiaries. Moreover, the participatory approach enhanced Government's commitment, which facilitated - from inception to closure - the dialogue in particular, and, cooperation during the project's implementation. Other lessons reinforce the value of nongovernmental organizations' involvement in project implementation, and, the need to find the right balance between challenging objectives, and limited implementation capacity, in a country just emerged from crisis.

title: Malawi - Emergency Drought Recovery Project
report_name: Malawi - Emergency Drought Recovery Project
project_id: MW-EMERGENCY DROUGHT RECOVERY PROJECT -- P080368
abstract: Project ratings are as follows: Project outcome is satisfactory; sustainability is likely; institutional development impact is substantial; Bank performance is satisfactory; and Borrower performance is satisfactory. Several lessons were cited including the following: (1) A technically sound emergency operation can only be designed on the basis of sound statistics and credible policy. (2) For emergency recovery loans to be successful there should be no impediments in the institutional arrangements that could slow down disbursements as this would defeat the purpose of the loan. (3) To make the fullest impact, emergency projects have to demonstrate quick wins in the shortest possible time. This entails starting with income transfer programs and quick disbursements that gain momentum rapidly and reach their peak in the first year. (4) Since the emergency operation should focus on impacting the affected target group immediately, it is important that safety nets be given a high priority and be appropriately costed. (5) To be readily responsive to Government in an emergency setting, it is essential that the supervision task team is country office based. (6) To avoid being in a response mode all the time, Government needs to look at ways of developing ex-ante instruments for responding to and managing extreme weather-based shocks to the system. (7) Especially for Emergency Relief Operations, an efficient monitoring and evaluation system needs to be developed and implemented in a very short time. (8) Disbursements based on prior claims and the submission of detailed supporting documents and original receipts are not ideal for Economic Recovery Loans, nor for speedy disbursements, nor suited for an environment where foreign exchange transactions are liberalized.

title: Uganda - Northern Uganda Social Action Fund Project
report_name: Uganda - Northern Uganda Social Action Fund Project
project_id: UG-UG-N Uganda Soc Action Fund (FY03) -- P002952
abstract: Ratings for the Northern Uganda Social Action Fund (NUSAF) Project for Uganda were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: transparency and accountability can be a challenge in hard-to-reach areas. However, participatory involvement of communities in sub-project management, public sharing of expenditures at the local level and follow-up on misuse of funds resulted in improved accountability and transparency. NUSAF 2 aims to further strengthen bottom-up accountability through the TAAC (transparency, accountability, and anti-corruption) subcomponent. One of the key lessons from this project is the challenge of targeting transient/nomadic communities. For this reason, and with the exception of subprojects that were implemented through the missionary Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs), the Karamoja sub-region remained a challenge throughout the life of the project. Placement of the executing agency under the Office of the Prime Minister (OPM) made difficult decision-making processes possible, allowed for mediation between local governments, and facilitated the flexible approach to project implementation. For instance, the Youth Opportunities Program (YOP) randomized experimental design is likely to deliver robust lessons that can be used in other regions with similar youth unemployment challenges.

title: Uganda - Protected Areas Management and Sustainable Use Project
report_name: Uganda - Protected Areas Management and Sustainable Use Project
project_id: UG-Protected Areas Management and Sustainable Use Project -- P065437,UG-Protected Areas Management and Sustainable Use GEF -- P075932
abstract: Ratings for the Protected Areas Management and Sustainable Use Project for Uganda were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; GEO outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate; risk to GEO outcome was moderate; Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory; and borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: project design should have a robust assessment of the governance challenges and plan mitigation measures accordingly. Long-term engagement in the sector and consistency of the supervision team are assets in using adaptive management methods to resolve issues. A phased approach allowed for the implementing agencies to, first, build a basic operational capacity and, then to benefit more fully from substantial field investment was appropriate under the circumstances. Decentralized institutional support is critical when protecting a vulnerable natural and cultural resource base. In developing an environmental program, competing needs of ecosystems and commercial development need to be balanced.

title: Tanzania - Lower Kihansi Environmental Management Project
report_name: Tanzania - Lower Kihansi Environmental Management Project
project_id: TZ-Lower Kihansi Environmental Management Project -- P073397
abstract: Ratings for the Lower Kihansi Environmental Management Project for Tanzania were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate; Bank performance was satisfactory and borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: a fundamental lesson is not to ignore downstream impacts when large infrastructure or hydropower projects are undertaken in sensitive ecosystems. It is good practice for the Bank to support development of improved Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) legislation and capacity as part of implementation of infrastructure or hydropower projects. Preventing the potential extinction of an endangered species is an exceedingly complex task; it is costly because of the scientific uncertainty regarding the processes involved. Considerable time is needed to respond to unexpected events such as diseases, mortality, and predators, which can wipe out whole populations even in captivity. When environmental impacts involving scientific uncertainty need to be addressed, it is prudent to involve partners from the scientific community early in the project cycle. Internalization of environmental management plans within the developer's operating principles and framework is good practice.

title: Madagascar - Community Development Project
report_name: Madagascar - Community Development Project
project_id: MG-Community Development Project -- P055166
abstract: Ratings for the Community Development Project for Madagascar were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: 1) a social fund is a very efficient mechanism to build the capacity of communities; 2) benefits of social funds will be considerably increased if their design allows them to be innovative and flexible in order to adapt to changing country circumstances and meet urgent needs; 3) it is in the interest of development partners such as the World Bank to continue to support well-performing and flexible social funds as they can be an efficient mechanism to finance, at short notice, responses to emergencies or additional activities to benefit vulnerable groups, including cash-for-work programs and other kinds of cash transfers; 4) compliance with national sector policy and strategy, as well as strong coordination with sector ministries, lead to better synergy and improvement of quality of investment and better sustainability of infrastructure built; and 5) demand driven programs can run the risk that the most able communities will fare the best, and social funds need to find ways of working effectively with the most vulnerable communities.

title: Eritrea - Emergency Reconstruction Program Project
report_name: Eritrea - Emergency Reconstruction Program Project
project_id: ER-Emergency Reconstruction -- P044674
abstract: The Eritrea Emergency Reconstruction Program Project has a satisfactory outcome, is likely to be sustainable, and has a modest institutional development impact. The performances of both the Bank and the Borrower are satisfactory. The lessons cited addressed issues of design and implementation, the management of multi-sector operations and donor coordination, sector-supporting strategies and actions, and the needs of the poor and gender. Specific lessons included: 1) the need for projects operating in post-conflict countries to build in flexibility with fungible resources, accessed based on justification, to be able to address unforeseeable needs and conditions adequately, as they occur during implementation; 2) giving consideration to separating fast implementing and disbursing interventions for restoration of livelihoods and to address emergency needs, from interventions that may tend to take longer and have a longer-term development impact. 3) the importance of access to markets and services for any economic and social recovery process; 4) mainstreaming project component implementation and incorporating component design into the agency's strategic plan in order to facilitate internalization and enhance ownership and sustainability; 5) having smaller and more focused projects that involve fewer implementing agencies; 6) harmonizing procedures using the same manual of procurement as reference, and with central financial management using one MIS and with general management under one responsible entity; 7) the need to consult with beneficiaries in identifying and finalizing needs and areas of support in order to start-up and implement activities; 8) providing minimal support to displaced families as an incentive for returning to villages; 9) adopting design and build options for implementing emergency road infrastructure improvements; 10) ensuring that emergency operations that target the private sector include interventions and monitoring indicators that address immediate needs for jumpstarting the private sector's contribution to the economy, and also build sustainability into project design by targeting export-oriented activities; 11) narrower targeting of criteria embracing the poor in particular, such as increased employment or income, to better focus assistance; and 12) targeting the needs of the female members of the communities.

title: Sao Tome and Prince - Public Resource Management Credit Project
report_name: Sao Tome and Prince - Public Resource Management Credit Project
project_id: ST-ST - Public Resource Management -- P069909
abstract: The Public Resource Management Credit Project for Sao Tome and Prince had the following ratings: the outcome was unsatisfactory, the sustainability was likely, the institutional development impact was modest, and the Bank and borrower performance were both unsatisfactory. The lessons learned indicate that it is important to ensure that any policy reform has government ownership. Without government commitment, it is difficult to achieve development objectives. During project preparation, special attention should be given to the project's content and how it fits within the context of prevailing weak institutional capacity and the level of political volatility. The Letter of Development Policy (LDP) should have been much less ambitious, and more focused, and the project design should have been much simpler. Consideration should be given whether an adjustment operation is the right instrument for a country like Sao Tome and Principe given its political volatility and weak institutional capacity. The program showed an unusual phenomenon that while the adjustment operation did not perform well, the Technical Assistance (TA) part of the program did well. The linkage between the Credit and the TA activities was not strongly established at the beginning and the TA was not programmed closely/in a timely manner to support the reform program sufficiently. Increased supervision funding would have been useful so as to undertake multisectoral and more in-depth supervision. The government should ensure that capacity that has already been built with the help of the project will remain within MOF, and that staff, who were involved with the project, are not shifted to other places. The collaboration of the Bank with the IMF had a positive impact on the outcome of the project.

title: Tanzania - Programmatic Structural Adjustment Credit (PSAC) Project
report_name: Tanzania - Programmatic Structural Adjustment Credit (PSAC) Project
project_id: TZ-Programmatic Structural Adjustment Credit  CR.no.3379-TA -- P002822
abstract: Project ratings are as follows: Project outcome is satisfactory; sustainability is likely; institutional development impact is substantial; Bank performance is satisfactory; and Borrower performance is satisfactory. Lessons learnt from this operation concerned the need for government commitment; recognizing limitations and constraints faced by governments; the need for an effective communication strategy to develop stakeholder consensus; involving downstream/partner implementing agencies at an early stage; being realistic in program design and implementation; effective design of complex actions by laying out the steps clearly and to paying attention to their sequence and linkages; the advantages of the flexibility of floating tranche design; building crucial capacity at an early stage; the importance of involvement of the Country Office; improving predictability of resource flows; and coordinating with bilaterals and other international financial institutions.

title: Mozambique - Coastal and Marine Biodiversity Management Project
report_name: Mozambique - Coastal and Marine Biodiversity Management Project
project_id: MZ-Coastal and Marine Biodiversity Management Project -- P070305,MZ-Coastal and Marine Biodiversity Management Project -- P035919
abstract: Ratings for the Coastal and Marine Biodiversity Management Project for Mozambique were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; Global Environment Objectives (GEO) outcomes were moderately satisfactory; the risk to development outcome was significant; the risk to GEO outcome was significant; the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory; and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the project structure and objectives had a focus on learning, but the lack of an adequate monitoring and evaluation (M&E) system prevented to capture some of the lessons learned during implementation. From the identification the Bank should support the implementing agencies in establishing and maintaining a valid M&E system, in case of insufficient capacity. This project was designed as pilot which objective to test sustainable development mechanism involving conservation and economic activities. The project had altogether 10 indicators which were difficult to achieve given the economic and political situation of the country at the time. Small project should have modest objectives and indicators. A combination of integrated development planning outcomes with policy reform would have been beneficial to the project outcomes, by setting the legal or administrative basis for the use of spatial development plans after the project closes.

title: Burundi - Emergency Economic Recovery Project
report_name: Burundi - Emergency Economic Recovery Project
project_id: BI-BI Emergency Economic Recovery Credit -- P064556
abstract: The ratings for the project were as follows: the outcome was satisfactory, the sustainability was likely, the institutional development impact was modest, and the Bank and borrower performance were both satisfactory. The lessons learned indicate that the credit served as an effective instrument during the emergency and early post-conflict phases in Burundi. It assisted in jump-starting a failing economy, stimulated the private sector and helped the government provide essential social services (especially in education, health and agriculture) and to cope with the resettlement of displaced persons and returning refugees. It likely helped demonstrate to a wider public that the peaceful settlement of conflict was not leading instead to greater misery among the non-participants. The economic, political and social returns accruing from investment in such activities in a post-conflict country is considerable, especially in Burundi, where the total collapse of the economy seemed imminent, due to risks associated with an extremely fragile and volatile political environment. However, in order to be effective, project design and implementation needed to be sensitive to the prevailing circumstances. The most important lesson emerging from the project is that in a crisis situation where the atmosphere of insecurity was pervasive, the degree of flexibility and devolution of responsibilities adopted at design stage of the project cycle greatly facilitated project implementation. The need to pay particular attention to "poverty alleviation" components of post-conflict programs cannot be overemphasized. The Bank's requirement to include these as an integral part of any program proved beneficial in the case of Burundi.

title: Mozambique - Flood Emergency Recovery Project
report_name: Mozambique - Flood Emergency Recovery Project
project_id: MZ-Flood Emergency Recovery Project -- P070432
abstract: The outcome of the Flood Emergency Recovery Project for Mozambique was satisfactory, the sustainability was likely, the institutional development impact was modest, and the Bank and borrower performance were both satisfactory. The lessons learned found that it was important to mobilize a multisectoral team at an early stage to produce the macroeconomic, sectoral damage, and reconstruction assessment. Sharing the analytical documents, such as the damage assessment and the macroeconomic evaluation with donors and Governments contributes to a common understanding of the dimensions involved. Promote partnership in an open and transparent manner. Coordinate and leverage external assistance from the international community for reconstruction. Field negotiations with quality control and legal staff permitted a smooth process in the emergency project.

title: Guinea-Bissau - Second Emergency Food Security Support Project
report_name: Guinea-Bissau - Second Emergency Food Security Support Project
project_id: GW-Second Emergency Food Security Support Project -- P148886
abstract: Ratings for the Second Emergency Food Security Support Project for Guinea-Bissau were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the successful implementation of this project can be attributed to an effective collaboration and coordination among the multiple stakeholders and implementing entities involved, including the TCU, WFP, regional technical offices (MoA and Ministry of Education), local NGOs and World Bank teams. In the absence of this effective teamwork, it will have been difficult to implement this project successfully given the shortened implementation period. Delivering results requires high quality and effective implementation readiness arrangements. A sound results framework is a critical tool of these arrangements. A robust results framework should include all building blocks that ensure the efficient allocation and management of the project resources (inputs) to generate the outputs necessary for achieving the expected development outcome of the project. Given scare resources and demanding development objectives, it is essential to leverage development partners’ in order to achieve the outcome expected from investment operations. However, assuring accountability for results for all participants involved in implementation is crucial, and requires strong leadership and commitment for results from all, especially on the part of the Borrower. The Borrower must ensure that all implementing agencies and partners put in place instruments and methods that ensure a systematic monitoring of implementation progress and results, and a thorough evaluation of the impact of the project operations. Without such a leadership, it is difficult to ensure the high quality implementation necessary to achieve development effectiveness measurable through objective outcome indicators.

title: Cameroon - Agriculture Investment and Market Development Project
report_name: Cameroon - Agriculture Investment and Market Development Project
project_id: CM-The Agriculture Investment and Market Development Project -- P143417
abstract: Ratings for the Agriculture Investment and Market Development Project for Cameroon were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: government counterpart budget availability and associated risks for project feasibility and sustainability need diligent assessment during project preparation for Cameroon. In fragile and conflict and violence (FCV) affected countries such as Cameroon, understanding capacity of different stakeholders is key to sustainability. Understanding supply side constraints is key to improving access to finance. Attention to post-production elements of commodity supply is necessary to ensure successful off-take productive partnership (PPs). Broader institutional reforms in key subsectors in the Project are important for successful implementation. Purchase contracts should be carefully designed to be aligned with user needs. In a country where the agroindustry is not fully developed, relying on large formal agribusinesses and commercial banks to build PPs was a missed opportunity.

title: Senegal - Casamance Development Pole Project
report_name: Senegal - Casamance Development Pole Project
project_id: SN-SN:Casamance Development Pole Project -- P125506
abstract: Ratings for the Casamance Development Pole Project for Senegal were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: addressing the challenges of inclusion, fragility, and sustainability characteristic of post-conflict situations require multi-faceted investments tailored to local circumstances. A focus on key value chains with a holistic approach is best suited to support the economic empowerment of marginalized groups like women and youth. Faced with a need to adapt to climate change and increase disaster risk management, a package approach to supporting livelihood activities and infrastructure can potentially deliver resilience. Participatory planning, programming, budgeting, and M and E processes can elicit ownership of targeted populations and guide implementation. Territorial and community-based approaches should be combined to both build on comparative advantage at the macro level and promote local development at the grassroots. Community-led approaches have the potential to strengthen social resilience, and, combined with the use of information, communication, and technology (ICTs), can be powerful ways to deal with emergency preparedness and management, for instance to respond to crises such as Covid 19.

title: Cote d'Ivoire - First, Second and Third Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Cote d'Ivoire - First, Second and Third Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: CI-Cote d'Ivoire PRSC 1 -- P127449
abstract: Ratings of First, Second and Third Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project for Cote d'Ivoire were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, risk to development outcome was high, Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately unsatisfactory. Some of the  lessons learned included: (i) The trade-off between reform and disbursement objectives needs to be acknowledged; (ii) A programmatic sequence of reforms is helpful to ensure proper implementation for any one sector or issue; (iii) Triggers should be agreed with enough advance notice to allow the authorities to ensure smooth implementation; (iv) If sensitive sectors are going to be addressed, as they probably should, attention needs to be paid to risks of policy reversal; and (v) Greater care should be taken to define results indicators and targets.

title: Liberia - Smallholder Tree Crop Revitalization Support Project
report_name: Liberia - Smallholder Tree Crop Revitalization Support Project
project_id: LR-LR Smallholder Tree Crop Revitalization Support Project -- P113273
abstract: Ratings of Smallholder Tree Crop Revitalization Support Project for Liberia were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) due consideration must be given to the country’s context in deciding the project scope and implementation arrangements; (ii) ensuring that the staff skill sets on the project team are fully aligned with the project objectives and related activities is critical to successful project implementation; (iii) engaging in extensive upfront consultations/negotiations to agree on prerequisites with potential partners is required, particularly with the private sector and in an fragility, conflict and violence (FCV) context; (iv) good timing and sequencing of project activities is important to ensure outcomes can be achieved and lessons learned; and (v) following the framework approach for environmental and social safeguards, sub-projects of similar characteristics in the same region should be combined under one environmental and social management plan (ESMP).

title: Togo - Fifth Economic Governance and Recovery Credit Project : Togo - Fifth Economic Governance and Recovery Credit Project
report_name: Togo - Fifth Economic Governance and Recovery Credit Project
project_id: TG-TG-Economic Recovery & Gov. Credit 5 -- P126897
abstract: Ratings for the Fifth Economic Governance and Recovery Credit Project for Togo were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was significant, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. One lesson is that even in a politically uncertain environment a well-designed and closely supervised operation can deliver results. A second lesson is that in a fragile country facing deep structural reforms, insufficient political attention might derail the entire reform program. This can be illustrated by how the casual introduction to the International Monetary Fund (IMF) of a proposal to create a revenue authority, contributed to a liquidity crisis late in 2012. Inspired by similar propositions in Burundi and Rwanda, the Togolese authorities decided in early 2012 to work towards the introduction of a revenue authority (OTR) by end 2012. The third lesson is that in a fragile country a political economy analysis should guide the team in selecting prior actions and indicators. Finally, a fourth lesson is that ownership requires political will as well as buy-in from technical staff. The technical level there was (and still is) considerable appetite to implement the reform program. Still, these same reformists demonstrated limited pro-activeness when it came to solving problems. This may be a capacity problem, but is more likely to be the long term effect from years of economic and financial mismanagement during which the ability to maintain a position depended on passive obedience to higher authorities. To change this, clear leadership from the political top is needed.

title: Chad - Agriculture Production Support Project
report_name: Chad - Agriculture Production Support Project
project_id: TD-Agriculture Production Support Project -- P126576
abstract: Ratings for the Agriculture Production Support Project for Chad were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the Bank performance was unsatisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality performance was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the project’s implementation shows that emergency operations are most effective when they prioritize actions oriented on the immediate restoration of productive assets for food-insecure rural households, instead of trying to build complex CDD systems. Lack of Government commitment to the project approach and the supervisory and oversight functions for the project undermines the implementation success of a project; Local commitment and ownership creates optimal conditions to the successful implementation of a project and the sustainability of the development outcomes; capacity building support through training and technical assistance enhances the ability of community associations and Councils to identify prepare and implement subprojects, thereby augmenting their capacity to compete for investment funds. Technical assistance needs to be targeted to weaker local government councils to improve their planning, management and financial capacity to participate in the project. Locating and/or developing sources of technical assistance in rural areas require significant attention and needs to be monitored at the local level. Sound and consistent technical support, upstream and downstream of productive and social infrastructure investments has a good chance to ensure sound execution of and good returns from such investments.

title: Ghana - Third and Fourth Agriculture Development Policy Operation Project
report_name: Ghana - Third and Fourth Agriculture Development Policy Operation Project
project_id: GH-Ghana Fourth Agriculture Development Policy Operation -- P122808,GH-Ghana Third Agriculture Development Policy Operation -- P122796
abstract: Ratings for the Third and Fourth Agriculture Development Policy Operation (AgDPO) Project for Ghana were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was substantial; Bank performance was satisfactory; and borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: outcomes and indicators that measure those outcomes should be closely linked to prior actions and triggers. Whereas policy reforms are expected to eventually result in changes at the farm level, prior actions and triggers only cause change at an intermediate level, which must be seen as necessary steps towards achieving the Project Development Objective (PDO). Sector-specific DPOs have a technical ministry as the main counterpart and client for policy development, and the Ministry of finance and economic planning as the contractual partner and client to whom the resources are disbursed. The AgDPO series successfully integrated a number of policy issues that are relevant to the implementation of a number of Bank-financed investment lending operations. An important lesson for future operations is to ensure that the policy agenda underlying the operation is complementary to the Bank's and other development partners' investment lending operations, thus creating synergies that enhance the policy making process as well as program implementation. The design of AgDPO policy matrices and the tracking of prior actions are major undertakings that need to be better integrated in Ministry of Food and Agriculture's (MoFA's) planning process.

title: Togo - Fourth Economic Governance and Recovery Grant Project
report_name: Togo - Fourth Economic Governance and Recovery Grant Project
project_id: TG-TG-Economic Recovery & Gov. Grant 4 -- P122806
abstract: Ratings for the Fourth Economic Governance and Recovery Grant Project for Togo were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: one lesson is that ensuring progress in a stand-alone Development Policy Operation (DPO), in an environment with low capacity by the recipient, requires the ability to supervise closely. A second lesson is that, in the face of low recipient capacity, the Bank's team needs to remind the authorities about the importance of the results framework and the implications of not meeting indicators. A third lesson regards the care that needs to be extended at the definition of prior actions and results indicators. A final lesson is that meaningful reforms (such as in cotton) may take years to complete and are hard to support by annual stand-alone DPOs.

title: Benin - Agricultural Productivity and Diversification Project
report_name: Benin - Agricultural Productivity and Diversification Project
project_id: BJ-Agricultural Productivity and Diversification -- P115886
abstract: Rating of Agricultural Productivity and Diversification Project for Benin were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: (i) A strong private sector involvement in the seed production system is key to improving the supply of planting materials to producer; (ii) Creating an enabling environment is key for increased private sector investments in the value chains; (iii) Key institutional reforms in the agricultural sector are important for empowering producers’ apex organizations and interprofessional bodies; and (iv) Access to finance, availability of inputs, and efficient advisory services are key ingredients for fostering the adoption of productivity-enhancing technologies and food and nutrition security.

title: Cote d'Ivoire - Third Economic Governance and Recovery Grant Project
report_name: Cote d'Ivoire - Third Economic Governance and Recovery Grant Project
project_id: CI-Economic Governance & Recovery Grant  III -- P117281
abstract: Ratings for the Third Economic Governance and Recovery Grant Project for Cote d'Ivoire were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was high, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: The main lesson is that a well designed, non-programmatic series of stand-alone operations can form a basis for reasonably predictable policy-based support, even in highly uncertain political environments such as Côte d’Ivoire. This operation has facilitated progress even as it faced major risks. Second, the identification of a single counterpart from the Government side offered a strong advantage in implementation of reforms. This has eased the return to the reform agenda after the crisis, many of the counterparts on the government side have remained in place. Third, the operations relative success in difficult circumstances illustrates the need, underlined in the good practice principles referred to earlier, for parsimony and selectivity in choosing prior actions, outcome indicators, and targets. A more complex design, a longer list of actions and targets, or a broader set of areas of intervention would likely have resulted in a less satisfactory operational impact.

title: Guinea-Bissau - Emergency Food Security Support Project  : Guinea-Bissau - Emergency Food Security Support Project
report_name: Guinea-Bissau - Emergency Food Security Support Project
project_id: GW-European Union Food Crisis Rapid Response Facility Trust Fund -- P120214
abstract: Ratings for the Emergency Food Security Support Project for Guinea-Bissau were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Grantee performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the World Bank was very pleased to work with World Food Program (WFP), which successfully implemented component one of the project. No problems were experienced at the working level. Even so, the very successful collaboration and other positive outcomes of this project will have improved further if WFP had been more transparent in sharing information with the Technical Coordination Unit (TCU) and World Bank on a timely basis; strengthened staff and/or Nongovernmental organization (NGO) capacity to monitor implementation in all regions over time; more stringently evaluated the qualifications of the phase one contractors for rehabilitating feeder roads; and evaluated the impact of the component's activities separately rather than as part of its overall program (as done for the school feeding program). Furthermore, problems arose in providing a consistent supply of food for the school feeding and food-for-work programs, so students and work program beneficiaries did not always receive the planned basket of food commodities. Future collaboration with WFP should address these issues prior to plan collaboration and project implementation.

title: Burkina Faso - Agricultural Productivity and Food Security Project
report_name: Burkina Faso - Agricultural Productivity and Food Security Project
project_id: BF-Agricultural Productivity and Food Security Project -- P114236
abstract: The overall outcome of the Agricultural Productivity and Food Security Project for Burkina Faso is satisfactory, with satisfactory Bank performance and substantial monitoring and evaluation quality. A new Project Development Objective (PDO) indicator was introduced in 2014 to measure the increase in fish production in the targeted zones, with a target of 540 metric tons (MT) at the end of the project. The number and names of components remained unchanged throughout the project, but all three components expanded the scope of some activities (adding interventions or increasing the number of targets) and reduced the scope of others (transferring some activities to other projects or suspending an activity as warranted). Lessons and recommendations the Project provided are: (a) Scaling-up warrantage could enhance access to finance for farmers and improve food availability for rural markets; (b) Thorough planning is important to successfully implement an e-voucher distribution system; (c) Strengthen government capacity for better service delivery instead of bypassing government actors; (d) Multi-stakeholder innovation platforms improve the prioritization of investments and enhance coordination of the value chain.

title: Mali - Third, Fourth and Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credits Project
report_name: Mali - Third, Fourth and Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credits Project
project_id: ML-ML-Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P117270,ML-ML-Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P122483,ML-Mali: Poverty Reduction Support Credit 3 -- P113451
abstract: Ratings for the Third, Fourth and Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credits Project for Mali were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory; risk to development outcome was high; Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory; and borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: the program was complex, ambitious and clearly did not have the full buy-in of the national authorities on key structural reforms. The lesson to take from this is that credibility of the World Bank is diminished when key actions are constantly delayed or watered down to maintain a program, such as, for instance, the adoption of a mechanism for electricity adjustment. Building a consensus is essential for introducing reforms. The decision making process is slow in Mali, as leaders seek to build consensus. As experience has shown, however, this results in constantly postponing much needed reforms. It can also act as a smokescreen for a small rent seeking elite that is likely to resist any reform that would reduce its privileges, even if it would benefit a large part of the population. Technical assistance is critical to help a low capacity administration implement a complex reform program. Rapid population growth (3.6 per cent per annum estimated in the 2009 census) is not conducive to significant poverty reduction in a fragile environment. Economic growth at about five percent per annum is a solid achievement, but not strong enough to make a sufficient dent in poverty. The main obstacle to higher economic growth is the poor investment climate due to weak physical infrastructure, limited access to finance, and corruption. Indicators are signals given to stakeholders on expected outcomes. They have to be carefully selected and monitored. World Bank credibility is diminished when many indicators are dropped/revised/introduced in mid-course. After an unsatisfactory program, it is necessary to take a pause and review in detail with the Government the reasons for poor performance. In the health and education sector, a lesson from the Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC) series is the clear need to continue strengthening service delivery for education and health in rural and underserved areas for more equitable access. In this perspective, the need to move ahead with the decentralization agenda is clear, as regards strengthening administrative and institutional capacities and financial resources.

title: Togo - Second Economic Recovery and Governance Grant Project
report_name: Togo - Second Economic Recovery and Governance Grant Project
project_id: TG-Togo: Economic Recovery and Gov. Grant 2 -- P113456
abstract: Ratings for the Second Economic Recovery and Governance Grant Project for Togo were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: seeking synergies between prior actions and follow-up measures can help amplify their effect, for example, the publication of phosphate revenues, together with the recapitalization of the banks and the referral of irregularities in the cotton sector to the Minister of Justice can be considered to have had a cumulative impact on the re-building of public confidence in government, a major underlying goal of reforms to promote transparency and better governance. The continuity of Bank staff is important as it helps create an institutional memory. Staff has been stable since 2005 in the case of financial sector reform, as well as continuity and growing experience of key Government officials in both the Ministry of Economy and Finance (MEF) and concerned line ministries. The importance of arrangements for ensuring responsive technical assistance to fill knowledge gaps or fine tune reform measures, the Low-Income Country Under Stress (LICUS) trust fund and ongoing operations such as the financial sector and governance project are examples.

title: Cote d'Ivoire - Second Economic Governance and Recovery Grant Project
report_name: Cote d'Ivoire - Second Economic Governance and Recovery Grant Project
project_id: CI-Cote d'Ivoire: Economic Governance & Recovery Grant (EGRG) II -- P112368
abstract: Ratings for the Second Economic Governance and Recovery Grant (ECRG II) Project for Cote d'Ivoire were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Lessons learned include: the design has benefited from the guidance provided by Operations Policy and Country Services (OPCS), lessons from the first EGRG and other development policy operations in post-conflict countries (notably in Togo and Liberia). The importance of careful risk analysis which takes into account the political and social dimensions, identifying and weighing the relative importance of special interests. This is especially important in the case of fragile transitional coalitions. Given the difficulty of getting a consensus on difficult issues, it is essential to limit the focus of reform to key functions of the Government (public finance management) and to sectors vital to economic recovery and growth (energy, cocoa, and the financial sector) and on policy measures which 'open the door' to a deepening of reform. Once reform has found its footing, it is important not to slow momentum but rather deepen the reform, rather than broadening the scope (which risks over-loading decision makers' focus and span of control while stretching high-level human resources). In a fluid, post-conflict environment, an important criterion for selecting policy measures is the likely impact on public confidence, notably by improving governance and transparency and communicating results effectively to the public, as well as informing other key factors such as external private investors. It is also important to complement and balance broad poverty-oriented measures with parallel steps to increase private sector confidence. The EGRG II disbursement supported a program of domestic debt arrears clearance which first helped small businesses weather the economic recession before moving on to address larger claims on the basis of their seniority. The process was transparent, with consultation of the creditors and publication of settlements. These steps did much to restore the private sector's faith in the Government.

title: Guinea-Bissau - Emergency Food Security Response Project
report_name: Guinea-Bissau - Emergency Food Security Response Project
project_id: GW-Guinea Bissau--Food Price Crisis Response Program -- P113468
abstract: Ratings for the Emergency Food Security Response Project for Guinea-Bissau were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, risk to development outcome was high, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: country ownership ensures achievement of development objectives, particularly in fragile countries where government leadership is lacking. The involvement of national non-governmental organizations (NGOs) in providing technical assistance and on the-job training to producer groups increases the likelihood of continued capacity building and guarantees that new development management skills stay in the communities that need those most. Capacity constraints in fragile countries are persistent; they often negatively affect results and must be properly mitigated during design - not just implementation. The new global food crisis response program (GFRP) project, currently being formulated, has taken under advisement the lessons related to persistent weak capacity and will rely heavily on world food program (WFP) for implementation, monitoring and evaluation (M and E), and fiduciary management. A minimum set of project readiness conditions, coupled with intense implementation support, is necessary for emergency operations to succeed.

title: Nigeria - Third National Fadama Development Project
report_name: Nigeria - Third National Fadama Development Project
project_id: NG-THIRD NATIONAL  FADAMA  DEVELOPMENT  PROJECT  (FADAMA III) -- P096572
abstract: Ratings of Third National Fadama Development Project of Nigeria were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: (i) Devolving management decision making and coordination to Management Committees of Fadama Community Association (FCAs), Desk Officers, and Facilitators in addition to the State Project Implementation Unit enhanced empowerment, social inclusion, participation, ownership, and sustainability; (ii) The presence of good local institutions is important to translate local aspirations into collective action, and it is a core element of a good Community-driven development (CDD) project; (iii) The project combined hard investments (FUG asset creation and community infrastructure) with soft technical support (capacity building and advisory support); (iv) Rather than pre-determining which assets to support, the project left the menu of options for investment support relatively open, to ensure that it would meet the actual demands of the project beneficiaries; (v) The design of Fadama III (and its predecessor projects) proved to be robust and agile; (vi) The inability to release counterpart funds in a timely way, exacerbated by the country’s financial constraints, affected timely implementation of programmed activities; (vii) The huge demand from states and local governments caused this national project to spread its efforts thinly and presented a challenge for implementation; and (viii) The Boko Haram insurgency in northern Nigeria nearly destroyed the Fadama legacy in that area. Collaborative engagements with specialized institutions (World Food Programme (WFP), Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) and Non-governmental organization (NGOs) were critical for delivering project interventions in this active conflict arena.

title: Cote d'Ivoire - Implementation Completion and Result Report on the Economic Governance and Recovery Grant Project
report_name: Cote d'Ivoire - Implementation Completion and Result Report on the Economic Governance and Recovery Grant Project
project_id: CI-Cote d'Ivoire: Economic Governance & Recovery Grant (EGRG) -- P083583
abstract: Ratings for the Implementation Completion and Result Report on the Economic Governance and Recovery Grant (EGRG) Project for Cote d'Ivoire were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: given the capacity bottlenecks in the country and the fragile and politically complex post-conflict situation, it is necessary to ensure that a development policy operation is accompanied by appropriately targeted technical assistance to ensure that the commitment and willingness lead to reform results. In this case, the EGRG was supported by Governance and Institutional Development Technical Assistance (TA) Project and a Low-Income Country Under Stress (LICUS) Trust Fund. In such post-conflict situations, it is particularly important to select a focused number of prior actions that are deemed essential to supporting the government's broader reform program. Such selectivity and coordination not only reduces the transaction costs for the government that is burdened by multiple management demands while the capacity is weak, but it also increases the likelihood that such focused actions are implemented and the reform program is moved forward. Given the risks and complexity of this operation in the country's context, close coordination with other development partners has been indispensable to mitigate risks, realize synergies, and improve the leverage in the policy dialogue. Collaboration with the International Monetary Fund (IMF) was particularly important.

title: Congo, Republic of - Agricultural Development and Rural Roads Rehabilitation Project
report_name: Congo, Republic of - Agricultural Development and Rural Roads Rehabilitation Project
project_id: CG-CG Rep. Agricultural Development and Rural Roads Rehabilitation Proj. -- P095251
abstract: Ratings of Agricultural Development and Rural Roads Rehabilitation Project for Congo, Republic of were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and M and E Quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: (i) The degree of private involvement, when carefully matched to the objectives and needs of the project, can bring stronger ownership and participation of stakeholders in project implementation; (ii) Mapping of stakeholders and profiling of different local conditions during project preparation would help avoid delays due to insufficient capacities; (iii) The link between actors along the entire value chain could be strengthened to ensure efficient matching of buyers and sellers especially in the outputs market; (iv) Synergy must be created among stakeholders to ensure the scaling up of project activities; (v) Considering the limited absorption capacity of project partners, a longer implementation period should be considered with phased approach and geographically-concentrated activities; (vi) The organizational capacities of producers in terms of a holistic approach to the value chain should be improved to facilitate the search for new markets and the negotiation of better prices; and (vii) Infrastructure management committees should be given the means to collect and manage funds.

title: Benin - Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Benin - Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: BJ-Poverty Reduction Strategy Credit - 1st PRSC -- P072003,BJ-THIRD POVERTY REDUCTION SUPPORT CREDIT -- P083313,BJ-SECOND POVERTY REDUCTION STRATEGY CREDIT -- P074313
abstract: Ratings for the First Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project (PRSC I) for Benin are as follows: outcome was satisfactory, risk to the development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also rated satisfactory. Ratings for the Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project (PRSC II) for Benin are as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, risk to the development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was rated moderately satisfactory. Ratings for the Third Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project (PRSC III) for Benin are as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, risk to the development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was rated moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: 1) selectivity and simplicity of the design are essential to the success of the operation. Obstacles due to administrative resistance to change, as well as due to lack of capacity cannot be underestimated. It is better to start with a very selective matrix and enlarge progressively, building on success in some components. There is also a need to be realistic about the scope and pace of reform; 2) it is important to properly align the PRSC with budget management, so that the priority components of the PRSC and the performance budgets of those ministries are supportive of each other. It is also important that budget support be phased with the budget cycle, in order to properly impact on budget composition, and to provide budget support in a timely manner; 3) the effectiveness of the PRSCs in supporting sector reforms can be highly increased by using a sector wide approach, as was successfully experienced in rural water supply. The approach federated donors around the government's objectives and allowed a mix of central government programs, decentralized programs, budget support and projects to combine efficiently; and 4) PRSCs (I-III) rightly focused on the critical areas of governance and basic service delivery. Future PRSCs need to deepen support in the areas of reforms where progress has been slow and focus on critical bottlenecks rather than broadening the areas of support to new areas.

title: Senegal - Agricultural Markets and Agribusiness Development Project
report_name: Senegal - Agricultural Markets and Agribusiness Development Project
project_id: SN-Agricultural Markets and Agribusiness Development Project -- P083609
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Markets and Agribusiness Development Project for Senegal were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: it is easier to build agricultural export facilities with project funds than to find private entrepreneurs willing and capable to take them over and run them. This holds true even when project management is aware of this fact and tries from the beginning to prepare for handover. Horticultural production and exports, especially in the greater Dakar area, are situated in a fast-changing environment, where factors such as rapid urbanization and relative costs of different modes of transport (air, sea, and road) can quickly change the optimal location of export facilities and render some of them no longer useful or financially viable. Projects that seek to introduce game-changing institutional and technical innovations and that depend for their success on the buy-in of people who cannot be identified in advance (auto-selection) must have a first-class, professional, interactive communication strategy that uses all forms of media, including the internet, and from the very beginning. And the public must be able to easily access updated information about how to participate in the project, otherwise implementation delays are unavoidable.

title: Mali - Agriculture Competitiveness and Diversification Project
report_name: Mali - Agriculture Competitiveness and Diversification Project
project_id: ML-Agricultural Competitiveness and Diversification Project -- P081704
abstract: Ratings for the Agriculture Competitiveness and Diversification Project for Mali were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: contracting a private service provider with experience and expertise in the sector to implement project activities is the best approach in countries or regions with limited capacities. This project relied on the private sector and nongovernmental organizations for executing project activities that reduced the burden on the PCU. Hiring a single lead contractor, charged with hiring and supervising the necessary experts for the job to be done, can bring about the desired outcome. It is necessary to include both investment and operating costs in business plans, which provides a fuller picture of resources needed to finance proposals prepared by beneficiaries. In addition to demonstration, dissemination, and adoption of innovative technologies, projects with support to value chains should include an incentive mechanism, preferably with grant or technical assistance to encourage developers to acquire and adapt innovative technologies and techniques promoted by the project. This project promoted appropriate technologies but these projects were adopted because of the grant mechanism the project provided. The dividend from reducing post-harvest loss, improvement in conservation, processing and packaging is very substantial that sufficient resources should be allocated early in project design. A corollary point to this is that projects should actively promote to the private sector the need for investing in these areas that have a huge return.

title: Mauritania - Second Phase of Integrated Development Program for Irrigated Agriculture Project
report_name: Mauritania - Second Phase of Integrated Development Program for Irrigated Agriculture Project
project_id: MR-MR:INTEGRATED DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM FOR IRRIGATED AGRIC. APL # 2 -- P088828
abstract: Ratings for the Second Phase of Integrated Development Program for Irrigated Agriculture Project for Mauritania were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was high, the Bank performance was unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: for many producers, credit, especially long-term credit to finance part of irrigation rehabilitation, is not the appropriate instrument. As the first order of business, an irrigation project has to demonstrate to farmers that the volume and quality of their products (and thus income) will be enhanced through a given operation before farmers commit to the objective of the project. High costs investments on equipment and facilities should only be undertaken once ownership, operations and management have been well settled. Otherwise there is the real possibility, as was the case under PDIAIM I and II, that the equipment and facilities will not be used (case of facility in Rosso) or for completely different purposes (case of Nouakchott where the project financed a cold storage facility that was intended for vegetables to be exported and is now being used to conserve fish). Satisfactory disbursement figures can be misleading as they may hide significant cost overruns, as was the case for irrigation rehabilitation under this project. In addition, APLs, by their very nature, have disbursement categories that are broad and not easy to match disbursements with quantitative achievement of physical outputs.

title: Africa - Emergency Locust Project
report_name: Africa - Emergency Locust Project
project_id: 3W-Africa Emergency Locust Project -- P092473
abstract: Ratings for the Emergency Locust Project (AELP) for Africa were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was low or negligible, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: regional projects with important environmental and safeguards aspects such as the AELP should be allocated sufficient implementation support funds to allow participation in each mission of experienced environmental and safeguards specialists from the Bank. Given the potential reputational risks associated with projects involving the use of chemicals that can have negative environmental, health, and social impacts, and the ensuing negative externalities at the regional level and costs associated with compensations, it is essential that due diligence of safeguards instruments be closely monitored with adequate budget through Bank staff instead of local consultants. In all frontline countries (Mauritania, Mali, Niger, and Chad), the project supported the creation of a national locust-control center whose services and maintenance are earmarked in the countries' national budget. Governments will need to disburse funds in a timely manner to fully guarantee sustainability of project achievements. The Bank's know-how with respect to Community-Driven Development (CDD) and social protection programs needs to be fully exploited in the design, implementation, and monitoring of community micro projects that seek to restore local livelihoods. This leveraging could have helped mitigate shortcomings related to the inadequate expertise of locust-control agents about key community development requirements and the absence of clear guidelines on compensation measures for victims.

title: Chad - Institutional Reform Support Credit (IRSC) Project
report_name: Chad - Institutional Reform Support Credit (IRSC) Project
project_id: TD-Chad: Institutional Reform Support Credit -- P083711
abstract: The outcome of the Institutional Reform Support Credit in Chad was unsatisfactory with a likely sustainability.  The institutional development impact was moderate with unsatisfactory performance by the Bank and highly unsatisfactory by the Borrower. Lessons learned from the IRSC experience and similar public finance reform programs indicate the importance of combining policy lending with comprehensive support in the areas of capacity-building and technical assistance. A second lesson concerns the program-budgeting process. Ideally, the preparation of program-budgets should be a two-phase process: 1) preparation of draft programs to help the government make appropriate decisions about overall budget priorities; and 2) sector ministries should review again their program-budgets with a view to optimizing the use of funds available to their sector. For the future, the budget preparation process should start with: (i) identification by the government of its overall strategic priorities and (ii) early issuance of the budget circular defining inter-sectoral allocations. A third lesson concerns the role of the civil society and the feasibility of supporting independent oversight institutions. The Bank should continue to give a high priority to building the capacity and protecting the independence of trade unions and other civil society representatives which, if successful, can help develop a culture of transparency in the management of public resources. A fourth lesson concerns the need to move more forcefully towards result-based indicators.

title: Chad - Agricultural Services and Producer Organizations Project
report_name: Chad - Agricultural Services and Producer Organizations Project
project_id: TD-Agricultural Services and Producer Organizations Project -- P074266
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Services and Producer Organizations Project (ASPOP) for Chad were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was high, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: ASPOP showed that the introduction of competitive funding mechanisms to support agricultural research can work even in a poor country such as Chad. The competitive funding mechanisms introduced under the project produced better research proposals and led to more careful screening of proposals for eventual financing based on broadly approved criteria that took into account the actual and current needs of the producers. The approach opened researchers' eyes to the demands of the marketplace and its priorities, as well as to means of identifying sources of funds for research. Researchers and research institutions came together to prepare joint proposals, engage in collaborative research, and participate in training programs and workshops. The collaborative review and monitoring process led to better results and papers, some of which were published. In a few cases, educational degrees were upgraded. Early efforts to publicize the project were very successful. Hundreds of rural communities were informed about the potential economic, social, and environmental benefits of the project and were led to believe that they stood a good chance of securing financing to implement subprojects. Later when it proved impossible to fund all of the subprojects that had been approved, frustration erupted among those who had prepared subprojects, especially when beneficiaries had already mobilized their own contribution and deposited it into a specially created bank account. This created a negative legacy that an eventual follow-on operation will have to overcome.

title: Cote D'Ivoire - Economic Recovery Credit Project
report_name: Cote D'Ivoire - Economic Recovery Credit Project
project_id: CI-Economic Recovery Credit -- P071375
abstract: The ratings for the project were as follows: the outcome was unsatisfactory, the sustainability was unlikely, the institutional development impact was negligible, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the borrower performance was unsatisfactory. The lessons learned indicate that the conflict that started three months after project approval essentially blocked policymaking and reform implementation and led to a substantial decline in population welfare. The government commitment to reforms is critical if policy actions are to be sustained but reform priorities can change significantly in the wake of a war situation. Country volatility was a risk clearly identified in the President's Report of the Economic Recovery Project (ERC) and the I-CAS and draft I-TSS: there was a recognition of a continuous risk that the national reconciliation process may stall or reverse. Selectivity and realism are essential when the reform agenda is vast and available capacity might be limited compared with the agenda, especially in volatile political situations. The design of a quick-disbursing credit in multiple tranches seems appropriate for countries which are moving slowly or taking only partial measures for their reforms, or when risk of non-implementation is high. It took a long time to convince the government of the need for the Bank's exit from this operation as country conditions effectively blocked project implementation. The ERC was just one of a broader Bank strategy and its collection of instruments for the pursuit of its dialogue with a country in a post-conflict situation. The Bank could possibly envisage a more modulated response in the design of specific projects in the context of volatile country situations.

title: Benin - Cotton Sector Reform Project
report_name: Benin - Cotton Sector Reform Project
project_id: BJ-Cotton Sector Reform Project -- P072503
abstract: Ratings for the Cotton Sector Reform Project for Benin were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: using investment lending in tandem with budget support to implement difficult reforms may be more effective than each instrument alone. The project under review experienced that reforms are extremely difficult to implement within a sector as strategic and as politically sensitive as cotton. Even if it is clearly stated initially, Government commitment can hardly be taken for granted on a long period of time, unless irreversible steps are taken at the beginning of the reform process, which was not the case in the Benin reform process. The privatization of National Company for agricultural production was finally completed under joint support of specific investment lending and budget support. Budget support can signal and facilitate commitment to reforms. Strong sector teams from the investment side are, however, needed to lead an effective policy dialogue. Institutional development and structural reforms should be outward looking and keep an eye on evolving global developments. The project under review and the underlying cotton sector reform were affected by decisions of key stakeholders driven by internal struggles and the political economy of the national cotton sector. Evidence based analysis highlighting the performance of alternative approaches followed in the region could have improved decision making. The Bank is well suited to provide such kind of input as shown by the recently completed comparative analysis of Organization and Performance of African Cotton Sectors (World Bank 2008). The project was further affected by developments on international commodity and financial markets. Designs need to be forward looking, anticipate such external effects and be flexible enough to adapt.

title: Chad - Fourth and Fifth Structural Adjustment Credit Projects (SAC IV & V) : Chad - Fifth Structural Adjustment Credit Project (SAC V)
report_name: Chad - Fourth and Fifth Structural Adjustment Credit Projects (SAC IV & V)
project_id: TD-SAC IV -- P052202,TD-Chad Fifth Structural Adjustment Credit -- P077781
abstract: Project ratings are as follows: Project outcome is satisfactory, sustainability is likely, institutional development is substantial, Bank performance is satisfactory, and Borrower performance is satisfactory. Three key lessons can be drawn, centering on 1) the importance of borrower empowerment, leadership, effective coordinating role and strong commitment to the Reform; 2) the importance of providing support through parallel capacity building operations in a context of weak capacities; and 3) the adequacy of the Structural Adjustment Credit instrument.

title: Mali - Agricultural Services and Producer Organizations Project
report_name: Mali - Agricultural Services and Producer Organizations Project
project_id: ML-AGRICULTURAL SERVICES AND PRODUCER ORGANIZATIONS PROJECT -- P035630
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Services and Producer Organizations Project for Mali were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: in financing ambitious, far reaching institutional reforms of ministerial departments for which political commitment is paramount, a clear distinction should be made between the preparation phase and the implementation phase. As part of the preparation phase there is need to pay due attention to political commitment and associated risks. As far as implementation is concerned, there is need for a clear road-map that details the operational arrangements and sequencing of reforms. If the Government and donors collaborate in this field, as was the case under this project, transformational achievements are possible. For agricultural services projects, special attention needs to be given to introduce and/or strengthen a permanent in house economic analysis capacity inside the research and extension organizations. This capacity building support should enable these organizations to carry out prospective impact studies of different specific research and extension activities that can guide their efforts towards the ones that are likely to have the highest economic returns and social payoff. The privatization of agricultural extension and the public funding for private agricultural service delivery based on contracts is a long term process and needs to be conceived as such. Including an emergency fund for natural calamities is sensible, as the risk of occurrence of such events is high, and as it allows International Development Association (IDA) to be of assistance immediately when the need is highest.

title: Mali - Third Structural Adjustment Credit Project
report_name: Mali - Third Structural Adjustment Credit Project
project_id: ML-SAC III -- P072785
abstract: The project outcome is rated satisfactory, sustainability likely, although the institutional development impact is negligible. Lessons are as follows. Policy-based lending has been an effective mechanism of support in Mali, but structural adjustment credits may not be the best mechanism for supporting public enterprise divestitures. Privatization is a complex process, particularly given the fragility of Mali's economy and high vulnerability to exogenous shocks, thereby forcing the Bank to either amend its conditionality, or penalize the government for good performance under difficult circumstances. The Bank supported a process that would not only be anti-competitive in outcome, but which was also likely to be markedly less attractive to potential investors; similarly, the Bank's acquiescence in the government's decision not to use an investment advisor, in a country fraught with capacity weaknesses, further weakened the likelihood of privatization success. This also suggests the need to ensure up-front that key implementing institutions have sufficient resources, since the shortfall of other donor funding had the potential to substantially undermine credit implementation. Instead, future operations should explicitly ensure that funding, technical assistance, etc. be reliably available; alternatively, provision of such funding should be included in the Bank's operation to make certain that it is forthcoming. Other lessons outline the need for maintaining a tight focus on a limited number of reforms; for an effective partnership with donors; and, for an effective partnership with the International Monetary Fund.

title: Ghana - Third Economic Reform Support Operation Credit Project
report_name: Ghana - Third Economic Reform Support Operation Credit Project
project_id: GH-ERSO III -- P050619
