abstract: Ratings convey satisfactory project outcomes, and a likely sustainability, in spite of negligible institutional development impacts. Implementation of the programs supported by the project, has been uneven: while progress has been achieved in improving macroeconomic stability, implementation of structural reforms was slower than expected; in areas such as public expenditure management, divestiture of state owned assets, energy and public sector, reforms proved difficult, especially given the international market conditions making the sale of enterprises impossible, or the domestic coalition in support of reforms, weakened. The recent experience points to three main lessons. First, Government commitment to reform is critical if policy actions are to be sustained. Recent reforms - such as the preparation of the Ghana Poverty Reduction Strategy - show that significant progress can be achieved, provided the authorities take the lead, and adopt a communication strategy in implementing the policy agenda. Second, the development of "ownership" may take longer than expected when a new Government comes to power, and a time-consuming consensus building needs to be attained to gain support of the civil society. Accordingly, Bank adjustment operations need to pro-actively, identify the potential for electoral-induced policy lapses; incorporate measures responsive to slippages, should these occur; and, ensure ownership of reforms by government, and civil society to improve the likelihood of policy implementation. Likewise, careful consideration needs to given as to what constitutes policy conditionality, as submission of legislation to Parliament does not necessarily translate into policy implementation. Third, selectivity, and realism are essential when the reform agenda is vast, and available capacity might be less strong than desirable. Overall, shortcomings of the traditional adjustment lending point to a different institutional environment, such as the one provided by programmatic lending under the envisaged series of Poverty Reduction Strategy Credits.

title: Burkina Faso - Health Services Reinforcement Project
report_name: NA
project_id: BF-Health Services Reinforcement Project -- P164696
abstract: Ratings of Health Services Reinforcement Project for Burkina Faso were as follows: outcome was moderately unsatisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: Tailoring Project Scope to Local Contexts especially in Fragility, Conflict and Violence Context; Early and continuous involvement of local authorities, communities, and other stakeholders played a pivotal role in the successful implementation of health and nutrition interventions; Improving quality and coverage of health services through strategic purchasing; and Strengthening investments in disease surveillance.

title: Benin, Mali Mauritania, Niger - Third Phase of Regional Disease Surveillance Systems Enhancement Project
report_name: NA
project_id: 3W-Regional Disease Surveillance Systems Enhancement (REDISSE) Phase III -- P161163
abstract: Ratings of Third Phase of Regional Disease Surveillance Systems Enhancement Project for Benin, Mali, Mauritania, and Niger were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: A regional approach is vitally important for health security; Operationalizing One Health requires a dedicated technical organization and a shift in institutional roles across countries; Using globally accepted indicators can help align projects with higher-level objectives, but it also poses challenges; Regional projects involving FCV countries need flexible designs and extra implementation support; Sustainability planning should begin early in the project and be finalized by the MTR; and Strengthening the supply of and demand for health security professionals across IHR core capacity requirements is essential to sustain the development of the regional, national, and sub-national ecosystem and to include more women and youth in this important field.

title: Mali - Livestock Sector Development Support Project
report_name: NA
project_id: ML-Mali Livestock Sector Development Support Project (PADEL-M) -- P160641
abstract: Ratings for the Livestock Sector Development Support Project for Mali were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: promoting productive partnerships between livestock SMEs and financial institutions and providing the needed liquidity for fostering transformational pathways that link pastoral herders to lucrative markets. Project-supported activities, such as technical assistance to livestock SMEs to formulate bankable investment proposals and to banks to provide suitably adapted financial products to livestock SMEs, led to a significant increase in the SMEs’ turnover. These SMEs were able to purchase large numbers of animals from pastoral systems, fatten them, and sell them in lucrative markets in Mali and the subregion. This approach can be adopted for similar countries in the Sahel that have similar production systems. Effective communication, transparency, continuous sensitization, and outreach campaigns are critical to the success of matching grant schemes. Some community members not selected for matching grants expressed dissatisfaction with the project, alleging exclusion. To avoid this, one needs clear criteria, robust communication tools, inclusive and transparent sensitization strategies, and outreach campaigns on how the criteria are administered for people to understand that they are not being excluded unfairly.

title: Mauritania - First and Second Fiscal Consolidation and Private Sector Support Development Policy Operations
report_name: Mauritania - First and Second Fiscal Consolidation and Private Sector Support Development Policy Operations
project_id: MR-Mauritania Second Fiscal Consolidation and Private Sector Support DPO -- P163057
abstract: Ratings of First and Second Fiscal Consolidation and Private Sector Support Development Policy Operations (DPO) were as follows: overall outcome was satisfactory, relevance of prior actions was satisfactory, and achievement of objectives was satisfactory.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) : A DPO can be used to help a country develop an adequate macroeconomic framework even in the absence of an IMF program; (ii) The use of the DPO instrument was critical in moving reforms forward that otherwise might have stalled; (iii) A technical assistance (TA) operation is an essential complement to a DPO to ensure follow through and, therefore, ensure implementation of reforms on the ground level, especially in a low capacity country like Mauritania; (iv) To achieve greater and faster impact of reforms, it is advantageous to have an actual activity/investment ready to go to put the reform into action; (v) Economic and financial constraints during or after a terms of trade (ToT) shock add pressure on governments to rapidly conduct reforms that otherwise would have taken longer to implement; (vi) Having strong ownership from the government and a champion within government of Mauritania (GoM) offered an excellent opportunity to achieve structural reforms and shifts in attitude, particularly during a time of high fiscal pressure; and (vii)  Support to monitoring and evaluation is a long-term effort, particularly in a low capacity country.

title: Burkina Faso - Livestock Sector Development Support Project
report_name: NA
project_id: BF-Burkina Faso Livestock Sector Development Support Project -- P159476
abstract: Ratings for the Livestock Sector Development Support Project for Burkina Faso were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: when defining indicators for livestock herd improvement programs, it is important to critically account for all the elements along the entire chain to adequately calibrate the time needed for successful implementation. Herd improvement programs, especially when starting from a very low infrastructural and human capacity base, require a lot of pre-requisites, including establishing the appropriate physical and logistical infrastructure (adequately equipping multiplication centers and decentralized handling facilities, acquiring high performance breeding stock and appropriate climate-controlled transportation mechanisms, etc.), and training staff at all levels. The gestation period of animals needs to be fully accounted for as well. All this helps in setting realistic expectations of the levels of productivity achievable during the project’s implementation period. It is important to address issues as they arise instead of waiting for mid-term review. The case of the indicators that needed adjustments to sharpen attribution or set realistic expectations is instructive. Waiting for mid-term review to act can create missed opportunities for timely intervention. Constantly recalibrating aspects of the project as and when the need for corrective measures is detected is highly recommended. Like Productive Alliances, innovation platforms should be initiated as early as possible in the project cycle. They require extensive consultation and ownership for their successful establishment. In this case, because of the lengthy consultation process, they were established towards the project’s closing, thus limiting their contribution to project outcomes within the project’s implementation period.

title: Western and Central Africa - Second Phase of the Regional Disease Surveillance Systems Enhancement Project
country: Central Africa,Western Africa,Western and Central Africa
report_name: NA
project_id: 3W-Regional Disease Surveillance Systems Enhancement (REDISSE) Phase II -- P159040
abstract: Ratings for the Second Phase of the Regional Disease Surveillance Systems Enhancement Project for Western and Central Africa were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the Ebola epidemic showed the importance of cross-country and regional collaboration to prepare and respond to health emergencies, as pathogens know no borders. In fact, the REDISSE program was designed based on the lessons learned from that and other outbreaks. In fact, the REDISSE program was designed based on the lessons learned from that and other outbreaks. A common approach and common (minimum) standards to prevent, detect and respond to health emergencies make the health security of the countries in the region, stronger. The COVID-19 pandemic proved the essential role of a regional approach to tackle health emergencies. Under REDISSE, a regional approach defined standards and policies for prevention, preparedness, and response to health emergencies, many of which supported a cohesive response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The cohesive and collaborative approach to the response to COVID-19 helped countries implement best approaches and follow minimum standards. WAHO made the development of these standards and policies a participative effort, which contributed to a greater buy-in and adoption of the standardized approaches. Regional approaches in health security also contribute to common agreements on how countries will work together and support each other during an emergency, whether it affects one or multiple countries.

title: Cameroon - Livestock Development Project
report_name: NA
project_id: CM-Livestock Development Project -- P154908
abstract: Ratings for the Livestock Development Project for Cameroon were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: flexibility when implementing complex and novel programs is essential. Flexibility when implementing complex and novel programs is essential. The trade-off between implementing more complex and novel designs with implementation capacity needs to be carefully considered.

title: Mauritania - Nouadhibou Eco-Seafood Cluster Project
report_name: Mauritania - Nouadhibou Eco-Seafood Cluster Project
project_id: MR-Nouadhibou Eco-Seafood Cluster Project -- P151058
abstract: Ratings for the Nouadhibou Eco-Seafood Cluster Project for Mauritania were as follows: outcome was moderately unsatisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: projects should carefully weigh the pros and cons of anchoring projects outside of central government agencies for three reasons: experience, political economy, and location. Take into account that in some countries, institutional processes and relationships are subordinated to personal relationships. Private sector actors need meaningful engagement and understanding of their priorities. Adding a component weakly linked to project goals can increase project complexity and affect performance. Allow sufficient time for implementing new activities. The World Bank should devote sufficient attention to M and E.

title: Western and Central Africa - Regional Sahel Pastoralism Support Project
report_name: Western and Central Africa - Regional Sahel Pastoralism Support Project
project_id: 3W-Regional Sahel Pastoralism Support Project -- P147674
abstract: Ratings for the Regional Sahel Pastoralism Support Project for Western and Central Africa were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: since pastoral activities cut across countries in similar agro-climatic zones, addressing the regional dimensions of pastoralism is key; the division of responsibilities between the regional and the national level should adhere to the strict subsidiarity principle. Adequate financial arrangements, organized at regional and national level under the subsidiarity approach, are an important factor of efficiency and accountability of regional programs. Social engagement is a critical action in fostering ownership, mitigating conflicts and ensuring adequate delivery of services and implementation of infrastructures attuned to the needs of beneficiaries. Strengthening inclusion to get at the root-cause of conflicts should remain embedded in all projects targeting pastoralists; if this is not possible, projects should seek to work in tandem with other programs specifically addressing the problem. Diversification out of pastoral livestock activities to provide alternative livelihoods will become increasingly important in a context of growing risks and uncertainty faced by pastoralists. Adopting more flexible operational approaches, in contexts of insecurity, like the one prevailing in the Sahel, is a necessity in a fast-evolving environment.

title: Guinea-Bissau - Second APL (APL-B1) West Africa Regional Fisheries Program
report_name: Guinea-Bissau - Second APL (APL-B1) West Africa Regional Fisheries Program
project_id: 3W-GEF - Africa - 2nd phase fish. (GBissau) -- P119380
abstract: Ratings for the Second APL (APL-B1) West Africa Regional Fisheries Program Project for Guinea-Bissau were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monetary and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the World Bank played an important role in bringing the different enforcement agencies to agree on their respective roles and pull together resources, contributing to the increased surveillance effort and reduced illegal fishing. A similar role was fulfilled by the World Bank in Liberia and Sierra Leone where memoranda of understanding formed inter-agency surveillance entities (the Liberia Monitoring, Control and Surveillance Coordinating Committee and the Sierra Leone Joint Maritime Committee), resulting in improved enforcement in these countries. Reforming the fisheries sector to generate lasting benefits requires an incremental approach and commitment over time. As experience from the project showed, carrying out sectoral reforms and affecting behavior changes to control resource use and reduce illegal actions in a context of limited government budgets and capacities requires considerable allocations of time and capacity building actions and gradual advancement of reform measures. In such cases, APL/Series of Projects is a useful instrument as it allows a phased and flexible order of interventions, as well as the addition of more countries as they become ready to join the process. While the World Bank has no control of external events, maintaining dialogue with the government is essential for long term reforms. In all cases, each program phase should consider the following principles: (i) the design should be incremental and realistic based on a thorough assessment of countries’ capacities; and (ii) adequate resources should be allocated to ensure strong technical assistance to counterparts undergoing policy reform. Second phase projects are being strategically focused around countries’ priority investments with emphasis on improving fisheries management in areas where basic capacity has already been built. In parallel, CSRP will be recruiting additional technical and operational experts to support the projects.

title: Mali - Fostering Agricultural Productivity Project
report_name: Mali - Fostering Agricultural Productivity Project
project_id: ML-Mali-Fostering Agricultural Productivity -- P095091
abstract: Ratings of Fostering Agricultural Productivity Project for Mali were as follows: outcome was moderately unsatisfactory, Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) It is important to design the monitoring and evaluation system prior to project appraisal; (ii) More focused interventions are needed in complex implementation settings like Mali; (iii) Successful implementation through country systems requires a proper understanding of government systems, their weaknesses and capacity strengthening needs; (iv) Similarly, transitions from project to programmatic country approaches need to be supported only after assessing the feasibility and capacity to deliver and after securing the public stakeholders’ buy-in; (v) Well-designed synergies between implementation actors improve project delivery and build sustainability; (vi) Systemic risks (social, political and economic) need to be better integrated in design choices and adequate adaptation measures have to be developed; (vii) More crowding in of private sector in service provision is needed in Mali; and (viii) Projet d'Accroissement de la Productivité Agricole au Mal (PAPAM) is one of several projects in Mali that have used matching grants to finance microprojects.

title: Africa - Senegal River Basin Multi-purpose Water Resources Development Project
report_name: Africa - Senegal River Basin Multi-purpose Water Resources Development Project
project_id: 3W-Senegal River Basin Multi-purpose Water Resources Development Project -- P093826
abstract: Ratings for the Senegal River Basin Multi-purpose Water Resources Development Project for Africa were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the Bank should consider the advantages of a multi-sector project and or a regional project when dealing with issues that are closely related and or trans-boundary. In future phases the requirements on member states to pay contributions will need to be increased, for example through dated legal covenants, and linking payments to clearly defined activities. Costing of activities should be based on actual contracts, wherever possible, or on more detailed market reviews even if it leads to taking studies out of the project to be funded from other sources. Close collaboration between the project executing agencies and with the beneficiaries can mitigate the negative effects of national civil strife on project implementation. Introduction of a more nuanced selection process among potential project beneficiaries is good practice, however a capacity requirement may conflict with the focus of the project on the poorest communities in the basin. Increased capacity building for beneficiaries before and after handing over assets is recommended. Rapid increase of production may lead to bottlenecks unless the whole value chain is considered, including processing, storage, and marketing. An appropriate value chain should be analyzed as part of project preparation.

title: Nigeria - Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise Project
report_name: Nigeria - Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise Project
project_id: NG-Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise Project -- P083082
abstract: Ratings for the Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise Project for Nigeria were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome is moderate; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory; and borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: results framework should be designed to measure direct impact of the project. This includes ensuring that monitoring and impact indicators directly correspond to the project objective and are easily measurable. This is essential not only to assessing whether project objectives have been achieved, but also to ensuring that the targets are practically achievable and within the control of the project management. In case of significant project delays, which may affect implementation of original project design, a review of planned project activities should be conducted to confirm that they can be practically implemented. As a result of long delays, operational and political circumstances may change. Implementing entities should include key staff with required qualification and experience with World Bank procedures from the very start of the project. As there was no suitable entity within the government, and based on previous experience with government run projects in Nigeria, a private entity was tasked with project management. The Project Management Unit (PMU) also employed national staff in some key positions thus building local capacity, while the Project Implementation Unit (PIU) was staffed with civil servants. As a result, it is likely that the local staff capacity to implement complex projects was increased, and should provide a significant human resource for future projects. While the engagement of a private sector operator may have increased overall implementation capacity in a complex implementation environment, the private PMU has also experienced initial challenges with applying World Bank procurement guidelines. All core project management functions should be within a single implementing entity. Implementation arrangements envisaged both the private PMU and government led PIU. While most of the project management functions were with the PMU, including procurement, the responsibility for financial management and disbursements and compliance with World Bank rules in these areas rested with the PIU. Though it was a pilot project, which allowed for a more flexible design and testing of new approaches, the breadth of the operation may have been too ambitious. This is especially true with regard to numerous activities aimed at reforming the investment climate which required wide institutional support which could not be fully secured.

title: Sierra Leone - Third Productivity and Transparency Support Grant
report_name: Sierra Leone - Third Productivity and Transparency Support Grant
project_id: SL-Third Productivity and Transparency Support Grant -- P169498
abstract: Ratings for the Third Productivity and Transparency Support Grant Project for Sierra Leone were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the achievement of objectives was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: a key ingredient to successful participation of the private sector is the government’s conducive mindset and its willingness to vacate a space, as was demonstrated in seeds supply. It was also important to ensure the cooperation of the Ministry of Trade so that the private sector had what it needed to be able to import and supply seeds to farmers. It was also important that the government not vacate that role until the private sector was able to assume it. The success was evidenced by the number of approved private suppliers of seeds which substantially exceeded the target. There is need to strengthen institutional capacity to implement measures to attract more capital-intensive private investment in fertilizer production, distribution, and marketing. Moving from good plans and strategies to implementation may be difficult if the government’s ability to implement (to be contrasted with commitment) is overestimated, as was the case in the electricity sector. The success of the reform was thwarted by the government’s inability to install bulk meters in a timely way. The government’s commitment was good, but that did not translate into implementation capacity. Related to this shortcoming was that the WBG-supported IPF experienced delayed procurement of bulk meters. This also underlines the importance of close coordination with sector colleagues in the WBG, not just on policy reforms but also on input timelines. Asset declaration requirements can be made more effective if they apply strictly to officials who are in a position that deals with financial transactions. The series narrowed down what had been a blanket requirement for all government officials to declare their assets. This requirement was ineffective because it was not feasible administratively to enforce compliance with so many people, most of whom were not in financially sensitive positions. By focusing on only the most relevant officials, compliance with asset declaration increased substantially.

title: Western and Central Africa - First Phase of the West Africa Region Fisheries Program
report_name: NA
project_id: 3W-West Africa Region Fisheries Program AF Guinea, Sierra Leone & Liberia -- P126773
abstract: Ratings for the First Phase of the West Africa Region Fisheries Program for Western and Central Africa were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: fisheries management reforms have a medium to long-term horizon and as such, the project was too ambitious in some instances, notably on controlling capacity and access in the small-scale segment. Establishing strong Community Management Association (CMAs) is essential for any long-term progress in the artisanal segment. Advances in transparency of information and fisheries management provides an authorizing environment for further partnerships for the countries. Performance-based management linked to disbursements can provide meaningful incentives to encourage swift and effective implementation, but their application must be appropriately designed and programmed.

title: Ghana - First and Second Agriculture Development Policy Operations Project
report_name: Ghana - First and Second Agriculture Development Policy Operations Project
project_id: GH-Ghana Agriculture DPL -- P102675,GH-Ghana Second Agriculture Development Policy Operation -- P110147
abstract: Ratings for the First and Second Agriculture Development Policy Operations Project for Ghana were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate; Bank performance was satisfactory and borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: sector development policy lending for the agriculture sector has proven to be an effective, flexible instrument that empowered the Government of Ghana to implement a country-owned policy and the related programs. An important lesson for future operations is to ensure that the policy agenda underlying the operation is complementary to the Bank's and other development partners' investment lending operations, thus creating synergies that enhance the policy making process as well as program implementation. The design of Agriculture Development Policy Operation (AgDPO) policy matrices and the tracking of prior actions are major undertakings that need to be better integrated in Ministry of Food and Agriculture's (MoFA's) planning process. Coordination between the World Bank's and Canadian International Development Agency's (CIDA's) budget support programs prevented duplication and contributed to sector planning and coordination.

title: Implementation Completion and Results Report (ICR) Document - Local Forest Communities Support Project - P149434
report_name: NA
project_id: BF-Local Forest Communities Support Project -- P149434
abstract: NA

title: Ghana - Natural Resources and Environmental Governance Technical Assistance Project
report_name: Ghana - Natural Resources and Environmental Governance Technical Assistance Project
project_id: GH-Natural Resources and Environmental Governance Technical Assistance -- P129769
abstract: Ratings of Natural Resources and Environmental Governance Technical Assistance Project for Ghana were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: (i) Staying engaged even during unfavorable fiscal situation provided benefits such as continuation of the important NREG dialog, as well as preparing the operational framework for future engagements; (ii) The multisectoral approach adopted by the project proved to be an effective means of managing natural resources; and (iii) The use of existing institutional structures for project implementation instead of creating a separate Project Management Unit is the way forward when implementing institutional capacity-building projects toward a common goal.

title: Benin - Forest and Adjacent Land Management Project
report_name: Benin - Forest and Adjacent Land Management Project
project_id: BJ-Forests and Adjacent Lands Management Project -- P069896,BJ-BJ-Forest & Adjacent Land Mgmt Addit Fin -- P132431,BJ-Benin AF Forest and Adjacent Land Management -- P131051
abstract: Ratings for Forest and Adjacent Land Management Project for Benin were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) An implementation period of five years would have been too short to see many of the positive changes made under the project; (ii) Participatory methods, consultative processes and systems based on traditional models (e.g., GRM), take time, but yield high returns with regard to building strong, respectful working relationships between communities and forest agents, more effectively implemented forest management plans, and a strong enabling environment for collective integrated ecosystem management; (iii) Targeted, rather than broad reaching, interventions are the most effective; (iv) Income generating activities are a strong tool for increasing access to income, empowering women, and reducing pressure on forests, but technical support is essential for effective implementation; (v) Current levels of return and the Forest Management Funds and other budgetary allocations do not supply sufficient funds for effective management; (vi) Population pressure and search for healthy soil for agriculture within the GFs is the most significant pressure on the gazetted forests and will not abate in the future due to continued pressures linked to population growth and lack of alternative employment; and (vii) Private sector interventions have shown strong returns with regard to providing needed fuelwood and timber and economic interventions in the fuelwood market have been an important first step in rationalizing the fuelwood markets.

title: Congo - Forest and Economic Diversification Project
report_name: NA
project_id: CG-Congo: Forest and Econ. Diversification Project -- P124085
abstract: Ratings for the Forest and Economic Diversification Project for Congo were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: in a complex sector reform project, apply an incremental approach to adjust the level of ambition to match a changing project context. Working with local champions helps achieve operational effectiveness and efficiency, particularly in remote locations where government capacity is weak. Investments in technology are necessary but not sufficient when improving government efficiency; also, staff incentives and organizational culture need to be addressed. Well-planned local community engagement is essential for achieving results.

title: Nigeria - Erosion and Watershed Management Project
report_name: NA
project_id: NG-Nigeria Erosion  and Watershed Management Project -- P124905
abstract: Ratings for the Erosion and Watershed Management Project for Nigeria (NEWMAP) were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: use an integrated watershed (or sub-watershed) approach to achieve meaningful and sustainable results and outcomes in projects aimed at addressing land degradation and erosion control objectives. Address key project design and implementation factors to achieve satisfactory results. Detail interventions to support communities, mainly through financing of improved livelihood subprojects to community interest group (CIGs), as this is an essential part of an integrated landscape approach - provided the support is focused on addressing the causes or consequences of the degradation process being resolved through the civil works. Focus site selection criteria to not only prioritize degraded sites where current socioeconomic impacts are most significant (generally urban areas), as in the case of NEWMAP, but also to select sites to prevent erosion processes from creating a pipeline of downstream degraded areas which will need major investments in the near future. Create a comprehensive gender strategy identifying relevant gender gaps, actions, and targets during project preparation, are needed for complex projects such as NEWMAP. Use an effective monitoring system to track physical and financial progress of a complex, decentralized project such as NEWMAP - in addition to tracking results framework indicators. Integrate remote project supervision using cost-effective innovative technology, such as 360-degree cameras, drones, and satellite imagery) with physical monitoring and supervision procedures throughout the life of the project, so as to supplement - not substitute - regular physical inspection of project implementation, as was learnt from the restrictions imposed by the Coronavirus disease (COVID)-lockdowns. Have regular virtual monthly check-ins organized by the Bank team with State Project Management Unit (SPMUs) and Bank management. In NEWMAP, this was done during final year of implementation and was a best practice that proved instrumental to completion. Allocate adequate resources to develop extensive documentation related to the before project situation on a systematic manner, including not only imagery of degraded areas and data on specific impacts, but also testimonies of community members affected by the consequences of such degradation, for effective communication, awareness and dissemination as project results.

title: Central African Republic - Third Economic Management and Governance Reform Grant Project
report_name: Central African Republic - Third Economic Management and Governance Reform Grant Project
project_id: CF-Economic Management and Governance Reform Grant III -- P120534
abstract: Ratings for the Third Economic Management and Governance Reform Grant Project for the Central African Republic (CAR) were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: a development policy operation can be an effective instrument for addressing key issues in restoring a functional government and contribute to setting the stage for deeper reforms. A programmatic series of DPOs may be better than stand-alone Development Policy Operations (DPOs), which may provide a timely response to exogenous shocks but could lead to inappropriate sequencing of the reforms over the medium term. Going forward the new DPO series should seek to link more strongly the public sector and economic governance reforms with measures to promote private sector development. A two-tranche budget support operation can be effective in supporting the continuation of reforms, especially in fragile countries. The project, a two-tranche grant disbursing on agreed conditions, helped maintain the focus on critical reforms and ensured that the Bank remain engaged in policy dialogue with the authorities before and after the elections. The second tranche helped support the government's efforts to restore fiscal discipline following election-related slippages. Its disbursement helped clear arrears which, in turn, permitted other donors including the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the African Development Bank to move forward with their support program. In a fragile post-conflict country such as the CAR, Technical Assistance (TA) should be available to help implement the reform program supported by the DPO and build up capacity in key areas. TA should promote synergies and built on them. The policy measures under the project that were most successfully implemented were those that benefited from greater cooperation among the departments and units concerned. To help build robust budget processes, reforms should be closely monitored and a close dialogue with the government should be maintained through frequent supervision missions and a strong field presence.

title: Senegal - Second Sustainable and Participatory Energy Management Project
report_name: Senegal - Second Sustainable and Participatory Energy Management Project
project_id: SN-Second Sustainable and Participatory Energy Management (PROGEDE II) -- P120629
abstract: The outcome of the Second Sustainable and Participatory Energy Management (PROGEDE II) Project for Senegal was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation was substantial. Some lessons learned during the project include: 1. A three-dimensional approach simultaneously addressing supply, demand and institutional sides of the sustainable community driven development, has proven to be most productive. 2. As far as the legal interventions are concerned, it is key for the success of the project to initially assign a national champion responsible for moving the legislative reform forward and where possible to keep the core of the staff throughout the legislative process. 3. Having two profile Ministries responsible for the project implementation has helped to achieve a necessary synergy in addressing the challenges in both sectors. 4. As two PROGEDE projects had been consistently and significantly overperforming on a similar set of targets (with some of those targets being achieved almost halfway through the project implementation), it might be necessary to reconsider the approaches to designing a results framework in the next operations and come up with more ambitious targets. 5. To keep the project results sustainable, it might be practical to increase the geographical scope of the next PROGEDE operation so that it covers the entire territory of the country. 6. Absorption capacity remains a challenge for financial interventions at the local level in PROGEDE projects. 7. While designing a project with a significant institutional part, it is important to make sure that government agencies both at the central and local levels are adequately staffed and financed to be able to continue supporting policy interventions achieved during the implementation of the World Bank’s project. 8. When designing participatory community-based sustainable forest management projects covering large areas and hundreds of villages, an implementation period of at least seven years should be considered to fully realize the institutional changes enabling supply demand shifts. 9. The empowerment of local communities carried along with the development of their sense of ownership for the management of the local natural resources, were crucial for the success of the project and a powerful tool against illegal logging. 10.  Introducing new clean energy technologies shall be combined with enhancing demand by ensuring the presence of the related products in the market supported by the efficient marketing strategy and proper financial solutions for consumers. 11. In a community driven project, it is instrumental to have a field-based task team leader.

title: Ghana - First, Second, and Third Development Policy Operations for the Natural Resources and Environmental Governance Project
report_name: Ghana - First, Second, and Third Development Policy Operations for the Natural Resources and Environmental Governance Project
project_id: GH-Natural Resources and Environmental Governance Project -- P102971,GH-Ghana Natural Resource and Environmental Governance - DPO -- P118188,GH-Ghana Natural Resource and Environmental Governance - DPO -- P113172
abstract: Ratings for the First, Second, and Third Development Policy Operations for the Natural Resources and Environmental Governance Project for Ghana were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the transition from financing individual projects to sector budget support (SBS) rejuvenated ownership, and revolutionized multi-agency dialogue. Although difficult to quantify, the adoption of an SBS program based around a government-led target-setting process resulted in a much greater sense of ownership, empowerment and enthusiasm amongst the implementing Ministries, Departments and Agencies (MDA) than previous, discrete project investments. It also engendered a far more active dialogue between the sector MDAs, and more substantive engagement with both Ministry of Finance and Economic Planning (MoFEP) and National Development Planning Commission (NDPC). Particularly in the case of the environment, this engagement was considered crucial to improving the understanding of environmental and climate change issues, and to mainstreaming environment through e.g. development of Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) tools for government planning. It has also allowed effective collaboration on financial performance, particularly in regard to the work of the Revenue Management Task Force in the mineral sector. NREG was worked in two parallel arenas, policy reform (the primary focus of Development Policy Operation, or DPOs) and strengthening sector management systems (the primary focus of SBS). These should be complementary, but delivery through a single instrument may have complicated both. The SBS modality emphasized broad engagement in sector activities rather than focusing on select policy issues, whereas use of a World Bank DPO template as the key program document encouraged the use of the PAF as a primary planning tool and de-emphasized specific investments in sector systems, such as monitoring and evaluation (M&E) and technical assistance (TA). A broad sector program, addressing challenges in institutional capacity as well as policy reform, may be better supported by a DPO in conjunction with other types of instruments. In practice, sector DPOs, such as the forestry and environment DPO in Cameroon, are often supported by an additional TA facility. Structured TA support will have been especially important in NREG as the initial analytical work established a policy agenda, but not the detailed reforms needed to pursue it.

title: Ghana - Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Ghana - Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: GH-Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P095730,GH-Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P103631,GH-Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credit -- P099287
abstract: Ratings for the Fourth and Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC) Project for Ghana were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Ratings for the Sixth PRSC Project were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some key lessons learned included: while there were instances that the PRSC enabled the Government to pursue reform policies that will otherwise be unaffordable for the country, such as the extension of capitation grants and the expansion of the national health insurance scheme, there were other instances where the PRSC were less successful. Energy sector reforms are a case in point. Notwithstanding the Multi-Donor Budget Support (MDBS) efforts, the Government implemented these reforms when it found fitting to do so. As a result, increases in electricity tariffs were gazette but only implemented belatedly and when every other source of funding for subsidizing consumers had been exhausted. This mix record reflects the fact that PRSC are very complex instruments, with many dimensions that need to be considered jointly (e.g., the fungibility of funds, the centrality of the policy dialogue, the nature of conditionalities, donor alignment and harmonization). More broadly, however, the progress in maintaining prudent fiscal management was not sustained once the Ghanaian government no longer had an International Monetary Fund (IMF)-sponsored program.

title: Cameroon - Forest and Environment Development Program Project  : Cameroon - Forest and Environment Development Program Project
report_name: Cameroon - Forest and Environment Development Program Project
project_id: CM-Cameroon Forest & Environment Sector Program (FESP) -- P073020,CM-FOREST AND ENVIRONMENT DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM -- P070656
abstract: Ratings for the Forest and Environment Development Program Project for Cameroon were as follows: outcomes were unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome and risk to Global Environment Objective (GEO) outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: several key personnel involved in the preparation of the Development Program Operation (DPO) were reassigned during implementation. A system ensuring swift training as well as motivation of incoming staff should be embedded in project design to avoid institutional memory loss and to facilitate ownership and continuity of skills. Bank management and task teams should ensure that actions and recommendations in aide-memoires do not contradict with policy dialogue conducted by management and senior management. The message is a key to maintain the Bank's credibility with the client and maximizing the chance that the policy dialogue will produce positive outcome. This has important implications for Bank participation in joint donor supervision missions of sector wide programs, where the Bank does not have sole control over the content of aide-memoires.

title: Gabonese - Natural Resources Management Development Policy Loan (NRM-DPL) Project
report_name: Gabonese - Natural Resources Management Development Policy Loan (NRM-DPL) Project
project_id: GA-Gabon Natural Resources Management Development Policy Loan -- P070196
abstract: Ratings for the Natural Resources Management Development Policy Loan (NRM-DPL) Project for Gabonese were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the NRM-DPL operation is one of the few DPLs where Sectoral Investment Lending (SIL) related elements were maintained. While in the context of this operation, it turned out to be a relevant solution, as indicated by the Borrower-, it has generated important delays and confusions. It is thus not recommended to replicate such 'hybrid' instrument in other operations. Governmental reform programs are broad by definition. The design phase of a development policy operation aims at targeting a set of reforms within the overall reform program of the Government in a given sector. It is very important that both parties share the same understanding of the reforms to be implemented under the policy lending as well as the associated prior actions and triggers: indicators need to be well-specified, agreed up-front with the Borrower, and closely monitored by both the Borrower and the Bank during implementation (the policy matrix proved to be a useful tool). Any change during implementation needs to be jointly discussed and agreed upon. When other critical issues emerge during the course of the operation, those should not be included as new prior actions or triggers (as was the case for the Forest Fund in the NRM-DPL operation) but should rather be part of the broader policy dialogue between the Bank and the Government.

title: Benin - Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Benin - Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: BJ-Poverty Reduction Strategy Credit - 1st PRSC -- P072003
abstract: The Benin Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project had a satisfactory outcome, likely sustainability, modest institutional development impact, and satisfactory performances by both Bank and Borrower. The following are findings and implications for subsequent operations: 1) The work under a prior operation in developing government experience in results-budgeting was highly instrumental in successfully implementing the project. Nevertheless, monitoring and evaluation capacity in most ministries was still fragile and limited. Team dialogue with Government should devote greater attention to the result-framework and indicators, and to related capacity building activities. The main stakeholders' understanding of the Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC) process and requirements was fragile and required improvements over time. Greater team efforts to expand stakeholder understanding and ownership would have improved the environment for subsequent operations. Donor coordination mechanisms needed to be enhanced so as to lay the ground for greater harmonization of procedures. Such harmonization was essential both to simplify messages for Government but also to enhance the efficiency of reform programs and of donor support. Starting with three sectors was an ambitious beginning, and for the included themes the policy matrix was quite long and detailed. It would be better for the team and Government to work to simplify the matrix.

title: Guinea - Integrated Agricultural Development Project
report_name: NA
project_id: GN-Guinea Integrated Agricultural Development Project (PDAIG) -- P164326
abstract: Ratings for the Integrated Agricultural Development Project for Guinea were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: in a low-capacity context, building on existing projects’ operational framework is key to success rather than establishing a new Project Coordination and Implementation Unit (PCIU). It is critical to build the country’s preparedness for food insecurity and crises. Technical assistance that is critical for the project’s implementation needs to be recruited very early in the project cycle. Overall capacity for irrigation development needs to be critically assessed during project appraisal for projects with irrigation development activities. Innovations, such as revolving funds for the integrated maize program can amplify the impact of input support programs.

title: Western and Central Africa - Sahel Irrigation Initiative Support Project
report_name: NA
project_id: 3W-Sahel Irrigation Initiative Support Project -- P154482
abstract: Ratings of the Sahel Irrigation Initiative Support Project for the Sahel were as follows: Outcome was moderately satisfactory; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory; M&E quality was substantial. A regional approach anchored in capable institutions can mitigate fragility, conflict, and violence risks and generate durable public goods when its added value is explicitly defined and operationalized; future programs should clearly specify the regional public goods to be delivered, secure hosting and financing arrangements for knowledge platforms, and establish incentives and mechanisms that ensure country teams adopt and apply regional outputs. Differentiated irrigation typologies and strong integration between agriculture and water management are essential to control costs, improve performance, and ensure sustainability; projects should tailor cost ceilings, implementation arrangements, and risk management to each typology, treat agriculture–water integration as a core design element, and systematically document and compare cost and performance data across typologies to support adaptive portfolio management. Design realism, early implementation readiness, and clear co-financing arrangements are critical to efficiency in multi-country operations; teams should apply structured readiness checklists for procurement and implementation before effectiveness, define coordination mechanisms and contingency protocols with co-financiers based on clear milestones, and incorporate flexibility into results frameworks while maintaining a disciplined focus on a limited number of outcome-level indicators. These lessons point to practical recommendations that strengthen resilience, accelerate learning, and improve delivery in complex regional irrigation programs.

title: Guinea - Agriculture Sector Support Project
report_name: Guinea - Agriculture Sector Support Project
project_id: GN-Guinea Agricultural Support Project -- P148114
abstract: The development objective of the Agriculture Sector Support Project for Guinea is to strengthen the capacity of selected sector institutions to support the effective implementation of the recipient's national agriculture and food security investment plan (PNIASA). The project comprises of two components. The first component is strengthening of sector management capacities. The second component is analytical support to PNIASA implementation. The project facilitated the preparation of sub-programs based on analytical studies and three master plans that allowed two sectoral ministries (agriculture and livestock) to have key investment plans to inform the design of new operations or negotiate future operations.

title: Nigeria - Transforming Irrigation Management in Nigeria Project
report_name: NA
project_id: NG-TRANSFORMING IRRIGATION MANAGEMENT IN NIGERIA PROJECT -- P123112
abstract: Ratings of the Transforming Irrigation Management in Nigeria Project for Nigeria were as follows: Outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank Performance was satisfactory, and M&E Quality was substantial. Key lessons learned include that farmer led irrigation development (FLID) can effectively mobilize private on-farm investment when reliable water sources are assured, but future operations should systematically study, integrate, and scale FLID, explicitly accounting for its water demand within public scheme design to safeguard water productivity and system performance; prototype facility designs, such as for Farmers Management and Service Delivery Centers, must be adapted to local geotechnical and adverse weather conditions and supported by stronger supervision, client ownership, and accountability to avoid delays and quality issues; the absence of unified national dam safety standards led to inconsistent designs and approval delays, underscoring the need to transition to a programmatic, risk-based dam safety regime that institutionalizes regular inspections, emergency preparedness, and dedicated funding for remedial works; deeper engagement of State Governments is essential given their proximity to Water User Associations (WUAs) and experience with FLID, and future projects should formalize state roles in supporting and strengthening farmer organizations and ensuring continuity of reforms; scaling up the three-tier farmer institution model composed of Water User Groups, WUAs, and Apex WUAs requires an enabling legal framework to secure financial and managerial autonomy and to institutionalize participatory irrigation management, with advocacy for approval of WUA law and clear funds flow arrangements; demand-driven, applied research linked to farmer needs accelerates adoption, so future operations should prioritize practical innovations such as System of Rice Intensification and Typha management alongside coordinated training, demonstrations, and coaching; and operating in fragile and conflict-affected settings requires security-responsive project design that integrates upfront site-specific risk assessments, adaptive planning, and clear safety protocols, including the option to drop high-risk areas and the provision of protective measures for contractors and communities to maintain continuity of implementation.

title: Senegal - Sustainable and Inclusive Agribusiness Development Project
report_name: Senegal - Sustainable and Inclusive Agribusiness Development Project
project_id: SN-SENEGAL - Sustainable and Inclusive Agribusiness Development Project -- P124018
abstract: Ratings for the Sustainable and Inclusive Agribusiness Development Project for Senegal were as follows: overall outcomes were moderately satisfactory, bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: a systematic registration approach that includes an extensive awareness campaign is critical in stimulating demand for certification of land user rights. It renders field work more efficient, land demarcation more contiguous and the process more inclusive, contrary to sporadic, on-demand approaches which are likely to result in high costs of land document issuance. They are also likely to limit access to the wealthier or more influential members of the community. With a systematic approach, adjudication is done locally and transparently with the involvement of neighbors and local authorities. The project implementation team was able to adjust to unforeseen challenges and catch up with implementation delays through a comprehensive restructuring. This consisted of: (i) identifying and strengthening areas where traction was possible, clarifying and strengthening oversight by responsible agencies, and keeping beneficiaries interested by pairing faster gestation period activities (such as Matching Grants) with long gestation period investments (such development of primary and secondary irrigation infrastructure).

title: Cameroon - Agricultural Competitiveness Project
report_name: Cameroon - Agricultural Competitiveness Project
project_id: CM-CM-Agricultural Competitiveness Project -- P112635
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Competitiveness Project for Cameroon were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: assessment of the availability and sustainability of service providers and key stakeholders.success factors of institutional capacity building. a clear definition of respective roles and responsibilities of the different parties and their inter-linkages.Inter-ministerial collaboration should involve: strong leadership and commitment at the highest level of all participating ministries; clearly spelled out role of the lead ministry; and a strategic plan that incorporates and aligns collaborative activities to the interests of the ministries.Needs based planning of rural road rehabilitation. It is important to: (i) concentrate on the roads that are priorities in a limited geographic area; (ii) carefully assess the type of rehabilitation needed (depending on the state of the road and commensurate with its actual/expected use to connect producers to inputs and markets); (iii) ensure that engineering studies are ready at the start of projects that include road rehabilitation works and presence of local contractors; (iv) formally involve a road engineer in the Bank’s supervision team; and (v) define modalities for medium and long-term maintenance.

title: Niger - Agro-Pastoral Export and Market Development Project
report_name: Niger - Agro-Pastoral Export and Market Development Project
project_id: NE-Niger Agro-Pastoral Export and Market Development Project -- P095210
abstract: Ratings of Agro-Pastoral Export and Market Development Project for Niger were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) physical investments need to be complemented by regulatory measures, in particular those that create a more friendly business environment and facilitate trade flows; (ii) time was insufficient to get to the point where fully developed Productive Alliances (PAs) would have emerged, in particular, as regards the develop of legal and enforceable contractual relations, in a context such as Niger where transactions typically remain informal; (iii) that matching grants work well to the extent that the investment is sustainable in the long-term (during the cruising period), meaning that the investor can take over project funding and renew investments after project closure, using the proceeds of his own sub-project; and (iii) regarding weirs and thresholds, sufficient attention needs to be given to properly target beneficiaries, and establish sustainable management and maintenance mechanisms involving their participation, which are the main ingredients for their sustainability.

title: Mali - First and Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Mali - First and Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: ML-PRSC 2 -- P103466,ML-PRSC 1 -- P083803
abstract: Ratings for the First Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC) Project for Mali were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Ratings for the Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project for Mali were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: a selective focus on policy areas with already concrete reform actions or strong prospects for reforms is preferable for a PRSC series. The first PRSC series did not make a real break from the past. It was complex, lacked selectivity, was heavy on processes and was not sufficiently focused on outcomes.  Not surprisingly, the results are broadly similar to those obtained in the previous ten years. While an incremental approach can serve to build up a consensus for parts of a broader reform agenda that require more consensus-building, there is a risk of loss of focus when this incremental process approach is applied across the board to a broad and complex range of policy reform areas. Monitoring and evaluation is an essential part of any Bank-supported operation. It is important to ensure, during preparation and approval of the operation, that there is a sound but simple monitoring and evaluation system within Government's institutions. Few indicators should be selected, with an emphasis on outcome rather than output or process indicators. The links between policy actions and indicators should be clarified and credible, and the selection of indicators should take into account limitations in the availability of data and not impose additional costs to the Government.

title: Western Africa - Niger Basin Water Resources Development and Sustainable Ecosystems Management Project
report_name: Western Africa - Niger Basin Water Resources Development and Sustainable Ecosystems Management Project
project_id: 3W-Niger Basin Water Resources Development and Sustainable Ecosystems Management Project -- P093806
abstract: The Niger Basin Water Resources Development and Sustainable Ecosystems Management Project for Western Africa had a moderately satisfactory overall outcome, a moderately satisfactory performance by the Bank and the monitoring and evaluation was modest. Some of the lessons learned were: (1) How to design a regional project that accommodates at the same time local, national and regional agendas considering the disparity of approaches and existing capacities. (2) The organizational reform conducted in 2005 had led to the reorganization of the institution during the project preparation. (3) The Sustainable Development Action Plan is well aligned with the countries’ priorities. (4) As a regional project, its objectives were articulated around objectives assigned to the Niger Basin Authority and to countries by extension. (5) When dealing with a complex project involving many countries, including fragile states, it is important to consider the risks assessment of the externally driven delays and provision of a contingency plan to mitigate accordingly to the risk. (6) Project implementation requires periodic field presence and continuous follow-up by task teams and continuity in the team leadership. (7) The original project was ambitious relative to its implementation period in terms of both the legal reforms and some of the infrastructure activities.

title: Burkina Faso - Agricultural Diversification and Market Development Project
report_name: Burkina Faso - Agricultural Diversification and Market Development Project
project_id: BF-Agricultural Diversification and Market Development Project -- P081567
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Diversification and Market Development Project for Burkina Faso were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: an intervention involving a multi-sector/multi-level approach with different institutions, components, areas of concentration (geographic and thematic) is challenging to implement when managerial/institutional capacity in the country is not so strong. While the project meant well to simultaneously try to change incentives for stakeholders in several value chains, improve the enabling environment, reduce the gap in infrastructure, and build stronger institutions, the range of activities was too large. Future ground-breaking operations in Burkina Faso and in the sub-region with the World Bank or other donors need to be kept simple, use piloting and evaluation for future scale-up, and receive closer supervision from authorities so that the learning opportunity is optimized and the client can start a new phase of investments with confidence. Linking small farmers to markets could work! Such a linkage has often been a vexing proposition in going from design to implementation. The project made this feasible by leveraging two key instruments at its disposal: (i) The establishment of the inter-professional organizations (IPO) gave small producers a seat and a voice at the table, around which value chain participants are convened to identify mutually beneficial solutions to commons issues; (ii) The clustering of enterprises implemented by the project was another lever that helped link small animal producers to input and regional export markets. By working closely together to coordinate their activities, small producers in the cluster achieved together the economy of scale each could never have attained on their own. This allowed the cluster to gain access to credit, other inputs, and to wholesalers on behalf of the members. Without the consolidated quantities offered by the clustered enterprises, these wholesalers would have bypassed the individual small producers. Maison de l’Enterprise du Burkina Faso (MEBF), a business incubator, offers a model to emulate. In its free advisory role, MEBF is like similar agencies in developed countries, such as the SCORE Association of business mentors supported by the US Small Business Administration (SBA). As with SCORE, MBEF helps with actors trying to start a business, or needing assistance with their existing business. MEBF goes one step further, as they can take on projects to follow in-situ operations and turn them into formal SME. A formal SME enhances its chance to access credit and compete in public bids. In this role, MEBF is an effective business incubator. With its successful collaboration with the project, MEBF has showed that it can expand entrepreneurship, including in rural areas. Future value chain development projects in Burkina Faso should capitalize on this home-grown expertise developed and should even consider MEBF as a potential home for projects to be considered under the strategic pillar of agro-competitiveness of its current and future national development plans.

title: Mali - Economic policy and Public Finance Management Credit (EPPFMC) Project
report_name: Mali - Economic policy and Public Finance Management Credit (EPPFMC) Project
project_id: ML-Economic Policy and Public Finance Management Credit -- P083799
abstract: Ratings for the Mali Economic Policy and Public Finance Management Credit (EPPFMC) Project were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: for a country such as Mali with weak institutional and administrative capacities, it is important to be realistic on what can be achieved, and be selective on the issues to be undertaken within a short timeframe; the operation demonstrated the merits of including in Mali's policy-based lending operations, policy and institutional reforms measures complementary to and supportive of ongoing sector operations; it is important to develop a complete M&E/Results framework to enable clear assessment of program outcomes; and finally, policy measures should be better matched to the objectives sought.

title: Niger – Projet de promotion de I’irrigation privée
report_name: Niger - Private Irrigation Promotion Project
project_id: NE-NE-Priv Irrigation Promotion SIL (FY02) -- P072996
abstract: Les notes qui ont été attribuées au Projet de promotion de l’irrigation auprès du secteur privé au Niger sont les suivantes : les résultats du projet étaient très satisfaisants, le ratio risque-développement était important, la performance de la Banque mondiale a été jugée satisfaisante et la performance de l’emprunteur a été jugée modérément satisfaisante. Les leçons apprises comprennent : l’accent mis par le projet sur des installations d’irrigation efficientes avec la pleine participation du secteur privé demeure valide et très pertinent pour le Niger. La conception du projet et les technologies utilisées pourraient servir d’exemple pour d’autres pays faisant face à des contraintes beaucoup moins importantes. Les investissements dans l’irrigation dans le cadre de ce projet ont été d’un coût moyen de 2 000 dollars par hectare. Ces investissements peuvent apporter des améliorations mesurables au bien-être des agriculteurs et générer un retour sur investissement élevé, possiblement d’environ 20 %. Le projet montre que des responsabilités supplémentaires sont créées pour le secteur privé lorsqu’il gère des projets qui sont très visibles et d’une grande importance politique. La transparence est essentielle dans les domaines de la gestion financière et de la passation des marchés. Le suivi et l’évaluation aident également grandement à prendre des décisions éclairées.

title: Niger - Private Irrigation Promotion Project
report_name: Niger - Private Irrigation Promotion Project
project_id: NE-NE-Priv Irrigation Promotion SIL (FY02) -- P072996
abstract: Ratings for the Private Irrigation Promotion Project for Niger are as follows: outcomes of the project were highly satisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, bank performance was satisfactory, and borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the project's emphasis on cost-effective small-scale irrigation with full participation of the private sector remains valid and highly relevant for Niger. The project design and the technologies could be an important example for other countries facing much less severe constraints. Irrigation investments under the project had an average cost of approximately US$2,000 per hectare. These investments can bring measurable improvements in farmers' welfare and achieve high economic rates of return, possibly above 20 percent. The project shows that additional responsibilities are created for the private sector when it manages projects that are highly visible and politically charged. Transparency in financial management and procurement is essential. Monitoring and evaluation greatly helped to inform decision-making.

title: Mali - National Rural Infrastructure Project in Support of the First Phase of the National Rural Infrastructure Program
report_name: Mali - National Rural Infrastructure Project in Support of the First Phase of the National Rural Infrastructure Program
project_id: ML-National Rural Infrastructure Project -- P041723
abstract: Ratings for the National Rural Infrastructure Project in Support of the First Phase of the National Rural Infrastructure Program for Mali were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was modest, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: effective implementation of reforms required a realistic timeline to build capacity, consensus, and government commitment. This process, which often takes more time than expected, requires continuous policy dialogue. The public-private partnership established for the new irrigation financing mechanism is a good method of increasing the area developed for irrigated agriculture. This model should be extended to various actors in a more cohesive manner. National Rural Infrastructure Project (PNIR) investments should serve as a means of piloting a new agricultural development model, characterized by new modalities of access to land and water that will ultimately make it possible to create an official market for land and permit co financing of land settlement by beneficiaries. The pilot undertaken for the project has demonstrated that, to be effective, the package transferred to the private producer must include completely finished plots (that is, plots in which tertiary canals exist) as well as access to credit, inputs, and extension advice. In addition, the study of the irrigation funding mechanism and the pilot in Koumouna showed that the Malian banking system is prepared to finance land development when appropriate procedures are in place and producers have clear title to their land. However, this approach needs to be monitored and its impact assessed with respect to: (i) the feasibility of collecting the property tax on privatized land; and (ii) the risk of land speculation and land ownership by a minority of people, which will contradict the objective of reinforcing direct farming by family businesses.

title: Burkina Faso - COVID-19 Crisis-Response Development Policy Financing Project
report_name: NA
project_id: BF-Burkina Faso COVID-19 Crisis-Response Development Policy Financing -- P174315
abstract: Ratings for the Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) Crisis-Response Development Policy Financing Project for Burkina Faso were as follows: overall outcome and achievement of objectives (efficacy) were moderately satisfactory, and relevance of prior actions was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: when designing a program in a crisis context, World Bank Group (WBG) teams should consider that there may be additional leverage to achieve more ambitious, longer-term reforms. At the same time, WBG teams should exercise caution when setting targets for indicators, particularly for standalone operations, that depend on establishing new data platforms. Bank teams can improve prospects for implementation and sustainability, including building client ownership, for which there is no substitute, as well as mitigating the risks of delays especially for a standalone operation, by using complementary operations, both investment policy financing (IPF) and development policy financing (DPF). A WBG team should be mindful of several potential success factors in establishing a new framework for dealing with a crisis. One strategy to increase the deployment and use of digital platforms is to create a kind of performance competition between ministries. Substantial progress can be achieved in the area of debt transparency in a short amount of time, when the WBG, development partners and the client are able to realize several critical success factors simultaneously.

title: Chad - First and Second Economic Recovery and Resilience Development Policy Financing Project
report_name: NA
project_id: TD-Chad Second Programmatic Economic Recovery and Resilience Grant -- P168606
abstract: Ratings for the First and Second Economic Recovery and Resilience Development Policy Financing Project for Chad were as follows: overall outcome and achievement of objectives (efficacy) were moderately unsatisfactory, and relevance of prior actions was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: Chad’s fragility, conflict, and violence (FCV) context and its limited capacity to deal with shocks and accepting that there will likely be high risk-high reward tradeoffs. Formulation of RIs and targets - they should still be relevant and potentially achievable if external risks materialize, and they should be precise and measurable in a low data environment. Complementary technical assistance through complementary operations and analytics to support the government to implement and closely monitor reforms in a low institutional capacity environment.
