Complementing fiscal stabilization with reforms aimed at improving the capability of the state to protect the poor and vulnerable through human capital development and adaptive social protection programs. Building an engagement model with the government using virtual platforms that can be resilient during crisis situations that are more likely to occur in FCV contexts.

title: Niger - First and Second Fostering Rural Growth Reform Development Policy Financing
report_name: Niger - First and Second Fostering Rural Growth Reform Development Policy Financing
project_id: NE-Niger Second DPO Series -- P166124
abstract: Ratings for the First and Second Fostering Rural Growth Reform Development Policy Financing for Niger were as follows: overall outcome and achievement of objectives (efficacy) was moderately satisfactory, and relevance of prior actions was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: long-term engagement embodying both technical and political economy considerations is essential to preserve and build on reform gains. Despite some stakeholder opposition, it is possible to implement a cost-based tariff adjustment that improves an electric utility’s financial position, but the effort needs to be sustained. The creation of new arrears to local governments in 2018 and 2019 shows the need to put in place a strong framework that prevent the recurrence of this problem. Targeting of farm inputs through an e-voucher implementation program is a promising alternative to governments continuing to subsidize the procurement of farm inputs for all farmers but may require continued development partner support until it is established on a national basis. It can highly beneficial for the quality of the results framework and, later, for program evaluation, to retain a results expert during preparation to ensure a high-quality results framework that has a better chance of remaining stable throughout the program. In countries with significant security issues, it is beneficial for program implementation to have a strategy with both short- and long-term measures to lower the risk of disruption. When establishing a new institution, it is a good practice to help operationalize the new entity with pilot activities to build its capabilities.

title: Niger - Refugees and Host Communities Support Project
report_name: NA
project_id: NE-Niger Refugees and Host Communities Support Project -- P164563
abstract: Ratings for the Refugees and Host Communities Support Project for Niger were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was high. Some lessons learned included: the integrated, multi-sectoral, geographically focused approach that advocated supporting the entire population affected by a crisis, regardless of their status (as refugees, IDPs, hosts), proved to be highly appropriate and effective in Niger. The robust remote monitoring system (GEMS), used as a design and implementation tool, helped PARCA to anticipate alternative project sites and to swiftly execute site switches; this was necessary when growing insecurity and/or beneficiary movement prevented project completion at a selected site and required shifting to another site. As the project needed to be aware of the dynamic security and FD situations in its target sites, it operated out of three implementation offices in three regions, which provided critical information on local context and flexibility to adapt to changing circumstances. Use of a national telecommunication operator for mobile cash transfers allowed the project to efficiently expand its geographic reach and increase its number of economic opportunity beneficiaries as the operator was able to provide services nation-wide. Well-designed procurement documents, with a breakdown of works by infrastructure type (education, health, water infrastructure, etc.) and by municipality, enable the recruitment of construction companies with solid knowledge of the local context regarding social and security issues, market-competitive pricing. Additionally, the integration of security costs into private sector bidding documents and contracts solicits viable bids and responsible service providers who deliver results. The use of standard Nigerien infrastructure designs promotes timely infrastructure completion, effective O&M and repairs with materials available at local markets, at local prices and with local know how, increasing the chances of realizing infrastructure work in other FCV areas within the country.

title: Benin - Second Fiscal Reform Growth Project
report_name: Benin - Second Fiscal Reform Growth Project
project_id: BJ-Benin Second Fiscal Reform and Growth DPF -- P166115
abstract: Ratings for the Second Fiscal Reform Growth Project for Benin were as follows: overall outcome and achievement of objectives was moderately satisfactory, and relevance of prior actions was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the use of the development policy operation (DPO) instrument was critical in moving reforms forward that otherwise might have stalled, particularly in line Ministries. DPOs can become a key instrument to support complex reforms in sectors of World Bank engagement and link transversal objectives. Longer term engagements are necessary to achieve sectoral overhauls. To achieve greater and faster impact of reforms, it is important to coordinate with other donors and Bank-financed investments loans. Having strong ownership from the government is the best way to achieve structural reforms and shifts in attitude. Innovative solutions and flexibility to support the government’s requests can bring additional benefits. Additional effort is needed to integrate the monitoring and evaluation (M and E) in government systems from the outset, particularly in a low capacity country.

title: Sierra Leone - Agro-Processing Competitiveness Project
report_name: NA
project_id: SL-Sierra Leone Agro-Processing Competitiveness Project -- P160295
abstract: Ratings for the Agro-Processing Competitiveness Project for Sierra Leone were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: for projects implemented in countries with fragility and significant fiscal constraint, it is crucial to embed the sustainability of project activities in the design of the project to sustain impact. The project could have been more proactive in planning for sustainability, as some key MDAs had limited capacity in core areas. For example, SMEDA staff could have been embedded at the TA Facility to ensure transfer of knowledge on the SME development program. Similarly, the MTI technical staff could have had stronger collaboration with the PCU to build practical project management and coordination expertise. This would have been particularly beneficial given the high turnover of ministers affected by cabinet reshuffles and a change in the Government due to a general election. Going forward, the Government of Sierra Leone will need to ensure a closer link between fiduciary and technical aspects of a given project. While the centralized PFMU model could in principle lead to efficiency gains and closer oversight of projects by the MOF, it will need to be better integrated with the PCUs, have a clear operation manual to guide its operations, and have a clear and transparent funding mechanism to sustain its operation to minimize delays and avoid reporting gaps. This is especially true for the procurement function, which requires that technical staff are aware of the procurement stage for any given activity and are part of evaluation committees. The project, in line with previous research findings, has shown that good-quality TA to SMEs can be beneficial and help optimize the impact of business grants. Finally, clear and regular communication around project achievements and status is essential to ensure satisfaction of both direct and indirect beneficiaries.

title: Cote dIvoire - E-Agriculture Project
report_name: NA
project_id: CI-CI: Cote d'Ivoire E-Agriculture Project -- P160418
abstract: Ratings for the E-Agriculture Project for Cote dIvoire were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: flexibility and adaptability are crucial in responding to external crises such as the COVID-19 pandemic and Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, requiring projects to reorient funds and activities to address emerging needs. A strong political will and good implementation arrangements can enhance coordination and implementation. The implementation arrangements could have been better prepared by being inclusive, identifying constituent members of the different organs and the relationships between them. Similarly, getting the buy-in of these institutions on the framework designed is critical to avoid competency overlaps. For future digital operations, it will be of critical importance to foster cross-Ministerial collaboration, particularly with the leading coordinating role of the Digital Economy Ministry. A key approach to successfully foster cross-Ministerial collaboration is to have ad hoc technical committees (comprising technical staff of key stakeholders) to implement and follow critical procurements. As countries in Africa are facing acute budget constraints, there is an increasing interest in implementing financing gap mechanisms (such as the reverse auction approach) to deploy digital infrastructures. However, there are challenges related to the countries' familiarity with, understanding of, and willingness to adopt these mechanisms. Specifically, Governments are reluctant to provide catalytic subsidies to private operators as they would like to retain the ownership of the digital infrastructure deployed, even though this would significantly decrease the sustainability of the assets. To address these challenges, the World Bank should continue providing resources to improve the skills and knowledge necessary for countries to effectively implement and manage financing gap mechanisms and encourage exchange visits between countries to share knowledge and lessons learned.

title: Cote d'Ivoire - Cashew Value Chain Competitiveness Project
report_name: NA
project_id: CI-Cashew Value Chain Competitiveness Project -- P158810
abstract: Generate

title: Nigeria - Agro-Processing, Productivity Enhancement and Livelihood Improvement Support Project
report_name: NA
project_id: NG-Agro-Processing, Productivity Enhancement and Livelihood Improvement Support Project -- P148616
abstract: Ratings for the Agro-Processing, Productivity Enhancement and Livelihood Improvement Support Project for Nigeria were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: early and continued engagement with the State Government on World Bank policies and regulations can help reduce delays. Limited familiarity with Bank procedures led to implementation delays in Kaduna, Enugu, and Cross River Sates. Success in high performance states (such as Kano, Lagos and Kogi), can be attributed to strong government support and flexibility to implement the Project in accordance with Bank regulations and guidelines. Communicating these guidelines to state governments at preliminary stages of project preparation and throughout implementation can ensure seamless implementation. Stakeholder collaboration and partnerships at all levels (government, non-governmental organizations, research entities, private sector, etc.) can be effective ways of developing value chains. The project fostered fruitful collaborative arrangements with several research institutions and extension agencies for the generation and dissemination of appropriate technologies, as well as with private sector service providers, input suppliers, and off-takers. Some beneficiaries leveraged additional financial resources from the private sector through such business alliances. All these structures and collaborative arrangements greatly facilitated project implementation.

title: Western and Central Africa - Regional Disease Surveillance Systems Enhancement Project
report_name: NA
project_id: 3W-Regional Disease Surveillance Systems Enhancement (REDISSE) -- P154807
abstract: Ratings of Regional Disease Surveillance Systems Enhancement Project for Western and Central Africa were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: (i) Having a regional approach is vitally important for health security; (ii) One Health requires a full-fledged technical organization to be a steward at the implementation level to operationalize One Health; (iii) Health Security requires investments tradeoffs between regional and national, between sectors and between sub-national regions; (iv) Regional and national data collection and harmonization was an important achievement of the project for 12 out of 16 countries covered across the REDISSE phases; (v) Sustainability planning needs to be considered throughout the early stages of the project and should be settled by the MTR; (vi) Strengthening the supply of and demand for health security professionals across IHR core capacity requirements is essential to sustain the development of the regional, national, and sub-national ecosystem and to include more women and youth in this important field; and (vii) Measuring results of regional interventions were not addressed in the JEE and became challenging to attribute to the REDISSE project.

title: Western Africa - Africa Higher Education Centers of Excellence Project
report_name: Western Africa - Africa Higher Education Centers of Excellence Project
project_id: 3W-Africa Higher Education Centers of Excellence Project -- P126974
abstract: Ratings for the Africa Higher Education Centers of Excellence Project for Western Africa were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: results-based financing can be a powerful mechanism for incentivizing effort and behavior change when used appropriately. Strong institutional ownership is an essential component for a successful center of excellence model. Leadership plays an important role in the center of excellence model. National efforts to reform higher education systems will be essential to complement efforts made by the Project for sustained results. Increasing participation of women will require greater attention. Strengthened linkages with the private sector will be important for knowledge transfer. There is a need to expand the role of the Regional Facilitation Unit (RFU) beyond its core mandate tasks of verification, implementation support, and technical assistance. Extensive multi-layered implementation support is important for success of a large regional project.

title: Niger - Third Phase of Community Action Program Project
report_name: Niger - Third Phase of Community Action Program Project
project_id: NE-NIGER COMMUNITY ACTION PROGRAM PHASE 3 -- P132306
abstract: Ratings for the Third Phase of Community Action Program Project for Niger were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: after sixteen years of continuous Government of Niger (GoN) and Bank commitment, a considerable dynamic of decentralized development has emerged in Niger. There are strongly positive returns to a focus on gender but support to women needs to be assessed carefully. Payments for ecosystem services (in this case carbon) can foster environmental conservation. GoN and the Bank showed great foresight in engaging on a three phase, fifteen-year adaptable program loan (APL). In the event, this time frame turned out to be the right one, allowing completion of nationwide coverage, the capitalization of lessons, and the extension of the community-driven-development (CDD) model to a wider menu of agricultural and natural resource management activities.

title: Burkina Faso - Third Phase of the Community‐Based Rural Development Project
report_name: Burkina Faso - Third Phase of the Community‐Based Rural Development Project
project_id: BF-Third Phase Community Based Rural Development Project -- P129688
abstract: Ratings for the Third Phase of the Community‐Based Rural Development (CBRD) Project for Burkina Faso were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory; Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory; and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: Community driven development (CDD) and local development projects should clarify whether they aim for general poverty reduction, decentralization, economic development, or community patrimony management (natural capital). In this case, despite the strong focus that was announced in the project appraisal document (PAD) on national resources management (NRM) and SLM, the project has financed mostly local development, access to public services (education, health), and capacity building for the decentralization process. CDD projects can only achieve results at scale if there are decentralized capacities able to implement and maintain. the success of CBRD raises the question on what the barrier for development are and what is the most efficient way to remove them. The principal limitation seen in phase three was the decision to develop a new results framework, without considering the achievement of the prior phases. This has made it difficult to evaluate the contributions of phase three to the overall program. Thus, for multiphase projects like the CBRD, the phases should build upon each other.

title: Niger - First, Second and Third Grant Shared Growth Project
report_name: Niger - First, Second and Third Grant Shared Growth Project
project_id: NE-Second Shared Growth Credit -- P132757,NE-NIGER - Shared Growth Credit I -- P125272,NE-Third Shared Growth Credit -- P145251
abstract: Ratings for the First, Second and Third Grant Shared Growth Project for Niger were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was significant, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included : It is beneficial to tie a Development Policy Operation (DPO) into relevant investment operations to help both operations achieve better outcomes.Electricity reform requires the proper institutional setup prior to tariff changes to ensure appropriate rate adjustments and successful implementation. When institutional capacity at sector ministries is limited, it is advisable toconcentrate on a limited number of ministries to improve the prospects for Program outcomes.Procurement verification needs to be more than a one-time effort to beeffective. It is also necessary to have sustained follow-up and monitoring if auditfunctions are to be improved.To be able to better evaluate program results, it is important to haveindicators that are closely related to the prior actions.

title: West Africa - Second Phase of the Support Program for the West Africa Agricultural Productivity Program
report_name: West Africa - Second Phase of the Support Program for the West Africa Agricultural Productivity Program
project_id: 3W-West Africa Agricultural Productivity Program 2A -- P129565
abstract: Ratings of Second Phase of the Support Program for the West Africa Agricultural Productivity Program (WAAPP) for West Africa were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) the regional approach with an adaptable program lending  instrument was the key to WAAPP’s success in general and for WAAPP‐2A in particular; (ii)  without the coordinated regional research effort and the creation of RCoS there would have been little first‐class research, little research cooperation among  the countries, and no synergies with relevant Consultative Group on International Agricultural Research (CGIAR) or other international research centers;(iii) technologies should only be developed through the national and regional research centers if no solutions to a given problem already exists in other parts of  the  world; (iv) repeated funding of certain successful technologies should be the norm and all constraints for such technologies along the value chain should be tackled; (v) research  into  agricultural  mechanization  needs  a  paradigm  shift; (vi) the e‐voucher system that seeks to rationalize the distribution of subsidized inputs to make it more transparent  and  to  allow  to  target  specific  farmers,  namely the  poor is  a good  policy; and (vii) the Bill foresees the creation of a Tree Crops Development Authority and would initially involve  oil palm, rubber, cashew and shea.

title: Republic of Niger - First Growth Policy Reform Grant Project (GPRG-1), Second Growth Policy Reform Credit Project (GPRC-2), and Second Growth Policy Reform Credit Supplemental Credit Project
report_name: Republic of Niger - First Growth Policy Reform Grant Project (GPRG-1), Second Growth Policy Reform Credit Project (GPRC-2), and Second Growth Policy Reform Credit Supplemental Credit Project
project_id: NE-Niger Growth Policy Reform Grant  (Growth DPL-1) -- P107741,NE-GPRC-2 Supplemental Financing -- P129793,NE-Growth Policy Reform Operation II -- P117286
abstract: Ratings for the First Growth Policy Reform Grant Project (GPRG-1), Second Growth Policy Reform Credit Project (GPRC-2), and Second Growth Policy Reform Credit Supplemental Credit Project for Republic of Niger were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned include: the programmatic development policy operation approach proved extremely helpful in maintaining dialogue and reforms in a rapidly changing political environment. In particular, having an agreed reform program in place allowed the Bank to engage with the military transition government at a technical level and to continue to support the implementation of these reforms. It would have been unlikely that a new reform program could have been developed and agreed with the transition government. Close collaboration with other partners in a complex political environment is essential to come to a shared assessment of the situation and to adopt a coordinated and coherent approach in dealing with a de facto government. A programmatic development policy operation (DPO) approach plays an important role in keeping the reform agenda on track and mobilizing the Government on cross-cutting issues in an environment of frequent external shocks and instability. Most reforms would have never realized or accelerated without bank support through the growth policy reform series.

title: Africa - Adaptable Program Lending for West Africa Agricultural Productivity Program Project
report_name: Africa - Adaptable Program Lending for West Africa Agricultural Productivity Program Project
project_id: 3W-West Africa Agricultural Productivity Program APL (WAAPP-1C) -- P122065
abstract: Ratings of Adaptable Program Lending for West Africa Agricultural Productivity Program (WAAPP) Project for Africa were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) An effective regional organization like Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) was key to the success of the WAAPP series; (ii) A regional project, implemented successfully, can be a real driver of regional integration, motivating participating countries to cooperate beyond the traditional francophone/anglophone divide; (iii) The interdependence of national agricultural innovation systems can strengthen resilience to shocks at the national and regional level, regardless of whether those shocks are related to climate change, market volatility, or a pandemic; and (iv) A regional approach to agricultural development remains very relevant to speed Africa’s agricultural transformation.

title: Sierra Leone - Rural and Private Sector Development Project (RPSDP)
report_name: Sierra Leone - Rural and Private Sector Development Project (RPSDP)
project_id: SL-Rural and Private Sector Development -- P096105
abstract: Ratings for the Rural and Private Sector Development Project (RPSDP) for Sierra Leone were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was low or negligible, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: maintenance should begin on the first day after the road improvement works are completed. The problems of feeder road maintenance are not uniquely related to finance. There are technical issues related to the lack of planning and the lack of information on the state of the road network. For sustained maintenance of these roads there are measures that need to be instituted within the project implementation framework. The focus of construction of link roads should be based on community approaches where beneficiaries who opt for roads should lead the process of using local internal resources and labor with funding and support from the project to connect to higher order road. This will ensure ownership and guarantee maintenance. Also the local government should be in the picture from the beginning. Given the complexity and multi sectoral nature of the project, it will be appropriate for the project design to maintain synergy with other donors’ program or seek co-financing for some of the activities that will lead to sustainability of the benefits. One of the main take away of the project is that value chain development which is inclusive to stakeholders requires a more comprehensive approach.  This approach should address all parts of the targeted value chains by expanding investments in production, post-harvest (including processing), and marketing/supply to buyer and improving service delivery in parallel. As such, identifying investment models that are specific to existing business/market opportunities should be part of the design.

title: Senegal - Second Agricultural Services and Producer Organizations Project
report_name: Senegal - Second Agricultural Services and Producer Organizations Project
project_id: SN-Agricultural Services & Producer Organizations Project 2 -- P093622
abstract: Ratings for the Second Agricultural Services and Producer Organizations Project for Senegal were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory; risk to development outcome was substantial; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: attempting a radical transformation of agricultural services with the attendant institutional reforms is risky, transaction intensive, requires adequate staff and budget, and requires long-term involvement by the World Bank. Within the framework of an agricultural services project, support to producer organizations is crucial. One key issue in agricultural services projects is the level of funding that beneficiaries should provide. Evaluating the effects of agricultural services projects is a complex and methodologically challenging task.

title: Nigeria - HIV/AIDS Program Development Project
report_name: Nigeria - HIV/AIDS Program Development Project
project_id: NG-HIV/AIDS Program Development Project -- P070291
abstract: Ratings for the HIV/AIDS Program Development Project for Nigeria were as follows outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was substantial; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: institutional capacity development is the most critical component of an effective and sustained response. Training and skills upgrading alone will not be sufficient in the absence of such capacity strengthening, which must include clearly defined and well understood roles, responsibilities and management structures. Development of a comprehensive Monitoring and Evaluation (M and E) framework should be one of the first project interventions. This is important both to demonstrate progress against well defined baselines, and also to inform program development in order to choose the most effective interventions and target these to the areas where they would have greatest impact. Civil Society organizations can be important agents of change, but their contributions must be well managed if they are to be maximally effective. Principles for maximizing the effectiveness of community mobilization should be implemented with project support. Maximizing outcomes and impact depends on clear focus and prioritization, including selection of a limited number of key ministries and agencies, increased focus on those states which are experiencing the greatest number of new infections, and delivery of services at scale to the most at-risk and vulnerable groups and populations within those states.

title: Ghana - Agricultural Services Sub sector Investment Project
report_name: Ghana - Agricultural Services Sub sector Investment Project
project_id: GH-Agricultural Services Subsector Investment Project -- P000968
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Services Sub sector Investment Project for Ghana were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderately, the Bank performance was moderate satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderate satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: combining in one operation: (i) investment lending for a very large number of activities; and (ii) a broad institutional reform agenda concerning several centralized and decentralized organizations. Accelerating mid-term reviews when governments have changed: for a major sub-sector loan with several distinct policy orientations and institutional reform objectives, the election cycle must be taken into account in establishing the time line for the different steps in loan processing (Board presentation and effectiveness) and the expected time for implementation. Donor coordination and parallel financing: although ultimately donor coordination is the borrowers responsibility, in this kind of operation where success is to a certain extent predicated on better donor coordination, specific mechanisms for collaboration, including process mechanisms, procedures, and leadership arrangements will be explicitly and formally discussed and agreed upon by donor representatives in the field as well as their headquarters at the outset of the project. In addition, securing availability of other sources of funds during project preparation is critical for ensuring the coherence and integrity of the overall financing plan. Otherwise the program cost and design should be adjusted accordingly before International Development Association (IDA) approval.

title: Liberia - First, Second, and Third Inclusive Growth Development Policy Operations Project
report_name: NA
project_id: LR-Liberia Third Inclusive Growth Development Policy Operation -- P176993,LR-Liberia First Inclusive Growth Development Policy Operation -- P168218,LR-Liberia Second Inclusive Growth Development Policy Operation -- P175570

title: Togo -  Emergency Covid-19 Development Policy Financing
report_name: NA
project_id: TG-Togo Emergency Covid-19 DPO 2021 -- P174376
abstract: Ratings for the Emergency Covid-19 Development Policy Financing for Togo were as follows: overall outcome, achievement of objectives (efficacy), and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and relevance of prior actions was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: when dealing with a crisis, operations should focus on new priorities of the Government to manage the shock. Operations should target crucial measures addressing direct and indirect impacts of the crisis. Use of digital is key for resilience in face of shocks and can play a determinant role in the success of a reform. However, where mobile internet penetration is low, digital transition should be gradual. In times of crisis, balancing ambition and realism is crucial. Complex reforms should be avoided, and implementation of prior actions should be supported by technical assistance and projects. Information-sharing and strong political will are critical in times of crisis. Transparent and well-coordinated partnerships can also be used as a major driving force. Supervision of prior actions implementation post disbursement is critical and should become systematic. Formulation of results indicators and targets should be relevant and measurable in a low data environment.

title: Nigeria - Kaduna Economic Transformation Project
report_name: NA
project_id: NG-Nigeria - Kaduna State Economic Transformation Program-for-Results -- P161998
abstract: Ratings for the Kaduna Economic Transformation Project for Nigeria were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was high. Some lessons learned included: On-the-ground support and the ability to make frequent trips to the state provided a solid understanding of the challenges and allowed the team to go relatively fast and build a rapport with the state counterpart, which meant decisions were often expedited. From the outset, there was ownership and buy-in from the state due to the ambitious plans already laid out by the state which the program keyed into. The KSDP (2016–2020), Sector Implementation Plans (2017–2019), Kaduna State Infrastructure Master Plan (2018–2050), and the Kaduna State Industrial Plan have all helped in setting the vision for the state as they show clearly where the state is, where it is planning to go, and how it can get there from all sectors. The aim of all these plans is to deliver on jobs, social justice, and prosperity. They stress the importance of fast-tracking private investments, which will lead to growth and job creation and strengthen fiscal sustainability of the state. The strength, integrity and commitment of the PIU in driving implementation is an important highlight of the program. The PIU was staffed and financed by the state government and the project manager of the PIU (who was a civil servant) was highly competent and driven, ensuring that the steps set out in the PAD were followed religiously. The Program Action Plan was robust and useful for addressing gaps in the government system and capacity issues. The project manager had the ability to motivate the civil servants and create buy-in to the PforR as well as the wider KSDP. The Kaduna governor carefully selected reform minded individuals within the civil service to head key agencies and deliver on their mandate.

title: Côte d'Ivoire - Infrastructure for Urban Development and Competitiveness of Second Cities Project
report_name: NA
project_id: CI-CI-Infrastructure for Urban Development and Competitiveness of second cities -- P151324
abstract: Ratings of Infrastructure for Urban Development and Competitiveness of Second Cities Project for Côte d'Ivoire were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some of the lessons learned include: the project’s focus on improving competitiveness in secondary cities like Bouaké and San Pedro was innovative, serving as a template for similar operations in the region and crowding-in donor interest in supporting secondary cities in Côte d’Ivoire; the importance of continued local and national buy-in, including citizen engagement; procurement, contract management, and the importance of implementation readiness; strengthening M&E frameworks; effective MSME support but challenges with matching grants; and sustainability and innovation in MSME financing.

title: Chad - Value Chain Support Project
report_name: Chad - Value Chain Support Project
project_id: TD-Chad Value Chain Support Project -- P133021
abstract: Ratings for the Value Chain Support Project for Chad were as follows: outcome was moderately unsatisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: the project design should have incorporated more gender-related activities to contribute to the theory of change. In low-income fragility, conflict, and violence (FCV) countries with difficult political environments the governments’ administrative burdens should have been better anticipated and mitigated. When dealing with site construction that entails risks to inhabitant’s safety or environmental damages, it is compulsory to assign environmental and social development specialists during the project’s identification phase and onboard a safeguards expert in the project unit in addition to the focal point in a ministry. To avoid disproportions between objectives and allocated resources, the targets at project design stage must derive from precise and technical studies and analytical underpinnings. Weak monitoring and evaluation (M and E) framework and lack of sound system for accurate data collection affected assessment of results and their attribution to achieving the project objective (for example, the volume of meat produced in slaughterhouses and slaughter areas in target zones). Monitoring indicators in the results framework should be realistic, attributable, and achievable during implementation. It is highly recommended to increase sustainability and client’s ownership in a fragile and politically volatile environment like Chad by ensuring participation of private sector stakeholders as critical actors. When dealing with sensitive matters as healthy nutrition, the project preparation must be derived from sectoral technical studies. Resources allocated through the matching grant facility (MGF) must be based on a thorough analysis of small and medium-size enterprises (SMEs) needs. Strong leadership by task team leader (TTL), profound technical skills, strategic vision, integrity, and innovative approach to problem solving are key characteristics contributing to a successful project restructuring.

title: Implementation Completion and Results Report (ICR) Document - SUSTAINABLE LIVELIHOODS FOR RETURNEES AND HOST COMMUNITIES IN THE SENEGAL RIVER VALLEY - P132998
report_name: Implementation Completion and Results Report (ICR) Document - SUSTAINABLE LIVELIHOODS FOR RETURNEES AND HOST COMMUNITIES IN THE SENEGAL RIVER VALLEY - P132998
project_id: MR-SUSTAINABLE LIVELIHOODS FOR RETURNEES AND HOST COMMUNITIES IN THE SENEGAL RIVER VALLEY -- P132998
abstract: Ratings of Sustainable Livelihoods for Returnees and Host Communities in the Senegal River Valley Project for Mauritania were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest.  Some of the lessons learned include: (i) the importance of conducting a rigorous market assessment prior to vocational training; (ii) the value of carrying out a full-fledged cost-benefit analysis during appraisal to ensure economically sustainable livelihoods; (iii) complementing support for economic activities with basic social services; and (iv) clustering small farmers or community groups to promote economy of scale.

title: Guinea - Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises Development Project
report_name: Guinea - Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises Development Project
project_id: GN-Guinea - MSME Development Project -- P128443
abstract: The Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise (MSME) Development Project of Guinea had a moderately satisfactory overall outcome, a moderately satisfactory performance by the Bank and the monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. The lessons learned were: (1) Ownership, leadership, and support at all levels of the Government is key for successful implementation of the project. (2) Strong Institutional set up is key given the complicated, fragile, and low capacity setting. (3) Field team leadership is important in a fragile environment. (4) Improving the effectiveness of the institutions and developing instruments associated with the MSME development strategy is important in a fragile environment. (5) Deeper changes at restructuring would have higher impact. (6) Financial sustainability aspects need to be included early into the program design (7) In a fragile environment, the Results Framework needs to be simple and relevant.

title: Cote d'Ivoire - Postconflict Reconstruction and Recovery Grant Project
report_name: Cote d'Ivoire - Postconflict Reconstruction and Recovery Grant Project
project_id: CI-CI: Post-conflict Reconstruction and Recovery Grant (PCRRG) -- P122800
abstract: Ratings for the Postconflict Reconstruction and Recovery Grant Project for Cote d'Ivoire were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate; bank performance was satisfactory; and borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: first, a country emerging from a prolonged and severe crisis can still achieve significant progress as long as its government demonstrates ownership and commitment to reform; second, rapid bank support to a country in a post conflict period can be effective in supporting the return to stability; third, the effectiveness of a Bank development policy  operation can be enhanced by its complementarities with other Bank financed operations and the efforts of other active donors in the reform areas; and fourth, when operations are prepared under difficult situations of  institutional and/or political fragility, it may be important to limit the number and ambition of intermediate outcomes indicators.

title: Burkina Faso - Bagre Growth Pole Project
report_name: Burkina Faso - Bagre Growth Pole Project
project_id: BF-Burkina Faso - Bagre Growth Pole Project -- P119662
abstract: Ratings for the Bagre Growth Pole Project for Burkina Faso were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: for very complex projects such as Bagrepole, a programmatic approach (i.e., several sequential projects over a period of 10-15 years under a program umbrella) may be more effective than a single, standalone operation. Large, complex projects require proper sequencing both at the project design and at implementation phases. The right combination of conditions (infrastructure, services, value chain, investment climate) must be in place before the private sector fully engages, and it is a long-term challenge. Good results can still be achieved if there are appropriate adjustments during implementation, including increased supervisory support from the World Bank Group (WBG). Credit schemes, such as Bagrepole’s matching grants program, may be effective for some businesses, even though some are constrained by their own contribution of 20 percent requirement. An impact evaluation is a good idea to reinforce project results especially in terms of its support to grow micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) and create jobs, as well as the contribution of the project to designing similar interventions in other Sub-Saharan African countries. One very practical lesson from the project is to appreciate the importance of high-quality map generation throughout the life of a spatial project like Bagrepole. In general, a comprehensive communications strategy is needed for a project like Bagrepole. A complex, multi-dimensional project requires additional preparation time and the authorizing environment at the WBG needs to accommodate that extra time.

title: Congo, Republic of - Support to Economic Diversification Project
report_name: Congo, Republic of - Support to Economic Diversification Project
project_id: CG-CG Rep. Support to Economic Diversification Project -- P118561
abstract: The Support to Economic Diversification Project of the Republic of Congo had a moderately satisfactory overall outcome, a moderately satisfactory performance by the Bank and the monitoring and evaluation was modest. The lessons learned were: (1) When the realities of a fragile state, together with complicated political economy and macroeconomic conditions, are not taken into consideration neither when designing a project nor when steering it, this can critically affect project outcomes. (2) Importance of a “pilot-type” project for future WB operations in the Republic of Congo. (3) Instability at the PIU level and lack of appropriate technical staff can affect the timeliness of delivery of activities and ultimately the ability to analyze the impact of said activities. (4) Design of the Matching grant activity, including the number of steps in approval for the processing and overall method for management, caused delays in implementation of the activity and an inability for some approved firms to eventually participate. (5) The inclusion of such a large percentage to come from counterpart funding posed significant risks to achievement of project outcomes.

title: Gambia - Public Sector Reform and Growth Grant Project
report_name: Gambia - Public Sector Reform and Growth Grant Project
project_id: GM-Budget Support -- P107398
abstract: Ratings for the Public Sector Reform and Growth Grant Project for Gambia were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the participation of several donors in a multi-year program for budget support can increase the level of resources and focus them on a common objective but this arrangement is more vulnerable to annual changes in donor priorities and requires timely communication and coordination. The sequential donor financing of programmatic budget support is inherently more risky than co-financing of a single annual operation because planned resource allocations may change by the time the donor's annual program for a given country is approved. And this risk seems likely to have increased with the global economic downturn and shifting resource priorities. Measurable performance indicators aligned with the capacity of the Borrower are necessary but not sufficient elements of timely, effective Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E). It needs to be accompanied by a clear delineation of reporting responsibilities. The program document also specifies that even after disbursement of its single tranche, the Bank was to continue participating in annual reviews of the program. These reviews were to monitor reform actions, evaluate the impact of the reforms on the development objectives, and discuss strategic adjustments to the policy matrix taking into account the latest country developments, stakeholder support and alternative options for realizing the intended development objectives.

title: Benin - Supplemental Financing for the Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project : Benin - Supplemental Financing for the Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Benin - Supplemental Financing for the Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: BJ-Fourth Poverty Reduction Strategy Credit -- P096928,BJ-BJ- Poverty Reduction Support Grant - PRSC 6 -- P117287,BJ-Fifth Poverty Reduction Support Grant-PRSC 5 -- P107498
abstract: Ratings for the Supplemental Financing for the Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project for Benin were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory; and borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: stated objectives and monitored results indicators need to be realistic and focused on indicators that are attributable to Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC) supported actions. Some of the indicators in the series were situated at a level where attribution was difficult, if not impossible, and where a variety of exogenous factors weighed far more heavily on the achievement, or non-achievement, of results than supported actions. It is clear that policy actions are often not enough to effect significant and sustainable change. While specific policy actions may be necessary to create an environment conducive to change, additional enabling interventions are also frequently required. An additional lesson learned may be that a PRSC series will be more effective if the reform agenda supported by the series goes deeper rather than broader. In addition to including key policy actions in the operation, it may be beneficial if supporting actions, those related to implementation measures or second generation reforms associated with the initial policy action, are also included in the operation in order to ensure increased effectiveness of the initial policy action. Coherence across prior actions and triggers is also important to ensuring the effectiveness and sustainability of supported reforms. Prior actions and triggers should be designed as a coherent, mutually reinforcing package of reforms that together contribute to intended results rather than as a disparate number of individual actions each with its own individual targeted result. It is helpful if the provision of the credit/grant could be closely aligned with the budget cycle. The fiscal constraints imposed by declining and uncertain revenue streams required that expenditures and revenues be closely matched in order to avoid burgeoning budget deficits. The series benefited from significant donor cooperation and coherence and this mutually strengthened donor influence with the Government and allowed for more effective engagement with the authorities.

title: Togo - Economic Recovery and Governance Grant Project
report_name: Togo - Economic Recovery and Governance Grant Project
project_id: TG-TG-Economic Recovery and Governance Grant -- P110618
abstract: Ratings for the Economic Recovery and Governance Grant (ERGG) Project for Togo were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: close coordination with other development partners, both with regard to substance of the program and timing, has been indispensable to mitigate risks and improve the leverage in the policy dialogue. It also provided some scope for dividing responsibilities according to the main strengths of each partner. Coordination between the ERGG, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility (PRGF) and the African Development Bank (AfDB) budget support operation created a critical mass and proved particularly important. Given the capacity bottlenecks in the country and the fragile political situation, it was necessary to ensure that the arrears clearance and development policy operation were accompanied by appropriately targeted technical assistance, by the Bank and other donors. In this case, the ERGG was supported by the low income countries under stress (LICUS) grants and the Financial Sector and Governance Reform Technical Assistance (TA) Project. In fragile country situations, it is particularly important to select a focused number of prior actions that are deemed essential to supporting the Government's broader reform program. Selectivity not only reduces transaction costs for the Government that is burdened by multiple management demands while the capacity is weak, but also increases the likelihood that these reforms are successfully implemented and the reform program moves forward.

title: Mali - Rural Community Development Project
report_name: Mali - Rural Community Development Project
project_id: ML-Rural Community  Development Project -- P040653
abstract: Ratings for the Rural Community Development Project (PACR) for Mali were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: necessity for authorities to discuss and decide early on Project hosting arrangements. Government authorities need to be as proactive as possible on project hosting arrangements so as to discuss and decide on this critical aspect as soon as possible so that not only possible delays in project preparation can be avoided or significantly reduced but also ownership is fostered and stronger commitment of authorities early on. It is important to note that when a conflict situation does occur in any country, central institutions can be severely weakened. In this context, CDD approach and related projects are often among the few interventions that may still function. Experience has shown that CDD approach can play an important role in the reconciliation process between communities affected by the conflict. PACR approach and implementation procedures of the local productive initiative fund (LPIF) were critical to give more responsibility to the different actors, those of government institutions, but particularly at the community level. This allowed a better ownership of local investments and an increase of awareness for appropriate maintenance and sustainability.

title: Guinea - Second National Rural Infrastructure Project
report_name: Guinea - Second National Rural Infrastructure Project
project_id: GN-National Rural Infrastructure Project -- P065127
abstract: Ratings for the Second National Rural Infrastructure Project for Guinea were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was high, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the labor intensive maintenance components had strong ownership by project beneficiaries and the targeted length was entirely executed. This is because the beneficiaries of this particular component and local contractors worked together to ensure the implementation of labor intensive maintenance works. A well-functioning institutional arrangement for the project is a critical factor to ensure implementation success. In the case of the project, this risk should have been mitigated as part of the preparation process, even if it will have resulted in a delay in project approval. As the project implementation unit is a key to project implementation, earlier training program should have been put in place for project key staff. The project included an indicator on road maintenance while the project did not support this activity directly since it did not provide funding to the Road Maintenance Fund. This means that there is need for a careful selection of indicators and a need to ensure that sufficient leverage exists at the project level for some indicators and even reforms.

title: Niger - Agro-Pastoral Export Promotion Project
report_name: Niger - Agro-Pastoral Export Promotion Project
project_id: NE-Niger Agro-Pastoral Export Promotion Project -- P058730
abstract: Ratings for the Agro-Pastoral Export Promotion Project for Niger were as follows: the outcome was unsatisfactory, the sustainability was unlikely, the institutional development impact was negligible, and the Bank and borrower performance were both unsatisfactory. Lessons learned from the project: A series of in-depth analyses of the selected sub-sectors should be conducted as part of project preparation to identify the key obstacles towards improved export performance. In integrated value chain approach should be adopted from the out start whereby support is provided to the different stakeholders in each selected sub-sector to develop and agree on a comprehensive strategy for achieving export growth. Grant support should be primarily for support services; grant support for physical investment should be provided only in cases where operators further down the value chain are unable to obtain financing from other sources. Project management should be contracted out to an independent team of experts who have been given decision making authority based on a detailed procedures manual, with ex-post controls by the Government. A rigorous monitoring and evaluation system should be developed as part of project preparation with as key element. It should be recognized that some value chains will require substantial private investment to achieve their full export potential. In future matching grant schemes, alternative means for managing grant resources need to be looked at including the possibility of contracting out the management of the scheme.

title: Cote d’Ivoire - Land Policy Improvement and Implementation Project
report_name: NA
project_id: CI-Cote d'Ivoire Land Policy Improvement and Implementation Project -- P157206
abstract: Ratings for the Land Policy Improvement and Implementation Project for Cote d’Ivoire were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: social inclusion was central to the success of Land Policy Improvement and Implementation Program (PAMOFOR), particularly through the introduction of the new clarification phase and formal land use contracts (LUCs). Sustained, high-level commitment and support from champions within the World Bank (WB) and government were critical to achieving the dual objectives of strengthening institutions while simultaneously delivering land records. Broader impacts, such as increased investment, productivity, and access to credit, often take time to materialize following land registration and typically depend on complementary reforms. However, the additional time, funding, and coordination invested in implementing the IE proved worthwhile, as it demonstrated early results that validate the Project’s theory of change (TOC). The project’s success was largely attributable to the digitization and streamlining of rural land registration procedures, which increased efficiency and scalability, and substantial counterpart financing, which is pivotal for sustainability.

title: Congo, Republic of - Integrated Public Sector Reform Project
report_name: NA
project_id: CG-Integrated Public Sector  Reform Project -- P160801
abstract: Ratings of Integrated Public Sector Reform Project for Congo, Republic of were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: The implementation schedule of governance programs should be sufficiently long and flexible; Proper establishment and capacitation of the Project Coordination Unit (PCU) with clear institutional setup and labor division before effectiveness is critical; World Bank project leads should ideally be based in country to make them easily accessible to PCU/Project Implementation Unit (PIUs); High-level political commitment and champions of reform are vital elements for the project's success but can often be a vulnerability; For a low-capacity environment, from the onset, the project design should be simple and easy to follow with a clearly defined scope and theory of change; and On the rollout of digital systems, the project design should be comprehensive from end to end and strictly target the achievement of the ultimate outcomes and not outputs.

title: Gabon - Statistical Development Project
report_name: NA

title: Niger - Climate Smart Agriculture Support Project
report_name: NA
project_id: NE-Climate Smart Agriculture Support Project -- P153420
abstract: Ratings for the Climate Smart Agriculture Support Project for Niger were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: local governments can be good entry points for engaging on community initiatives regarding climate change adaptation, but their limitations need be adequately assessed at appraisal and constantly monitored during project implementation. An e-voucher system can be a useful tool in delivering agricultural input subsidies to the poor, but it requires a critical understanding of the political economy. A climate change adaptation program is a truly collaborative effort of different agencies. Several factors are critical for successful project outcomes in a fragile environment.

title: Mali - Obsolete Pesticides Project
report_name: Mali - Obsolete Pesticides Project
project_id: ML-Mali Obsolete Pesticides Disposal and Prevention Project -- P146247
abstract: The overall outcome of the Obsolete Pesticides Project for Mali is moderately satisfactory, with satisfactory Bank performance and substantial monitoring and evaluation quality. The Project offers several lessons: 1. A highly skilled technical advisor on disposal operations to support the country on developing bidding documents, vetting bids, monitoring Environmental Management Plans (EMPs), and monitoring collection, transport, and site cleanup is essential; 2. Ambitious and complex data management systems always fall short; 3. Priorities for disposal, risk reduction, and prevention of future accumulation of stocks should be clearly prioritized in project design; 4. The Government’s ability to manage pesticides could be enhanced by mainstreaming the issue into the design of projects in other sectors rather than just in disposal projects; 5.  A sustainably-financed structure for pesticide management in Mali is essential for management over the long term and to avoid the re-accumulation of new OP (Obsolete Pesticide) stocks.

title: Nigeria - Second Phase of the Rural Access and Mobility Project
report_name: Nigeria - Second Phase of the Rural Access and Mobility Project
project_id: NG-NG-RURAL ACCESS & MOBILITY PROJECT-Phase 2 -- P095003
abstract: Ratings for the Second Phase of the Rural Access and Mobility Project for Nigeria were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: to maximize impact on agriculture, rural roads investments need to be supported by on and off-market interventions. Criteria for road selection are key determinant of success, for maximizing impact and for mitigating implementation challenges. When a project intervenes in diverse geographical areas, a differentiated approach for each state is recommended to best adapt technical responses and budgets. The phasing of the road rehabilitation work was intended to allow project implementation units (PIUs) to learn in phase one and provide lessons for phase two. In capacity-constrained environments, recourse to delegated project management can facilitate implementation and allow implementing agencies to focus on their core competencies. The community-based road maintenance experience has demonstrated the interest of communities in participating in road maintenance and their capacity to perform basic maintenance works. The project has initiated new practices and provided necessary tools for better road asset management of rural roads in the four participating states, but it is unclear whether the momentum will continue after the project ends.

title: Ghana - Second Land Administration Project
report_name: Ghana - Second Land Administration Project
project_id: GH-Land Administration Project - 2 -- P120636
abstract: Ratings for the Second Land Administration Project for Ghana were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: it is necessary to prepare and consider lessons from previous projects prior to finalizing the preparation of the next phase. Land projects are complex, expensive, and time consuming but highly beneficial if they are successfully completed. Major information technology (IT) systems are notorious for not being completed within a five-year project cycle. The process of customary land demarcation and surveying was expensive and complex. It is not clear that the benefits outweighed the costs, and the process brought to light a boundary conflict that was previously dormant.

title: Africa - First Phase of West Africa Regional Fisheries Program Project
report_name: Africa - First Phase of West Africa Regional Fisheries Program Project
project_id: 3W-West Africa Regional Fisheries Program -- P106063,3W-West Africa Regional Fisheries Program -- P108941
abstract: Ratings for First Phase of West Africa Regional Fisheries Program Project for Africa were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, GEO outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was moderate, risk to GEO outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: (i) Reforming the fisheries sector to generate lasting benefits requires an incremental approach and commitment over time; (ii) Political commitment and incentives play a key role in the success of reforms; (iii) Enhancing the size and efficiency of the post-harvest segment of the fishery value chain should wait until reforms are sufficiently advanced and government capacity is sufficiently strengthened; (iv) Community-led fisheries management is an effective mechanism for reducing fishing effort in the artisanal segment that requires substantial time and know-how to succeed; (v) Regional project results are likely to improve by partnering with relevant regional organizations provided sufficient allocation of resources; and (vi) Finally, multi-country/regional projects require sufficient staff and budget to ensure adequate implementation support in each of the participating countries.

title: Guinea - First Fiscal Management, Competitiveness, and Energy Reform Development Policy Financing Project
report_name: NA
project_id: GN-Guinea First Fiscal Management, Competitiveness, and Energy Reform DPF -- P166322
abstract: Some of the lessons learned in First Fiscal Management, Competitiveness, and Energy Reform Development Policy Financing Project of Guinea were as follows: (i) Sectoral diversification can help hedge against high political and governance risk, however there are tradeoffs with capacity constraints; (ii) The drawbacks of a low-capacity environment can be partly overcome by well-coordinated partnerships and complementary operations; (iii) Assuring sound results indicators while controlling for cofounding factors polluting results measurement is crucial; and (iv) In a low ‘monitoring and evaluation’ capacity context, selecting comprehensive, well-defined, and already regularly published indicators should be prioritized.

title: Nigeria - Nigeria for Women Project
report_name: NA
project_id: NG-Nigeria For Women Project -- P161364
abstract: Ratings for the Nigeria for Women Project (NFWP) were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: the NFWP was a pilot designed to test Women Affinity Group (WAGs’) ability to promote women’s social and economic empowerment at the community and household levels, in line with the economic recovery and growth plan. Beyond their critical savings and loans activities, WAGs can also tackle broader challenges. The WAG system should eventually foster women’s full financial inclusion through commercial banks. WAGs are not one-time projects, but a first phase of larger programs or Series of Projects (SOPs). Providing grants to WAGs to establish revolving funds as a permanent feature of WAG financial operations, as opposed to individual microgrants to WAG members, is more efficient. WAGs offer a platform for supporting social services more extensively.

title: Central African Republic - First and Second State Consolidation Development Program
report_name: Central African Republic - First and Second State Consolidation Development Program
project_id: CF-State Consolidation Development Program 2 -- P164442
abstract: Ratings of First and Second State Consolidation Development Program for Central African Republic were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was high, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some of the lessons learned include: (i) In a fragility context, with limited institutional capacity, and competing priorities, focusing on a set of core reforms and coordinating interventions across donors and within WB portfolio, bring better results; (ii) Support to reforms should go beyond the adoption of the policy measures and have longer term outlook toward their implementation; (iii) Belonging to a monetary union can help mitigate the effect of crisis on monetary framework; (iv) Significant investment on Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) is needed in support of development policy operations;  and (v) The definition of targets and objectives in context of fragility and weak institutional capacity should be made conservatively.

title: Liberia - First, Second, Third, and Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Operations and Supplementals for the Second and Third Operations
report_name: Liberia - First, Second, Third, and Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Operations and Supplementals for the Second and Third Operations
project_id: LR-Poverty Reduction Support Credit II -- P146619,LR-Poverty Reduction Support Credit III -- P151502,LR-Liberia Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Development Policy Operation -- P162111,LR-LR-Poverty Reduction Strategy Credit I -- P127317
abstract: Ratings for the First, Second, Third, and Fourth Poverty Reduction Support Operations and Supplementals for the Second and Third Operations for Liberia were as follows: outcome, achievement of objectives (efficacy), and Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and relevance of prior actions was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: good fiduciary practices require sustained repetitive measures to become ingrained in government operations. Good communication with all stakeholders (government, legislature, civil society) from the start is needed to avoid preparation and implementation delays. It is wise to keep the range of policy areas flexible so that new areas of reform can be pursued if circumstances warrant. Capacity for reform in countries like Liberia, which experienced a lack of rule-of-law based governance over several years requires heavy technical assistance (TA) to support a successful reform program.

title: Liberia - Land Administration Project
report_name: NA
project_id: LR-Liberia Land Administration Project -- P162893
abstract: Ratings for the Land Administration Project for Liberia were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: in contexts of institutional and legal transition, adopting a flexible, broadly defined project design that is not conditioned on the passage of potential legislation changes can enable projects to build capacity and support early progress towards achieving the project development objective (PDO) before adapting to the evolving framework in due course. Strong engagement with the donor community and civil society through the establishment of a multi-actor platform and the appointment of a director responsible for land sector stakeholder coordination can help spur implementation progress and maximize the impact of project activities. Support for the creation of a new land agency in a fragile context requires long-term support that extends beyond a 5-year project, at the risk of jeopardizing recent achievements.

title: Guinea - Third Village Community Support Project
report_name: Guinea - Third Village Community Support Project
project_id: GN-Third Village Community Support Project -- P156422
abstract: Ratings for the Third Village Community Support Project for Guinea were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: strengthened stakeholder ownership and accountability at all levels and capacity building at the grassroots are vital for the long-term sustainability of local government actions. Enhancing the institutionalization of participatory local development and service delivery is key to ensuring sustainable local governance. Bottom-up participatory models are effective tools for conducting strategic policy dialogue to advance long-term decentralization vision. Improved financial management systems - to facilitate fiscal transfers, manage tax revenues and fees, and possibly take advantage of commercial banking facilities - are key for effective decentralization.

title: Niger - Public Sector Capacity and Performance for Service Delivery Project
report_name: NA
project_id: NE-Niger - Public Sector Capacity and Performance for Service Delivery Project -- P145261
abstract: Ratings for the Public Sector Capacity and Performance for Service Delivery Project for Niger were as follows: outcome was moderately unsatisfactory, bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: in fragile countries with limited implementation capacity, the Government’s technical capacity and readiness to implement major ICT solutions should be assessed and confirmed before project approval. In the case of PCDS, a successful project outcome could not be achieved without the successful implementation of a modern HRMIS integrating HRM with payments. However, there was a lack of analytical underpinning at the time of approval, including on the political economy factors related to cleaning up the database and improving accountability and transparency in the management of the public wage bill in Niger. At the end, a new HRMIS could not be deployed. Efficiently using project preparation advances (PPA), non-lending TA, or existing operations to set the foundation for upcoming ICT activities, in general, and politically sensitive ones, is critical to ensure readiness during implementation. In addition, hiring technical expertise in project management under a PPA has proven to be effective in accelerating project implementation. Furthermore, the World Bank should provide hands-on support to governments to implement ICT solutions (and be selective on the number of ICT solutions). Due to the inherent complex nature of ICT activities, procurement processes in fragile settings are often long and equally complex, and further complicated by relatively short project cycles of 4-5 years (and usual resistance from numerous actors). Therefore, in addition to setting up the foundation for upcoming ICT activities through PPAs and existing operations, World Bank support is required to help the Government recruit qualified technical expertise in project management. For this reason, hands-on extended implementation support (HEIS) should be offered to the Government to expedite the procurement process, ensure the recruitment of qualified consultants, and contribute to adequate contract management during implementation.

title: Western and Central Africa - Senegal River Basin Climate Change Resilience Development Project
report_name: NA
project_id: 3W-Senegal River Basin Climate Change Resilience Development Project -- P131323
abstract: Ratings for the Senegal River Basin Climate Change Resilience Development Project were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: regional projects often involve the Regional Integration unit and several CMUs, which do not have these regional projects in their portfolio, although they oversee the Bank-country dialogue. It is critical for task teams to keep CMUs involved so that the project can contribute to and benefit from the country dialogue and help solve country-specific issues. A very good example of fruitful collaboration between the task team and the CMU took place in Mali to help address the issues which stalled the implementation of the irrigation investments, and find ways to restore the companies’ confidence that their work will be paid, which led them to remobilize and greatly improve delivery progress during the last months of the Project, resulting in increasing the area equipped under the Project from 200 ha to 1,549 ha by the closing date. Properly designed web-based Project monitoring platforms can facilitate the integration and tracking of key Project management functions, such as, technical progress, procurement, and financial management for individual country programs, thereby promoting more effective monitoring, transparency, and coordination of regional operations. Such web-based platforms should ideally have interactive interfaces such as portals that facilitate and capture transparent and timely two-way communication in real time between country-level implementing agencies and regional oversight entities on key aspects such as, invoice submissions, review, and payments, to more effectively track and monitor Project implementation.

title: Mali - Recovery and Reform Support Credit Project
report_name: Mali - Recovery and Reform Support Credit Project
project_id: ML-Mali RRSC -- P125866
abstract: Ratings for the Recovery and Reform Support Credit (RRSC) Project for Mali were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, and Bank and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the preparation and supervision of the RRSC benefitted from lessons learned from previous operations for improved design and supervision. Notwithstanding improvements in the quality of design and supervision, the outcome is still mixed. Borrowers and implementing agencies have a tendency to focus on the realization of prior actions, dedicating less attention to the results framework. There is a potential for the World Bank to build on leadership on donors' coordination to support a more consolidated reform program while leveraging stronger financial support.

title: Cameroon - Social Safety Nets Project
report_name: NA
project_id: CM-Cameroon Social Safety Nets -- P128534
abstract: Ratings for the Social Safety Nets (SSN) Project for Cameroon were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: it takes time to build government ownership for the SSN agenda. The institutional aspect of building the SSN may be better addressed through the full suite of World Bank instruments rather than solely through investment project financing (IPF). Surge capacity for the Project Implementation Unit (PIU) is required in instances where safety net projects are supporting emergency response.
