There are benefits in adopting an integrated approach to support refugees and host communities across the portfolio. Factoring in childcare arrangements is critical in ensuring that women are able to benefit equally from public works activities. Cash transfers have the potential for several secondary impacts, which are not usually monitored but are nevertheless critical. Developing an effective communication strategy from the outset, which cuts across different levels of government and other stakeholders, is essential.

title: Nigeria - State Employment and Expenditure for Results Project
report_name: Nigeria - State Employment and Expenditure for Results Project
project_id: NG-State Employment and Expenditure for Results Project -- P121455
abstract: NA

title: Chad - Second Local Development Program Support Project
report_name: Chad - Second Local Development Program Support Project
project_id: TD-Local Development Program Support Project II -- P113030
abstract: Ratings for the Second Local Development Program Support Project (LDPSP 2) for Chad were as follows: outcomes were unsatisfactory, risk to development outcome was high, Bank performance was unsatisfactory, and Borrower performance was unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: project development objectives (PDOs) should be less ambitious and more realistic for countries with limited implementation capacity and a fragile political environment. Bottom-up development projects that use community driven development (CDD) approaches are challenging to implement and scale up, because they require extensive support with respect to capacity building. One aspect that should be especially emphasized as it is crucial to the success of decentralization is the participatory process. Hence the need for such expertise as trained community facilitators on the ground. LDPSP 2 has done some headway on that front that need to be preserved. Project remedies should be clearly identified as different from overall dialogue between the Bank and the Borrower. Realistic implementation risk assessment should result in considering alternative institutional arrangements.

title: Guinea-Bissau - Biodiversity Conservation Project
report_name: Guinea-Bissau - Biodiversity Conservation Project
project_id: GW-Guinea-Bissau Biodiversity Conservation Project -- P122047
abstract: Ratings for the Biodiversity Conservation Project for Guinea-Bissau were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: intense and sustained supervision is appropriate for high-risk environments but does not need to be costly or always direct, face-to-face encounters with country counterparts and stakeholders. The project demonstrated how to ensure sustainable financing for the conservation of globally-important biodiversity in a situation of fiscal constraints. Guinea-Bissau is an emblematic example of uniting donor collaboration around a longer-term vision with the Bank as catalyst, providing relatively small financing with technical assistance to leverage the commitment and resources of diverse entities to achieve a weightier result. It is recommended that the Guinea-Bissau case be used to promote discussion in the Bank on how to ensure minimum financing for protected areas management in the many African countries where this continues to be a struggle despite the significant environmental services such areas provide. Stability of the Bank and Client core teams and the depth/mutual trust of their engagement have been highly advantageous. Such stability can have major advantages over time especially for technically challenging projects in difficult country conditions. The Bank and Client teams remain largely in place with a strong commitment to the over-arching vision, an excellent working relationship based on trust, and the benefit of institutional memory. It is recommended that this stability be preserved to the extent possible through continued Bank financial support until IBAP and the FBG are clearly able to walk on their own.

title: Gambia, The - Growth and Competitiveness Project
report_name: Gambia, The - Growth and Competitiveness Project
project_id: GM-The Gambia - Growth & Competitiveness -- P114240
abstract: Ratings for the Growth and Competitiveness Project for The Gambia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to global environment outcome was significant, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: (i) the focus was on business environment improvement and support to two economic clusters– horticulture and tourism; (ii) both the institutional design (Project Steering Committee and Tourism Marketing Committee) and targeted beneficiaries (out-growers, Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise (MSMEs), The Gambia Tourism and Hospitality Institute) demonstrate the emphasis on public-private partnerships; (iii) inter-ministerial coordination and ownership had some challenges in implementation due to frequent cabinet reshuffles that occurred during the project; (iv) establishing an independent Project Coordination Unit and building capacity of the Ministry of Economic Planning and Industrial Development (MEPID); and (v) Growth and Competitiveness Project (GCP) design built on existing initiatives regarding both sections of the Project Development Objective (PDO), the business environment improvement and economic cluster strengthening with the matching grant facility.

title: Guinea-Bissau - Rural Community-Driven Development Project
report_name: Guinea-Bissau - Rural Community-Driven Development Project
project_id: GW-Rural Community-Driven Development Project -- P090712
abstract: The Rural Community-Drive Development Project of Guinea-Bissau had a satisfactory outcome. Bank performance was also satisfactory. The M&E Quality was substantial. The project development objective was to increase access to priority basic economic and social infrastructure and services. The Project was restructured twice and received two additional financing and responded to the country’s emerging and emergency needs. The Project received total financing of US 38.5 million dollars, and the closing date was extended from a planned 5 years to 10 years and closed on September 20, 2019. Some recommendations and lessons learned during the project include: Looking beyond the government to stimulate local development; High-level commitment is essential for managing sensitive political issues; Close monitoring and supervision; and Phased rollout informed challenges and appropriate adjustments to be made before scaling-up.

title: Central African Republic - Support to Vulnerable Groups Community Development Project
report_name: Central African Republic - Support to Vulnerable Groups Community Development Project
project_id: CF-CF-Support to Vulnerable Groups Community Development Project -- P111679
abstract: Ratings for the Support to Vulnerable Groups Community Development Project for Central African Republic were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was high, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: community driven development in Fragile and Conflict Affected situations requires costly additional institutional arrangements to accompany communities during the process and may result in it not resulting in the same cost efficiency benefits, participatory management approaches as it is usually associated with. Impact evaluations are a key to learning from operations. However, in situations of great needs, a mismatch between the geographical scope and financing envelope of the project, and no sustained commitment for subsequent project phases, random selection of beneficiary communities is not easily understood or accepted by those communities that are left out. There was consensus among stakeholders that a more need-based selection and/or subsequent financing phases would have been preferred over the random selection. The impact evaluation was cancelled at the MTR, adding to the feeling of disappointment over the selection process. Country office based staff, notably on procurement, and frequent intense project support missions greatly contributed to the implementation, coordination and technical assistance of the project. ‘Inverse missions’ in which the client meets with the Bank mission in a third country, and frequent video-conferencing are crucial to continue Bank engagement during political crises such as the crisis that led to the triggering of OP 7.30 in 2013-14.

title: Niger - Second Phase of Adaptable Program Loan of the Community Action Program Project
report_name: Niger - Second Phase of Adaptable Program Loan of the Community Action Program Project
project_id: NE-COMMUNITY ACTION PROGRAM (PAC2) -- P102354,NE-Integrated Ecosystems Management in Niger (APL phase 2) -- P107841
abstract: Ratings for the Second Phase of Adaptable Program Loan (APL) of the Community Action Program Project for Niger were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, Global Environment Objectives (GEO) outcomes were satisfactory, risk to development outcome was moderate, risk to GEO outcome was moderate, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: bottom-up development, based on a community-driven approach, is promising, although it is more complicated to implement than more traditional approaches. It is difficult to plan and design successive phases of a longer-term program to prevent gaps from occurring between phases. As much as possible, however, such gaps should be avoided. Local participation is vital for involving people in development by discussing plans, agreeing on priorities, and gaining an understanding of how to implement planned activities and monitor the sustainability of their investments. It is the policy of the Bank and the Government of Niger to integrate development projects gradually into regular government activities and obviate the need for separate project coordination or management units. The implementation completion report (ICR) mission noted that the project was more of a decentralization and communal development project than an agricultural project, and it recommended that the issue of the supervisory ministry be reviewed during the appraisal of CAP-III. Participation in the economic micro projects for CAP-I required a contribution of 10-20 percent of micro project costs, making it impossible to accommodate the poorest people. It is more challenging to foster the kind of entrepreneurship needed to operate an economically successful farmer organizations. Entrepreneurial, economic, and commercial skills are not easily taught. They require not only classroom teaching but also follow-up instruction and coaching, often for years after the original training. To interest people in long-term natural resource management and environmental protection (including the maintenance of trees), it is absolutely necessary to give them concrete incentives, such as food or cash for work, and perhaps to be more supply-driven.

title: Togo - Community Development Project
report_name: Togo - Community Development Project
project_id: TG-TG-Community Dev. Project ERL (FY08) -- P110943
abstract: Ratings for the Community-Driven Development Project (CDD) for Togo were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the project was highly effective in providing a quick response to Togo's difficult situation after fifteen years of socio-economic decline. The experience showed that rapid impact on poverty in such a situation can be achieved by a CDD approach backed up by an institutional structure that empowers local communities by decentralizing decision taking and strengthens their capacity through sustained support and capacity building. The principal lesson is that effective poverty reduction through community-level initiatives requires well-structured support and implementation mechanisms together with intensive supervision and capacity building. In the particular case of Togo, the resulting recommendation is that the Government and Bank now need to focus on how to institutionalize, strengthen and finance the functions currently carried out by the central (Technical Secretariat) and regional (AGAIB) organizations to provide the continued investment finance, institutional and technical support and capacity building necessary to sustain the CDD momentum in communities already supported and to extend the CDD approach to all poor communities nationwide.

title: Benin - Competitiveness and Integrated Growth Opportunity Project
report_name: Benin - Competitiveness and Integrated Growth Opportunity Project
project_id: BJ-Competitiveness and Integrated Growth Opportunity Project (CIGOP) -- P104881
abstract: The Competitiveness and Integrated Growth Opportunity Project on Benin had a satisfactory overall outcome, a satisfactory performance by the Bank and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. The lessons learned were: (1) Project design should be simple and aligned with the Government’s priorities. (2) Sustained Government commitment and ownership are key to successful project implementation. (3) Institutional and implementation arrangements should be clear and transparent. (4) Dedicated CMU’s support plays a critical role in successful implementation. (5) Strong leadership by TTL, profound technical skills, strategic vision, integrity and innovative approach to problem solving are key characteristics contributing to a successful project restructuring. (6) Monitoring indicators in the Results Framework should be realistic in attribution and achievable during implementation. (7) Proactive supervision and monitoring by both the Government and the World Bank contribute to a successful project implementation. (8) Under some conditions, the World Bank had capacity for a successful turnaround or poorly performing projects.

title: Benin - Community Based Coastal and Marine Biodiversity Management Project
report_name: Benin - Community Based Coastal and Marine Biodiversity Management Project
project_id: BJ-Community-Based Coastal and Marine Biodiversity Management Project -- P071579
abstract: The Community Based Coastal and Marine Biodiversity Management Project for Benin received a moderately unsatisfactory outcome rating with substantial risk to global environment outcome. Both the Bank and the Borrower received moderately unsatisfactory performances as well. One restructuring took place during this project and includes the follows changes: a revision of project components and indicators to compensate for project implementation delays, an extension of the closing date by eighteen months, and a reallocation of proceeds to align project funding with redefined activities. Due to the restructuring the PDO indicators were revised but there was no change in the PDO itself. Component 2 of the project saw a few changes as a result of the restructuring as well. The number of CBCAs was reduced to three sites and funds were increased to extend project support to fringe communities. Lessons learned that were drawn and which could serve as guidance for similar projects in the future include the following: (1) The countrys lengthy and bureaucratic nature of the National Department of Public Procurement needs to be taken into account when designing projects. (2) The Project's PDO and indicators should be carefully reviewed during project preparation. A strong results framework including indicators that are realistic and directly linked to the PDO is fundamental in achieving envisaged project outcomes. (3) A strong M&E framework including a dedicated M&E officer is of paramount importance. (4) Government commitment at all levels is a crucial factor in achieving the Project's envisaged outcomes. The Bank should react promptly (i.e., revising the PDO or closing the Project) to any indications of diminishing commitment levels or other factors that indicate envisaged project outcomes may not be achieved. (5) Sufficient time needs to be allocated for stakeholder consultations and negotiations associated with the establishment of the CBCAs. (6) Financing mechanisms that ensure project outcomes will be sustained beyond the Project's lifetime are important to establish during project implementation. (7) A regional approach to managing coastal marine biodiversity should be fostered. (8) It is important to carefully review the Project's last ISR to avoid a possible disconnect with the ICR's rating. (9) The ICR review meeting should be conducted as early as possible to allow for sufficient time for revisions and government consultation prior to its submission to the Board.

title: Guinea - Second Phase of the Village Communities Support Program Project
report_name: Guinea - Second Phase of the Village Communities Support Program Project
project_id: GN-GN-Village Comm Supp Program - Phase 2 -- P065129
abstract: Ratings for the Second Phase of the Village Communities Support Program Project for Guinea were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the involvement of decentralized institutions in planning and implementing community activities does not necessarily mean that a decentralization policy will be adopted in the various sectors. Increasing the tax revenues available to the CRs is not simply the result of establishing market infrastructure or implementing income-generating activities; it also depends on the development of civil society and good governance at the local level. The regular transfer of financial resources to communities is essential to improve local competencies and ensure that local institutions are sustainable. Participatory budgeting and M&E are key project management tools that should be made available to local stakeholders to effectively drive the local development process. Ensuring inclusive local planning and development with a focus on gender and the marginalized groups will contribute to the full achievement of program goals.

title: Liberia - Agriculture and Infrastructure Development Project
report_name: Liberia - Agriculture and Infrastructure Development Project
project_id: LR-LR-Agriculture & Infrastructure Development  Project -- P104716
abstract: Ratings for the Agriculture and Infrastructure Development Project for Liberia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: government commitment for reforms secured early i.e. during project conceptualization is critical to successful implementation of institutional reforms. Free standing grants to support projects should be planned with similar objectives, project activities, and implementing arrangements in order to provide flexibility and efficiency in the use of funds. The failure to deliver the entire planned water system provides a good example of the inherent risk in having different funding sources delivering different components. Splitting up interdependent systems into separate packages to be financed by a single financing source is therefore critical for a satisfactory outcome and to avoid mismatched implementation schedules. A higher standard of due diligence is required in the Procurement of suppliers in weak operating environments to ensure selection of qualified suppliers who are most likely to perform satisfactorily.

title: Burkina Faso - Seventh, Eighth, Ninth, Tenth, and Eleventh Poverty Reduction Support Credits (PRSC) Project
report_name: Burkina Faso - Seventh, Eighth, Ninth, Tenth, and Eleventh Poverty Reduction Support Credits (PRSC) Project
project_id: BF-Poverty Reduction Support Credit (7) -- P099010,BF-Poverty Reduction Support Credit (8) -- P099011
abstract: Ratings for the Seventh, Eighth, Ninth, Tenth, and Eleventh Poverty Reduction Support Credits (PRSC) Project for Burkina Faso were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned include: budget support can be an effective instrument for governments to finance their development programs and to weather economic shocks. A second lesson is linked to understanding the political economy. Cotton reform was successful because of a positive consensus between the cotton ginning companies, the farmers associations, and the government ministries. A third lesson from the PRSC is the need to be selective and strategic in scope - narrowing the choice and number of policy reforms to support - but ambitious in depth of reforms and their significance for economic growth, employment, and poverty reduction. However, a lesson from policy in Burkina Faso in recent years has been that in the absence of broad-based economic growth and job creation, macro stability and social programs will not on their own have sufficient impact on poverty indicators. A fourth lesson is that more focus needs to be put into statistical capacity-building in order to strengthen monitoring and evaluation systems. A fifth lesson from past lending is the importance of trying to provide finance linked to the government's own budget cycle. A sixth lesson relates to the importance of donor coordination. The experience of Burkina suggests that a well-institutionalized system of donor coordination can facilitate the aid management for the country and ensure better development outcomes.

title: Niger - First and Second Rural and Social Policy Credit/Grant (RSRC-1 and RSRC-2) Project
report_name: Niger - First and Second Rural and Social Policy Credit/Grant (RSRC-1 and RSRC-2) Project
project_id: NE-Rural and Social Sector Policy Reform Credit 1 -- P096411,NE-Rural & Social Policy DPL II -- P098963
abstract: Ratings for the First and Second Rural and Social Policy Credit/Grant (RSRC-1 and RSRC-2) Project for Niger were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: design of the budget supports needs to be adapted to the institutional environment. In a weak institutional environment such as Niger, it is better to avoid a long list of prior actions, and focus instead on a short list of key actions and their cross-sectoral linkages and synergies. It is important to provide a medium-term perspective, particularly when addressing policy constraints in sectors, where results are not achieved in the short-term. In that respect, the decision to discontinue the series of three rural and social policy reform operations and move to other sectors before gains in the initial sectors are fully consolidated is perplexing. This decision may give the Government the wrong signal that pursuing the reforms in the human development area is no longer needed. The Development Policy Lending (DPL) series demonstrated that satisfactory outcomes can be achieved with strong commitment on the part of government and with Bank support designed to develop the needed capacity for implementation. This is particularly evident in the education, health, and to a lesser extent in agriculture sectors, where authorities are committed to improve capacities and increase efficiency of service delivery.

title: Burkina Faso - Second Phase of the Community Based Rural Development Project
report_name: Burkina Faso - Second Phase of the Community Based Rural Development Project
project_id: BF-Second Phase Community Based Rural Development Project, -- P098378
abstract: Ratings for the Second Phase of the Community Based Rural Development (CBRD) Project for Burkina Faso were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, risk to development outcome was low, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: effective harmonization of approaches around government policies is critical. The sustained success of the CBRD program as a whole reflects its efforts to support the government's evolving vision on decentralization. Achieving effective decentralization requires sustained and holistic support to champions within government and in civil society. Capacity building is more effective when it focuses on sustainable institutions and occurs through learning by doing. Involvement of the whole community, especially the poor and marginalized, is essential to ensure that ownership and commitment to local development efforts are maintained. Communication and collaboration are critical for ensuring community ownership of project activities and social cohesion. Monitoring and Evaluation (M and E) information flows can become overwhelming as a project's scale increases, its activities evolve, and new lessons continually emerge. A programmatic approach, with adequate flexibility to incorporate innovations and sufficient technical assistance for piloting and testing at each stage, is a key success factor for building the confidence of staff and communities, helping to fine-tune the model, and enhancing the prospects for achieving development outcomes.

title: Gambia - Community-Driven Development Project
report_name: Gambia - Community-Driven Development Project
project_id: GM-Community-Driven Development Project -- P082969
abstract: Ratings for the Community-Driven Development Project for Gambia were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: investment in capacity building at all levels is essential for localizing development and promoting local governance. Commitment to reforms and strong political will to decentralization is fundamental to projects that seek to support its implementation. It is not enough to just transfer management of resources to beneficiaries, strong oversight is needed to guard against elite capture, political hijacking or deviation from the bottom up approach. The project coverage was ambitious, even though this was meant to extend the coverage of the benefit to many communities as possible, may result in lower per capita benefit. Also, there are many unmet needs in the approved Strategic Development Plan (SDP) which could have led to greater impact in case the project will have covered less villages and wards. The way it is, impacts were spread rather thinly.

title: Burkina Faso - Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credits
report_name: Burkina Faso - Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credits
project_id: BF-Poverty Reduction Support Credit (6) -- P078996,BF-Poverty Reduction Support Credit (4) -- P078994,BF-Poverty Reduction Support Credit (5) -- P078995
abstract: Rating for the Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Poverty Reduction Support Credits (PRSC) for Burkina Faso are as follows: overall outcome of the all three PRSC's series was satisfactory, risk to development outcome for all three series was moderate, and the performance of the Bank and the Borrower was also rated satisfactory for all three series. Key lessons drawn from the implementation of PRSC-4 to 6 are follows: (i) country ownership. The use of the PRSC instrument to support implementation of the poverty reduction strategy has contributed to strengthen country ownership. Specifically, this PRSC series greatly reinforced the importance of the budget and the country's own capacity for the success of government policies; (ii) harmonization of budget support among donors. The creation of the Cadre General d'Organisation des Appuis Budgetaires (General Framework for Budget Support) (CGAB) and its unified framework for budget support and joint performance matrix provided an effective framework to harmonize the dialogue and efforts of Burkina Faso's development partners, including this PRSC series, with the government's own strategy. In particular, it allowed for early entry points in the policy dialogue with the government and the identification of corrective measures or solution to implementation bottlenecks. It also allowed for all actors to make good use of analytical work. Furthermore, close and continuous dialogue with the government allowed CGAB members to better understand the constraints on the reform process and build government ownership. This was particularly relevant for some complex reform areas, like cotton, decentralization, privatization and the reduction of factor costs; and (iii) primacy of the cotton sector. Recent developments in the cotton sector have highlighted the importance of the sector for the country's macroeconomic stability and growth, the need to adjust the government strategy in the cotton sector to reflect recent exogenous shocks and the importance of continued Bank involvement in this sector. The increased focus of the third PRSC series on reforms in the cotton sector reflects the need to flexibly adjust the strategic orientation and emphasis of the PRSCs. The lessons drawn from the experience with cotton sector reform also show the need to broaden the analysis of the risks inherent in the development of this sector.

title: Senegal - Integrated Marine and Coastal Resources Management Project
report_name: Senegal - Integrated Marine and Coastal Resources Management Project
project_id: SN-Integrated Marine and Coastal Resources Management Project -- P058367,SN-Integrated Marine and Coastal Resources Management Project -- P086480
abstract: Ratings for the Integrated Marine and Coastal Resources Management Project for Senegal were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; Global Environment Objective (GEO) outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was high; risk to GEO outcome was high; Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory; and borrower performance was also moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: the fundamental constraint of open access to the fisheries resources may remain the key obstacle to the sector achieving its potential. However, the project did successfully pilot a model for local management of targeted fisheries that can help address this constraint in the coastal waters. In this model, the project supported communities to establish private associations of fishers that were legally recognized, and then these associations developed regulations and plans for the management of targeted fisheries. For this model to become truly sustainable and fully address the fundamental constraint of open access to the resources, the legal and institutional framework will need to provide local users and associations not just the authority to manage (i.e. regulate) targeted fisheries, but also to limit access. Unless there is some scope for control of access to these fishing grounds that are locally managed, their success will also be their downfall as improved fisheries and higher catch rates will only attract more fishers from neighboring waters, so that overall exploitation increase to the point that the stock sizes and catch rates decrease to pre-project levels. Greater community involvement in identifying issues and solutions ensured greater ownership of initiatives, enhancing prospects for sustainability of new approaches. Formalizing the co-management proposals through legal agreements between community associations and government gave communities a voice and shared accountability with decision-makers. The ecosystems approach requires a long-term commitment over 10-15 years in a phased approach, particularly as it relates to biodiversity conservation. In the shorter term, modest gains are to be expected. The establishment of biosphere reserves requires broad and sustained consultation with stakeholders to engender a common vision. This would have worked better if it was managed by an entity associated with the biosphere reserves.

title: Guinea-Bissau - Coastal and Biodiversity Management Project
report_name: Guinea-Bissau - Coastal and Biodiversity Management Project
project_id: GW-Coastal and Biodiversity Management Project -- P083453,GW-Coastal and Biodiversity Management Project -- P049513
abstract: Ratings for the Coastal and Biodiversity Management Project for Guinea-Bissau were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; Global environmental objective (GEO) outcomes were also moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate; risk to GEO outcome was also moderate; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory; and borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: the sustainability of projects in countries like Guinea-Bissau benefits from multiple, donor-supported interventions over a lengthy period. A series of follow-up projects can be the most important means for securing sustainability, and since organizational and institutional capacity is generally modest and the problems immense, multiple projects which address 'segments' of large, complex sectors such as sustainable land management, environment and biodiversity, may also be the best course as reflected in new, related operations Stability of the Bank and borrower core teams is advantageous, albeit not easy to achieve. Such stability can have major advantages over time especially for technically difficult projects in troubled environments. Intense and sustained supervision is entirely appropriate for high-risk environments like Guinea-Bissau but does not need to be costly or always direct, face-to-face encounters with country counterparts. The Cufada Park experience was a milestone in the Bank's relationship with the Government, showing how the Bank can support the country's natural resource management agenda. Poor procurement performance is a serious constraint for projects in Guinea-Bissau due to the lack of in-country capacity. Mentoring arrangements need a formal structure with guidelines on responsibilities and expected outcomes. Building capacity and empowering community beneficiaries through continuous 'learning by doing' which extends well beyond the specific micro-project cycle is prudent and innovative.

title: Senegal - Electricity Services for Rural Areas Project
report_name: Senegal - Electricity Services for Rural Areas Project
project_id: SN-SN-GEF Elec Srvc for Rural Areas (FY05) -- P070530,SN-SN-Elec. Serv. for Rural Areas (FY05) -- P085708
abstract: Ratings for the Electricity Services for Rural Areas Project for Senegal were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; GEO outcomes were also moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate; risk to GEO outcome was also moderate; Bank performance was moderately satisfactory and borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: leveraging private sector investments for rural electrification is possible if the legal and regulatory framework includes certain incentives. Among the factors that drove interest from international power utilities are fiscal incentives and legal insurance included in the 1998 electricity law (98-29) and the investment code. Implementation of projects with innovative features takes time which should be properly accounted for at the design stage. A longer implementation timeframe should have been provided to account for the high learning curve and potential implementation challenges. Flexibility or pragmatism is needed in certain contexts to achieve results. Having a permanent dialogue/consultation platform is critical in accelerating the implementation of projects with multiple involved stakeholders.

title: Ghana - Land Administration Project
report_name: Ghana - Land Administration Project
project_id: GH-Land Administration Project -- P071157
abstract: Ratings for the Land Administration Project (LAP) for Ghana were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was low, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the key activities supported under LAP, ranging from merging and streamlining land agencies, systematic land titling and demarcation of customary boundaries to developing systems for resolving land conflicts, were merely laying the basis for a longer term engagement in reforming land administration. The success and sustainability of land reforms require a longer term perspective and support from development partners and other social actors to help the government overcome social and political inertia. Since the restructuring of the LAP and launching of LAP2, a longer-term perspective has been the key guide to planning and implementation. Donor commitment to long-term programs of land management and administration is essential to their success. The success of land projects and programs depends on long-term commitment, including a commitment to avoid the premature termination of disbursements.

title: Burkina Faso - Third Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Burkina Faso - Third Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: BF-Poverty Reduction Support Operation (3) -- P076908
abstract: The ratings suggest a satisfactory project outcome, likely sustainability, substantial institutional development impacts, and satisfactory performances by the Bank and Borrower. Lessons highlight that the Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC) series greatly reinforced the importance of the budget, and the country's own capacity for the success of government policies, steadily strengthening the focus on the budget as the main instrument for PRSP implementation. PRSCs also successfully supported a number of cross-cutting reforms. These reforms included the application of de-concentration policies in budget and personnel management, which are part of the policies to improve social service delivery. In the cotton sector, the opening of two zones for private investors, involved the establishment of the new regulatory framework, and the definition of roles of the new and old stakeholders. However, despite achievements, a number of challenges remain for the PRSCs. Burkina Faso faces difficulties in attracting private investment as a result of low skill levels, and high costs of production factors. The growth agenda of the PRSP should therefore be an additional important area for support under future PRSCs, as well as for the Country Assistance Strategy. Other lessons outline improving the PRSC-related sectoral dialogue; aligning budget support with the budget cycle; reinforcing capacities; improving the link between policy actions and the monitoring and evaluation framework; and, improving inter-ministerial coordination.

title: Niger - Community Action Project in Support of the First Phase of the Community Action Program Project
report_name: Niger - Community Action Project in Support of the First Phase of the Community Action Program Project
project_id: NE-Community-based Integrated Ecosystem Management -- P073011,NE-Community Action Program -- P065991
abstract: Ratings for the Community Action Project in Support of the First Phase of the Community Action Program Project for Niger were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, Global Environmental Objective (GEO) outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: bottom-up development based on a community-driven development (CDD) approach can be achieved in a context like Niger if supervision is careful and benefits from a dedicated team. The process is particularly difficult if public services and institutions outside the main cities are limited or non-existent (as was the case in Niger). For a new program in such a challenging field as community-driven development, it is necessary to have modest expectations at the beginning. In Niger, the Community Action Program had an outreach target of 20 percent of the communes (districts) for the first four years. For an emergency program the intended outcome is that the effects of the crisis are minimized and recovery from the disaster can start. For an avian flu attack the outcome would be that information is quickly shared with responsible authorities and that deaths are avoided or at least minimized. Thus the original outcome indicators in the Project Paper were correctly conceived when they largely concentrated on the reaction and reporting times by the local population and then by the authorities responsible for surveillance and treatment.

title: Burkina Faso - The Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
report_name: Burkina Faso - The Second Poverty Reduction Support Credit Project
project_id: BF-Poverty Reduction Support Credit (2) -- P075378
abstract: The Poverty Reduction Support Credit (PRSC)-(2) Project for Burkina Faso had the following ratings:  the outcome was satisfactory, the sustainability was likely, the institutional development impact was substantial and the Bank and borrower performance were both satisfactory.  The findings and implications for subsequent operation(s) in series indicate that overall, the satisfactory progress made since the approval of PRSC-2 warranted the move to PRSC-3; that PRSC-2 could not be also aligned with the budget cycle as previously sought, and this should be a priority in the next operation; and that more attention should be paid in the subsequent operations, starting with PRSC-1, on capacity building in line ministries given the focus on improving service delivery.

title: Ghana - Northern Savanna Biodiversity Conservation Project
report_name: Ghana - Northern Savanna Biodiversity Conservation Project
project_id: GH-Northern Savanna Biodiversity Conservation Project -- P067685
abstract: Ratings for the Northern Savanna Biodiversity Conservation Project for Ghana were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to global environment outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the institutional set-up for this project, i.e. an institution (the Savannah Resource Management Center) where staff from various governmental agencies (Ministry of Lands Forestry and Mines (MLFM), Forestry Commission/Wildlife Division, Ministry of Food and Agriculture (MOFA), Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)) were seconded, proved to be an effective framework for inter-institutional cooperation, and provided a model that could be replicated for the implementation of other multi-sectoral projects/programs. The fact that the Project Coordination Unit (PCU) was located in Tamale, in the Northern region, limited the capacity of the project team to effectively exert political influence and lobby for its interests. As a result, the component on the establishment of a policy framework was the one that had more difficulties in being implemented. One of the achievements of the project is the establishment of two biological corridors between Ghana and Burkina Faso to connect the two ecological blocks of the two countries and allow free movement of wildlife between these blocks. The creation of the corridors also resulted in the signature of a memorandum of understanding (MoU) between the two countries on the management of transfrontier resources.

title: Benin - Public Expenditure Reform Adjustment Program
report_name: Benin - Public Expenditure Reform Adjustment Program
project_id: BJ-PERAC -- P061577
abstract: The ratings for the project were as follows : the outcome was satisfactory, the sustainability was likely, the institutional development impact was modes, and the Bank and borrower performance were both satisfactory. The lessons learned indicate that budget reform is a long-term process that will take decades to succeed in most member countries. The main issue, at the design stage, is to select a few strategic kick-off activities and operational targets that can be completed/achieved in one or two years. The success of a long-term budget reform will obviously depend on the long-term commitment of both the government and its financiers. A multi-sector budget reform program needs to mobilize considerable support from the Bank and possibly other donors, particularly at the supervision stage. Introducing entirely new budget preparation and evaluation techniques may require a significant input of technical assistance to be financed by donors. Both the Budget Directorate and most of the other departments in the Ministry of Finance and Economy tend to give priority to what they see as their main operational and administrative functions and do not have the staff, the time and the capacity to guide other ministries in the design, execution and monitoring of program-budgets. For the short-term future, the Bank may also wish to consider the following recommendations: the next phase of Bank programs in support of budget reforms should first aim at consolidating initial results; the fact that new ministries want to join the program-budget process shows the vitality of the reform but also creates a major absorptive capacity constraint.

title: Burkina Faso - Community-Based Rural Development Project
report_name: Burkina Faso - Community-Based Rural Development Project
project_id: BF-COMMUNITY-BASED RURAL DEVELOPMENT PROJECT -- P035673
abstract: Ratings for the Community-Based Rural Development Project for Burkina Faso were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was low or negligible, the Bank performance was satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was highly satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: The project approach and the implementation procedures of the local investment fund (LIF) were very critical to give more responsibility to the different actors, those of government institutions, but particularly at the community level. This allowed a better ownership of local investments and will therefore increase awareness for appropriate maintenance and sustainability. The approach has proven that if local communities have been given the opportunity, they are capable to manage their own development and be at the center of the decision-making process. In addition, the active involvement of the government institutions in the project implementation contributed widely to their capacity development and will ensure the sustainability of the results achieved. The execution of community based programs necessitates a good knowledge of local conditions and the adoption of simple management procedures. Without a minimum of flexibility regarding for example the direct transfer of funds to communities and the confidence on self control mechanisms on funds disbursement, it will not be possible to execute a large number of micro-projects in a short term period.

title: Ghana - Urban Fifth Project in Support of the First Phase of the Urban Development Program
report_name: Ghana - Urban Fifth Project in Support of the First Phase of the Urban Development Program
project_id: GH-URBAN 5 -- P050624
abstract: The Fifth Urban Project for Ghana had the following ratings: the outcome was satisfactory, the sustainability was likely, the institutional development impact was modest, and the Bank and borrower performance were both satisfactory. The lessons learned indicate that many development tasks require more years to accomplish than the life of a typical investment project, and phased instruments are very effective to build relevant institutions, deal with initial capacity constrains, and cope with changing interests and priorities of borrowing countries. Linking investment funds to performance incentives to improve local government capacity through learning by doing stimulates competition and increases the chances of achieving long-term development effectiveness. Professionals with backgrounds in financial management and institutional development should be put in leadership positions at the national level in projects that promote the decentralization of government service provision and the development of local government capacity. There was insufficient performance monitoring and evaluation of Ghana's construction industry within government. Sub-projects should be chosen with the participation of the affected population and should be prioritized by the DAs in Medium Term Development Plans. Projects assisted by several donors should be coordinated at the District level.

title: Lebanon - Wheat Supply Emergency Response Project
report_name: NA
project_id: LB-Lebanon: Wheat supply emergency response project -- P178866
abstract: Ratings of Wheat Supply Emergency Response Project for Lebanon were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: Leverage private sector capacity; Invest in M&E and support it with digital tools; and Plan to minimize social unrest during subsidy removal.

title: Republic of Yemen - Desert Locust Response Project
report_name: NA
project_id: RY-Yemen Desert Locust Response Project -- P174170
abstract: Ratings of Desert Locust Response Project for Yemen were as follows: outcome was moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: Even under the most difficult conditions, it is possible to establish basic operational capacity in a relatively short period; Attention to the sustainability of project outcomes is needed from the outset; Operational flexibility in technical solutions is key to addressing emerging implementation challenges; Regional coordination is an essential integral element of locust control programs; and Strategic partnerships are vital for the World Bank Group’s model of engagement in conflict-affected settings.

title: Yemen - Smallholder Agricultural Production Restoration and Enhancement Project
report_name: Yemen - Smallholder Agricultural Production Restoration and Enhancement Project
project_id: RY-Smallholder Agricultural Production Restoration and Enhancement Project -- P162659
abstract: Ratings for the Smallholder Agricultural Production Restoration and Enhancement Project for Yemen were as follows: overall outcomes were satisfactory, bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: effective delivery was in large part due to the partnership and trust amongst all implementing agencies and the target communities. Implementation of the project through a highly professional UN agency (FAO) and a seasoned and well-respected national organization (SFD) was the most important factor in the successful delivery of the project largely as designed and within a short time frame. The use of local implementing partners with local knowledge and the trust of the population was also a key factor. Another strongly positive factor was the proactive involvement of the beneficiary communities throughout the implementation process, an involvement strengthened by empowerment of local people through the CDD approach, community contracting, and job and income creation through cash for work. These lessons have been incorporated in the follow up FSRRP. The recommendation for comparable FCV situations, particularly where little or no reliance can be placed on government services, is to select experienced and reputable humanitarian and development agencies with tried and tested presence on the ground and, if possible, trusted local partners and to build reciprocal relations of trust with communities not only through a flow of benefits but also through empowering communities with responsibilities. This approach can also build institutional capacity for service delivery even where public services are largely absent. The clear targeting and project selection criteria were a strong factor in gaining support for the project. These criteria were not only set objectively based on the best available knowledge but were acceptable both to the authorities and to the beneficiary communities. A similar approach has been adopted for FSRRP. The general lesson, and implicit recommendation, is that clarity, fairness, and community involvement in setting criteria and selecting beneficiaries can win broad acceptance of a project even in the most difficult FCV contexts.

title: Tunisia - Second Natural Resources Management Project
report_name: Tunisia - Second Natural Resources Management Project
project_id: TN-Tunisia Second Natural Resources Management Project -- P086660
abstract: Ratings for the Second Natural Resources Management Project for Tunisia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: there should be an institutionally-mandated process for reassessing projects in rapidly deteriorating conflict and fragile environments. In situations of sudden conflict and fragility of governance it is imperative that mandatory assessments be carried out to review projects for continued relevance of the PDO and theory of change, activities and budgets, adequacy of implementation and institutional arrangements, M&E and data collection arrangements, etc. These reviews should determine the necessity for and scale of changes required to reset projects on the course of successful implementation (or alternatively cancellation). A deeper focus on access to finance and markets should be an indispensable element of implementing IGAs. While providing training, technical support and assets to IGA beneficiaries is an essential first phase, lack of access to finance and markets as they grow dampens their growth prospects and affects sustainability. While there are no easy solutions, developing appropriate agri-finance instruments and addressing constraints for access should be part of the design of projects such as NRMP2. It is equally important to work with farmers on linkages to markets as part of developing the business concept of an IGA. To this end, income-generating activities could benefit from specific market studies, which would guide beneficiaries in their attempt to link up with aggregators and value chains. The wealth of experience in applying the IPA in the current Tunisian context should be thoroughly considered in the implementation of new and up-coming operations such as the Integrated Land Management Project and the Irrigated Agriculture Intensification Project. Both projects have the IPA as part of their implementation construct and it would be highly advisable that links between proponents of these two projects be established with NRMP2 stakeholders. In the context of worsening climate conditions in resource-starved areas, where conflicts of interest are likely to increase over the use of water, pasture and forest resources, the IPA experience from PGRN2 could help stave off potential disagreements and social conflict.

title: Yemen - Rainfed Agriculture and Livestock Project
report_name: Yemen - Rainfed Agriculture and Livestock Project
project_id: RY-Rainfed Agriculture and Livestock Project -- P089259
abstract: The Rain-fed Agriculture and Livestock Project for Yemen received a moderately satisfactory outcome with substantial risk to development outcome. Both the Bank and the Borrower received moderately satisfactory performance ratings for their part in the project. The IDA Development Credit Agreement (DCA) was restructured three times. The first restructuring introduced changes which: (a) reinstated the geographical coverage of the project to the original five governorates, following an agreement with the International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD) to provide to bridge, inter alia, the remaining expenditures under Component 3 of the Project; and (b) modified the procurement thresholds. The second restructuring: (a) adjusted the scope of the project activities, and reallocated the proceeds across the modified activities (including the assignment of the unallocated fund in the IDA DCA; (b) modified the project implementation arrangements; (c) removed all procurement thresholds from the DCA and transferred these to the procurement plan; and (d) extended the project closing date by 27 months. The third restructuring of the IDA Credit aligned the PDO in the legal agreement with that in the PAD, and revised and enhanced the result framework and monitoring of the Project. Several lessons were learned from this project including the following: (1) in low-income fragile and conflict-affected states (FCS), for efficient project implementation under limited administrative capacity, develop a simple project design that promotes active participation of the beneficiaries, (2) create local-level coordination early because it may develop faster than that at the national level, (3) assess implementation capabilities carefully, including capabilities to use languages for procurement or to receive training, (4) assign the construction of large facilities to other entities when the implementing agency usually does not handle such large constructions, (5) improve evaluation designs so that PADs analysis could be replicated at completion.

title: Republic of Yemen - Sana'a Basin Water Management Project
report_name: Republic of Yemen - Sana'a Basin Water Management Project
project_id: RY-Sana'a Basin Water Management Project -- P064981
abstract: Ratings for the Sana'a Basin Water Management Project for the Republic of Yemen were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: projects should not underestimate the difficulty in implementing activities in the water sector in Yemen given the number, organizational structure, and weakness in capacity of Government of Yemen (GOY) institutions involved in Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM). Demonstration of new irrigation technologies to improve irrigation efficiency in pilot farms was very effective in expanding modern irrigation in rural Yemen. Projects should avoid establishing legal conditions or requirements that are clearly beyond the scope of the project to deliver. Timely action will have been better in recognizing the need for amending the Development Credit Agreement (DCA) when it was decided (2007) to consider farmer installation of localized irrigation equipment using equipment supply contracts, with outcome-based incentive structure for farmers. This new mechanism for transferring the responsibility of installing conveyance and distribution system to farmers worked very well with improvement in the pace of installation. It is crucial for the Management to be involved in resolving the institutional issues of the Government in the early stage of the project implementation. GOY officials should be fully involved from project identification to appraisal phases. Involvement of national consultants during these stages as counterparts to the foreign consultants would lighten the burden of GOY officials but the chance of on-the-job training of GOY officials would be lost, which would result in delay in project start-up due to lack of capacity.

title: Tunisia - Water Sector Investment Loan Project
report_name: Tunisia - Water Sector Investment Loan Project
project_id: TN-TN-Water Sector Investment -- P035707
abstract: Ratings for the Water Sector Investment Loan Project for Tunisia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the outputs of this project are significant and exceed expectations. Yet it is not possible to show that those investments generated their intended benefits because the objectives and results indicators do not reflect the activities that absorbed the greatest share of the funding. And the objectives that the project did have are too imprecisely defined to be useful to stimulate better integration, knowledge sharing and institutional change and as such represent a missed opportunity. This imprecision allowed those managing the project to focus on the component outputs rather than the overall strategic objective. Greater attention should have been given to social aspects in the design and implementation of the project which would probably have helped to improve the performance of certain investments. For example, the demand for individual household water supply connections should have better estimated. Also, the complexity of socio-economic issues should have been better addressed in the context of water resource management issues. Reducing consumption of groundwater is essentially an exercise in changing incentives to farmers and thus the groundwater management components would have benefited from the advice of a rural sociologist. Similarly, technical assistance supporting the associations of farmers managing irrigation schemes and of households managing rural water supply would have benefited from support from qualified sociologist.

title: Morocco - First and Second Development Policy Loan in Support of the Plan Maroc Vert
report_name: Morocco - First and Second Development Policy Loan in Support of the Plan Maroc Vert
project_id: MA-First Development Policy Loan in support of the Plan Maroc Vert -- P116557,MA-Second DPL in support of the Plan Maroc Vert -- P127822
abstract: The First and Second Development Policy Loan in Support of the Plan Maroc Vert received a moderately satisfactory outcome rating with significant risk to development outcome. The Bank received a satisfactory rating for its performance while the Borrower received a moderately satisfactory performance rating. No restructurings took place during the life of this project. Country developments led to three changes in the agreed prior actions for the second operation. These changes were: (a) the substitution of an agreement on the management model at Berkane for the previously proposed action on three wholesale market pilot projects. The concept remained the same but essentially the target was reduced from three to one. (b) The addition of the executive decision on market liberalization for red meat to the proposed action on concession to the private sector of modernized municipal slaughterhouses. (c) The substitution of the transmission of the draft laws creating the Regional Agriculture and Rural Development Offices (ORDARs) for the previously proposed action raising irrigation tariffs. There were several lessons learned from this project including the following: (1) A development policy loan (DPL) works best when it stems from a home-grown reform program with strong ownership within government. (2) A further strong value of Bank support under a DPL is to help create a platform for agencies that otherwise may have divergent agendas. (3) The linking of a DPL to a sectoral medium-term expenditure framework (MTEF) aligns incentives for both the finance ministry and the line ministry, and provides investment resources to accompany and help implement reforms that otherwise might not take place or might not yield their intended returns. (4) In the case of Morocco, submission of draft legislation to the Government General Secretary (SGG) may be inadequate assurance that a reform will be promptly executed. (5) Evaluation of a DPO is constrained by the short time scale, particularly for a two loan series, where the final evaluation occurs only twelve months after the second set of prior actions. (6) There is a need  and an opportunity  to accompany some reforms with project-style guidance and investment.

title: Djibouti - Rural Community Development and Water Mobilization Project
report_name: Djibouti - Rural Community Development and Water Mobilization Project
project_id: DJ-DJ-Rural Community Development and Water Mobilization Project (PRODERMO) -- P117355
abstract: The overall outcome of the Rural Community Development and Water Mobilization Project for Djibouti is highly satisfactory, with satisfactory Bank performance and substantial monitoring and evaluation quality. The Project provided the following lessons: (a) A robust Results Framework (Theory of Change) and sequencing of activities were critical for the project’s success; (b) The community-based, territorial approach was the right tool to achieve the expected results; (c) Beneficiaries’ contribution to Income-generating activities (IGAs) strengthened their ownership of these activities and the likelihood of success and (d) Accessible innovation and advisory services are key for sustainable agriculture and livestock development.

title: Tunisia - Fourth Northwest Mountainous Forested Areas Development Project
report_name: Tunisia - Fourth Northwest Mountainous Forested Areas Development Project
project_id: TN-4th Northwest Mountainous & Forested Areas Development Project (PNO4) -- P119140
abstract: Ratings for the Fourth Northwest Mountainous and Forested Areas Development Project for Tunisia  were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Monitoring and evaluation quality performance was modest. Some lessons learned included: The Fourth Northwest Mountainous and Forested Areas Development Project (PNO4) IPA has successfully confirmed that combining anti-erosion investments with investments in improving rural populations’ socioeconomic conditions is an effective way to leverage more attention from beneficiaries to natural resources management. Developing appropriate agri-finance resources and instruments, as well as addressing constraints for smallholder farmers to access microfinance, are at the core of the sustainability and replicability of projects such as PNO4. Participatory and community-driven projects have spearheaded initiatives which can contribute to the country’s on-going decentralization efforts.

title: Yemen, Republic of - Fisheries Resource Management and Conservation Project
report_name: Yemen, Republic of - Fisheries Resource Management and Conservation Project
project_id: RY-Fisheries Resource Management and Conservation -- P086886
abstract: Ratings for the Fisheries Resource Management and Conservation Project for Yemen, Republic of were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was significant, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: generating increased economic benefits and poverty reduction from fisheries depends first on a sustainable supply of fish and second, on the quality of the value chain and reduction of post-harvest losses, access to markets, etc. In the project, while infrastructure investments were completed, the governance and resource management areas were only partially successful. The Bank should seek means to tie disbursements for infrastructure and value chain improvements more closely to progress on governance and management reforms in the context of a programmatic approach with Yemen's other technical and financial partners. Outlining such issues in ISRs and management letters enables Bank management to take strong action that can lead to timely and dramatic results, as taken under the project, leading to renewed commitment by Government of Yemen (GOY), and the signing of large contracts.

title: Tunisia - Agricultural Support Services Project
report_name: Tunisia - Agricultural Support Services Project
project_id: TN-AGRICULTURAL SUPPORT SERVICES PROJECT -- P005750
abstract: Ratings for the Agricultural Support Services Project for Tunisia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, the Bank performance was moderately unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory.  Some lessons learned included: the project concentrated too much on cooperatives as a vehicle to help farmers. It neglected private marketing intermediaries, which were already providing some of the desired services (advice and help on variety selection, inputs, cultivation, harvest, post harvest handling, help on marketing, pest control, and in some cases credit). An analysis should have been done to identify several value chains for each product. Then each sector should have received assistance, beginning at the farm level, by looking to the end market and finding out exactly what buyers would pay for. Farm level actions could have been subsidized in part at the outset for newly created value chains, moving toward cost recovery after a transition period for the private sector value chains as they became well established. Competitiveness of Tunisian products is a matter of both quality and price. Quality standards are now better controlled thanks to several activities of the project through partnerships between importers and Tunisians processors, improved regulations, better laboratory infrastructure, and information management. The remaining challenge is to maintain a price that is competitive for both the increasingly demanding local market and the export market. However, the marketing of agricultural produce is currently characterized by controls of some food prices at the wholesale level, administrative controls of marketing margins and insufficient clarity in wholesale market transactions. This environment does not provide the right incentives for producers to address quality issues, as quality is often associated with higher costs that they might not be able to recuperate. Unless the current marketing practices do not change, improvements in quality as a result of better service delivery will not materialize.

title: Iran - Alborz Integrated Land and Water Management Project
report_name: Iran - Alborz Integrated Land and Water Management Project
project_id: IR-IRAN - Alborz Integrated Land and Water Management Project -- P071170
abstract: Ratings for the Alborz Integrated Land and Water Management Project for Iran were as follows: outcomes were unsatisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM) is an effective way to promote coordination between the many and diverse government ministries and institutions involved in watershed management, irrigation and drainage, agriculture and forestry, and municipal water supply within a basin. It is imperative to fully identify land acquisition requirements before commencement of project implementation and to complete land acquisition as soon as possible after the start of the project to avoid implementation delays. It is imperative that irrigation and drainage projects have up-to-date detailed civil works designs with up-to-date project design cost estimates before loan effectiveness so that tendering can start as soon as loan effectiveness is declared to avoid cost increases for civil works. A strong central Monitoring and Evaluation (M and E) system within the lead implementation agency should be standard in large projects with multiple agencies involved so that problems are detected early and signaled to the bank and to the government. Capacity building through adequate training of both staff and senior management at the outset, so that the relevance and importance of M and E to project implementation is understood, is key to M and E success. Financial and economic analysis, aside from the rate of return number itself, provides valuable information in the form of various indicators (yield, income) that can be tracked from appraisal through to project closing and that show how the main beneficiaries (farmers) take part and benefit from the project.

title: Egypt - Farm-level Irrigation Modernization Project
report_name: Egypt - Farm-level Irrigation Modernization Project
project_id: EG-EGYPT-Farm-level Irrigation Modernization -- P117745
abstract: Ratings for the Farm-level Irrigation Modernization Project for Egypt were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation (M&E) was modest. Some of the lesson learned: On-farm irrigation modernization through improvement of pumping stations and of quaternary canals, coupled with enhanced advisory services to farmers to improve water use and productivity, is feasible at a large scale and beneficial, both to end users and the economy; For the areas targeted by Farm-level Irrigation Modernization Project (FIMP), the main recommendations for follow up are: (i): expand the Farmer field school (FFS) approach to enhance the agricultural gains from improved irrigation modernization; (ii) develop or organize markets with smallholders (and their associations), traders and large commercial farms and support associated infrastructure and capacity building to enable a shift to higher value crops and access to markets; (iii) further improve soils and test other on-farm irrigation methods (bubblers, drip, etc.) that may save more water and be more suitable to fruit and vegetables and other high value crops; and (iv) broaden the role of Water User Association (WUAs) – in partnership with Ministry of Water Resources and Irrigation (MWRI) notably by training WUAs in governance, simple accounting and management in order to make them even more sustainable.For the remaining areas to be covered under GoE’s 5 million feddan program of irrigation modernization in the Old Lands, the main take away lessons and recommendations deriving from FIMP are: (i) support for on farm irrigation modernization along the lines of what FIMP implemented was successful, but technical design, construction and contract management inefficiencies need to be better addressed (summarized below and detailed in Annex 6); (ii) explore ways to possibly reduce investment costs and speed up/facilitate civil works on a much wider scale, for example through the use of trenchless machines for burying uPVC pipes (which would necessitate larger contracts to absorb amortization costs of such equipment by contractors) and reducing standards of uPVC pipes (from 4 to 2.5 bar) if cost-savings can be achieved in doing so; (iv) there is need for a comprehensive approach to modernizing the irrigation system—starting from main canals and ending with on-farm modernization. Both on-farm and off-farm irrigation modernization are important, and implementing them in close coordination is critical for the success of the overall program and the achievement of higher-level objectives, notably poverty reduction, maximizing food security and improving water resources management.

title: Morocco - Modernization of Irrigated Agriculture in the Oum Er Rbia Basin Project
report_name: Morocco - Modernization of Irrigated Agriculture in the Oum Er Rbia Basin Project
project_id: MA-MA-Modernization of Irrigated Agriculture in the Oum Er Rbia Basin -- P093719
abstract: The Modernization of Irrigated Agriculture in the Oum Er Rbia Basin of the Middle East and North Africa had a moderately satisfactory outcome, moderately satisfactory performance by the Bank and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. The lessons learned were: (1) Careful selection of irrigation sectors to modernize. (2) Long duration of works typical of modernization in large scale irrigation calls for long implementation period. (3) Targeting small farmers calls for tailored procedures. (4) Drip irrigation requires an efficient water service. (5) Design of the network should be done having its operation in mind. (6) Performance-based indicators are key to the management of service delivery improvement.

title: Tunisia - Second Water Sector Investment Project
report_name: Tunisia - Second Water Sector Investment Project
project_id: TN-Second Water Sector Investment -- P095847
abstract: Ratings for the Second Water Sector Investment Project for Tunisia were as follows: outcomes were unsatisfactory, the risk to development outcome was substantial, the Bank performance was unsatisfactory, and the Borrower performance was also unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the project will have greatly benefited from a solid institutional analysis. Such an analysis, possibly in the form of ASA, will have provided all stakeholders with a comprehensive, level playing field. Often, it makes sense to do a portfolio-wide institutional assessment, particularly as most public sector staff have the same incentives and face similar issues and challenges. During preparation and implementation of an irrigation project, assess carefully the amount of water available over time. Several irrigation sub-projects recorded lower water availability compared to the forecast in the detailed study. In some new perimeters, the target area was increased following the detailed study due to pressing demand from the population. In both cases, the water availability per hectare was reduced, limiting the opportunity for the farmers to intensify their production and thus find in agriculture a way out of poverty. While it is true that sometimes more farmers benefitted from the project, many of them remained in a situation of semi-subsistence agriculture.

title: Maroc – Pour un prêt à l’appui de politique de développement
report_name: Morocco - First Water Sector Policy Development Loan Project
project_id: MA-First Water Sector Development Policy Loan -- P095840
abstract: Les notes attribuées au Premier projet de prêt à l’appui des politiques de développement dans le secteur de l’eau étaient les suivantes : les résultats étaient modérément satisfaisants, le ratio risque-développement était modéré, la performance de la Banque mondiale a été jugée satisfaisante et la performance de l’emprunteur a été jugée modérément satisfaisante. Les leçons apprises comprennent : le succès d’un tel programme de réforme substantiel et à vaste portée tel que celui soutenu par cette série programmatique de prêt à l’appui des politiques de développement nécessite que tous les principaux acteurs et sous-secteurs de la réforme continuent à s’approprier la réforme et qu’un solide parrain soit en place pour diriger durablement le programme de réforme. Ce prêt à l’appui de politiques de développement ainsi que les travaux analytiques de grande qualité et les dialogues sur les politiques qui l’ont étayé ont contribué à amorcer un processus de changements profonds dans le secteur de l’eau au Maroc. Même si tous les objectifs n’ont pas été atteints, bon nombre de gains solides semblent avoir été enregistrés et peuvent même avoir eu des retombées positives sur d’autres secteurs.
