De l’avis de bon nombre d’autorités sectorielles, la préparation du prêt à l’appui des politiques de développement a fourni un forum sans précédent permettant aux parties prenantes de dialoguer sur un ordre du jour coordonné et complet de la réforme du secteur et de produire un ambitieux programme de réforme à vaste portée. En ce sens, il semble que le processus du prêt à l’appui des politiques de développement ait insufflé dans le secteur de l’eau un dynamisme qui a perduré au-delà de la durée de cette opération. Le programme de prêt à l’appui des politiques de développement a peut-être sous-estimé le temps nécessaire pour établir un consensus au sujet des règlements et décrets et pour que ces derniers soit adoptés par les autorités appropriées. Étant donné que tout changement apporté à une structure de gouvernance existante est vouée à rencontrer une résistance de la part de ceux dont les intérêts sont à risque, une participation continue de toutes les parties et une communication ouverte devraient être maintenues en tout temps.

title: Morocco - First Water Sector Policy Development Loan Project
report_name: Morocco - First Water Sector Policy Development Loan Project
project_id: MA-First Water Sector Development Policy Loan -- P095840
abstract: Ratings for the First Water Sector Policy Development Loan Project for Morocco were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: the full success of such a broadly scoped and substantial reform program as was supported through this Development Policy Loan (DPL) programmatic series requires that reform ownership remains strong among all key actors and sub-sectors involved, and that a strong champion be in place to sustainably lead the reform program. This DPL and the high quality analytical work and policy dialogue around it have helped to launch a process of far-reaching changes in the water sector in Morocco. Although all of the objectives may not have been achieved, many gains appear to be solidly established and may even carry cross-sectoral benefits. The DPL preparation, in the opinion of many sector officials, provided an unprecedented forum for all key stakeholders to pursue a dialogue on a coordinated and comprehensive sector reform agenda and to produce a broadly scoped and ambitious reform program. In that sense, it seems that the DPL process has created dynamism in the sector which will last beyond the immediate timeframe of this DPL operation. The DPL program may have underestimated the time that is required to develop consensus around by-laws and decrees and their adoption by the appropriate authorities. Because any change to an existing governance structure is bound to be met with resistance from those whose interests are at risk, continued involvement of all parties and open communications should be maintained at all times.

title: Egypt - Integrated Irrigation Improvement and Management Project
report_name: Egypt - Integrated Irrigation Improvement and Management Project
project_id: EG-EGYPT-Integrated Irrigation Improvement and Management Project -- P073977
abstract: Ratings for the Integrated Irrigation Improvement and Management Project for Egypt were as follows: outcomes were moderately unsatisfactory, risk to global environment outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: (i) Design projects more realistically by limiting outcomes to project’s direct influence; (ii) Be ready for implementation with priority contracts ready to be tendered; (iii) Irrigation management and infrastructure improvements must go hand in hand; (iv) Beneficiaries’ participation enhance benefits; (v) Procurement of civil works and contract management could be improved with larger procurement packages; (vi) Transparency and accountability are core elements of performance management and service delivery; and (vii) Sustainability beyond maintenance of works may require legal changes.

title: Yemen - Groundwater and Soil Conservation Project
report_name: Yemen - Groundwater and Soil Conservation Project
project_id: RY-Groundwater and Soil Conservation Project -- P074413
abstract: Ratings for the Groundwater and Soil Conservation Project (GSCP) for Yemen were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was substantial; Bank performance was satisfactory and borrower performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: the project was designed on the assumption that reducing extraction of water from an aquifer and reducing losses between the pump and the crop will make more water available for other uses. The main weakness of project design was that the project's main instrument governing water extraction (the tripartite agreement) was a contract with individual farmers while the regulation of extraction needed to be broadened to encompass the entire population of stakeholders using the groundwater aquifer. The implementation of small works by the communities through 'community contracts' was found to be a great success in terms of quality of works and creation of the sense of ownership by the communities involved. The GSCP has helped develop and demonstrate institutional and technical methods that can help slow aquifer depletion, but for areas where affordable groundwater stocks are being exhausted, assistance should also be provided to help farmers cope with the transition to non-irrigated livelihoods.

title: Yemen - Irrigation Improvement Project
report_name: Yemen - Irrigation Improvement Project
project_id: RY-IRRIGATION IMPROVEMENT PROJECT -- P062714
abstract: Ratings for the Irrigation Improvement Project for Yemen are as follows: outcomes of the project were highly moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was substantial, bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: improvement of spate irrigation management needs not only hardware interventions (rehabilitation and modernization of infrastructure) but also institutional development and information management measures. The project's Participatory Irrigation Management (PIM) and Agriculture Demonstration Program (ADP) components have resulted in very tangible progress towards achieving sustainable and efficient spate irrigation management and increasing agricultural productivity and rural incomes. To further develop and greatly extend these successes, they should be enhanced and replicated in other promising wadis under the recently approved Water Sector Support Project (WSSP). For future comparable projects including the WSSP, International Development Association (IDA), other donors and Government of Yemen (GoY) should specifically assure the financial sustainability of, and the modalities needed for, adequate Operation and Maintenance (O&M) of provided works and procured equipment.

title: Egypt - Second National Drainage Project
report_name: Egypt - Second National Drainage Project
project_id: EG-The Second National Drainage Project -- P045499
abstract: Ratings for the Second National Drainage Project for Egypt were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to development outcome was moderate, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and Borrower performance was moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: integrated management of irrigation and drainage (I and D) on sound institutional and financial arrangements should not be left as an unresolved issue. This requires the support of the ministry of water resources and irrigation (MWRI) and all the institutions in the sector, not only the Egyptian Public Authority for Drainage Projects (EPADP). Project design should be realistic and appropriate to the institutional capacity of the client, taking into consideration the qualifications and skills of the staff. Capacity building and institutional support if considered important and reflected in the objectives should also be reflected in the key performance indicators even if mainly financed through parallel means. Training provided on procurement and contract management should be sufficient and conducted regularly for the client staff, particularly considering the high turnover of staff. Complete the tendering process as early as possible, particularly toward the end of the project, to have enough time to complete the tendering and bidding requirements on time and to re-tender in case of failure due to some reasons. Supervision should be more proactive toward the implementation of the nonstructural components of the project, especially those activities aiming at institutional development of the implementing agency. The private sector should be engaged and fears of monopolies and financial risks should be analyzed and appropriate regulatory measures proposed, including risk sharing by both the government and the private sector.

title: Morocco - Strengthening Agri-Food Value Chains Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MA-Strengthening Agri-Food Value Chains -- P158346
abstract: Ratings for the Strengthening Agri-Food Value Chains Project for Morocco were as follows: overall efficacy is rated modest, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality is rated modest. Some lessons learned included the following: for activities requiring coordination and agreement among multiple stakeholders, the underlying institutional and implementation arrangements should be based on prior technical studies at the program's outset. Several of the Program’s technically innovative activities involving the construction of infrastructure and the operationalization of new services/management models (establishment of the CIA, construction of the wholesale market, and the Ceratitis center) were delayed as the related governance agreements between the designated implementing entities were put in place without the prior agreement of the involved parties on specific operational aspects and sufficient grounding in technical studies. Efforts to develop financing and operational arrangements were not started until advanced construction stages. Technical studies clarifying operational and financial elements should be completed before the construction phase initiation and based on realistic assessments of the involved entities’ economic and technical capacity. The Bank can help support robust operational arrangements from the outset through appropriate covenants or DLIs. The time between completion and operationalization of facilities with sensitive equipment, such as the center for the production of sterilized keratitis, should be minimal. Ensuring a seamless transition between equipment installation (e.g., air conditioning, filtering, ventilation systems, or other equipment with high maintenance needs) and operationalization can prevent deterioration and subsequent need for costly refitting and repairs. Related to the rearing of insects for biological pest control, it is key to ensure careful calibration and stress testing of the air conditioning system to provide sufficiently stable and conducive conditions for breeding activities.

title: Jordan - Horticultural Exports Promotion and Technology Transfer Project
report_name: Jordan - Horticultural Exports Promotion and Technology Transfer Project
project_id: JO-Horticultural Exports Promotion and Technology Transfer Project -- P076961
abstract: Ratings for the Horticultural Exports Promotion and Technology Transfer Project for Jordan were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; the risk to development outcome was substantial; the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory; and the Borrower performance was unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: The first lesson is that a clustering approach emphasizing collaboration among members of a grouping to achieve objectives that are beyond the capabilities of individual farmers or exporters is a requirement in horticulture. Delivery of services for promotion - research and extension - remains an important role for the public sector. However, to keep up with the continual changes in the horticultural market, the public sector cannot afford to stay behind in terms of service quality. Experience in the project suggests also that improving marketing and production requires a good mix of both soft and hard assets. Performance evaluation has proved to be a challenging task for the project. There is a need for a more systematic approach in assessing performance if Export Promotion and Development (EPD) activities are to have a substantial impact on Jordan's horticulture competitiveness.

title: Tunisia - Integrated Landscapes Management in Lagging Regions Project
report_name: NA
project_id: TN-Integrated Landscapes Management in Lagging Regions Project -- P151030
abstract: NA

title: Tunisia - Second Export Development Project
report_name: Tunisia - Second Export Development Project
project_id: TN-TN-Export Development II -- P071115
abstract: Ratings for the Second Export Development Project (EDP II) for Tunisia were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the risk to development outcome was negligible to low, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the Borrower performance was satisfactory. The main lesson to retain is striving to find a balance between the need to improve synergies among project components and coordination particularly with regards to the flow of information and administrative duties between project components and the coordination unit. Implementing units reported a lack of integration with other project components, noting they often had limited contact with other project components despite the significant cross-over in the work being pursued. The revolution coupled with economic deterioration due to the global economic recession had a profound impact on this project. Consultants and independent entities may be recruited by Government to help achieve outcomes more efficiently, however, without building public sector capacity, long term sustainability may be sacrificed if Government is unable to implement activities once the project is closes. At the time of writing, a third EDP project is being prepared that will seek to achieve greater sustainability by integrating matching grant activities within Center for Export Promotion (CEPEX) structures.

title: Tunisia - Communications for Policy Reforms Project
report_name: Tunisia - Communications for Policy Reforms Project
project_id: TN-Tunisia - Communications for policy reforms -- P144917
abstract: Ratings for the Communications for Policy Reforms Project for Tunisia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, risk to global environment outcome was moderate, Bank performance was satisfactory, and Grantee performance was moderately unsatisfactory. Some lessons learned included: (i) When a project is conceived to be implemented by a private sector entity operating in the market the first question to be answered is whether the demand exists for the product, and an adequate market analysis should be conducted; (ii) If there are delays in some project components there may be a need to revisit the design and implementation plan for the entire project; (iii) The trade-off between complexity and accessibility should be accepted when conceiving a similar project; (iv) In terms of human resources, it seems that the Bank underestimated the needs with the consequence of putting the small recipient team under excessive pressure; (v) It is important to have the capacity to delegate on operational functions; (vi) Another aspect to be considered is that for a marketing campaign to be successful it needs to influence other public media and public figures; (vii) Despite the limited capacity of the recipient, the decision to use a local actor for a similar communication project was an excellent decision; and (viii) The risk of the Bank as an institution trying to influence public opinion should always be considered, but this should not be a deterrent for engaging in substantive communication initiatives.

title: Morocco - Integrated Disaster Risk Management and Resilience Program-for-Results Project
report_name: NA
project_id: MA-Integrated Disaster Risk Management and Resilience Program -- P144539
abstract: Ratings for the Integrated Disaster Risk Management (DRM) and Resilience Program-for-Results (PforR) Project for Morocco were as follows: outcome was highly satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: comprehensive program design and government commitment enabling simultaneous efforts on all fronts - institutional, investment and policy - is required to uplift DRM and disaster risk financing (DRF) systems. Counterparts’ implementation readiness supported by sustained technical assistance (TA) is crucial. The DRF agenda should be integrated within the overarching financial sector development vision. The program showed that a PforR instrument can be highly effective in promoting scalable and interlinked DRM-DRF solutions. Flexible program design is crucial for maintaining relevance in moments of challenges and opportunities. The sustainability of structural investments co-financed by the Fund for the Fight Against Natural Disasters (FLCN) remains a focus of attention, with the Independent Verification Agent (IVA) emphasizing the need to adopt dedicated operation and maintenance mechanisms.

title: Morocco - National Human Development Initiative (INDH) Project
report_name: Morocco - National Human Development Initiative (INDH) Project
project_id: MA-National Initiative for Human Development Support Project (INDH) -- P100026
abstract: Ratings for the National Human Development Initiative (INDH) Project for Morocco were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory; risk to development outcome was moderate to significant; Bank performance: was moderately satisfactory; and borrower performance was also moderately satisfactory. Some lessons learned includes: the valuable lessons drawn from the implementation of first INDH are based on continuous learning and dialogue between the Government, the Bank and other partners, and are supplemented by contributions from a broad spectrum of stakeholders through stakeholder workshops. The overall conclusions are presented here, but given the need for intensive learning and applicability to second INDH, annex two and annex six contain more detailed lessons learned in all areas of the project. INDH has shown that new participatory approaches can be put in place rapidly. In an environment with a history of top-down, centralized decision making, implanting a culture of participatory diagnostic and programming and real empowerment of poor communities poses many policy and institutional challenges. The first five year phase of the INDH has succeeded, with strong government commitment, adequate resource allocation and good technical support, in building capacity in governance and support structures, establishing participatory approaches, and showing what improvements are needed. Further, INDH has also demonstrated that large numbers of sub-projects can be implemented in a very short period. Despite the risks, a well-designed and supported program can begin from almost nothing and achieve high levels of implementation in a very short time, provided that adequate supporting measures are in place.

title: Egypt - Second Pollution Abatement Project
report_name: Egypt - Second Pollution Abatement Project
project_id: EG-Second Pollution Abatement Project -- P090073
abstract: The Second Pollution Abatement Project for Egypt received a satisfactory outcome rating with moderate risk to development outcome. The Bank received a moderately satisfactory outcome rating while the Borrower received a satisfactory outcome rating. The Project was restructured twice. The first restructuring was a level 2 restructuring to extend the closing date by 18 months due to the delays caused by the global financial crisis and due to the political events in Egypt. The second restructuring was also a level 2 restructuring to (a) extend the closing date by another 12 months to properly complete project activities, and (b) revise two intermediate performance indicators to take into account that the CDM projects did not move forward in a timely manner. There were several lessons learned from this project and are as follows: (1) as the Bank becomes increasingly involved with private sector entities, there is a need to improve the capacity of the Bank to handle common commercial procurement practices, (2) the value added of issuing loans in local currency should be considered at the preparation stage, (3) use of concessional finance has proved effective in triggering private investments in pollution abatement and the approach should be scaled-up and replicated in other countries, (4) internal rates of return (IRR) evaluation for pollution abatement investments could be used in the future to develop a differential scheme, (5) installation of continuous monitoring systems should form an integral part of grant funded sub-projects, (6) carbon finance-linked projects require careful preparation with contractual commitments upfront.
